title: Foundations (lite)
text: bundled icews dataset ICEWS event-data slices 152 countries 2002 2014 four “quad” variables (verbal/material × cooperation/conflict) plus handful nodal covariates. Turn netify object one call: print() summary tells network unipartite, directed, longitudinal (13 periods), 152 actors, nodal dyadic features attached. numeric table shows density, missingness, mean edge weight, mutual-dyad proportion, transitivity averaged across time. quick glossary, since several terms appear ’re defined elsewhere: unipartite: one kind actor (countries ). opposite bipartite – two kinds, e.g., students--clubs. directed / symmetric: directed tie sender receiver (-> j different j -> ); symmetric tie . density: share possible ties actually present (0 = ties, 1 = complete graph). transitivity: probability two friends also friends – “friend friend friend.” Higher = clustering. mutual-dyad proportion (mutual): among ordered pairs least one tie exists, share -> j j -> present. reciprocity: correlation adjacency matrix transpose ^T; behaves like mutual binary networks generalizes weighted ones.
code: data(icews) head(icews[, c("i", "j", "year", "verbCoop", "matlConf", "i_polity2", "i_region")]) #>             i       j year verbCoop matlConf i_polity2   i_region #> 2 Afghanistan Albania 2002        6        0        NA South Asia #> 3 Afghanistan Albania 2003        1        0        NA South Asia #> 4 Afghanistan Albania 2004       10        1        NA South Asia #> 5 Afghanistan Albania 2005        0        0        NA South Asia #> 6 Afghanistan Albania 2006        6       21        NA South Asia #> 7 Afghanistan Albania 2007        3        0        NA South Asia verb_coop <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight   = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_region"),     dyad_vars  = c("matlCoop", "verbConf") ) #> ℹ `missing_to_zero` is set to "TRUE" (the default). #> ! Missing dyads will be filled with zeros. For latent space or other #>   statistical network models, structural zeros and missing data have different #>   meanings. Set `missing_to_zero = FALSE` to preserve NAs if this distinction #>   matters for your analysis. #> This message is displayed once per session.  print(verb_coop) #> ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! #> • Unipartite #> • Asymmetric #> • Weights from `verbCoop` #> • Longitudinal: 13 Periods #> • # Unique Actors: 152 #> Network Summary Statistics (averaged across time): #>           dens miss   mean recip trans #> verbCoop 0.418    0 19.114 0.976 0.627 #> • Nodal Features: i_polity2, i_log_gdp, i_region #> • Dyad Features: matlCoop, verbConf

title: Foundations (lite)
text: summary() returns one row per time period graph-level statistics: Note reciprocity (correlation AA ATA^T, useful weighted networks) mutual (classic mutual-dyad proportion). Use whichever fits audience. Actor-level stats — degree, prop_ties, centrality, strength — come summary_actor(). Quick definitions shows columns: degree_in / degree_out / degree_total: count incoming, outgoing, total ties actor. prop_ties_*: degree counts divided number possible partners. strength: sum / average / sd weights actor’s ties (parallel degree weight-aware). betweenness: often actor sits shortest path two others – broker score. closeness: short actor’s average distance everyone else. authority_score / hub_score (HITS): authorities nodes many hubs point ; hubs nodes point many authorities. Useful directed networks (e.g., ’s getting cited vs. ’s citing). Plot . default uses auto_format = TRUE adapts network size — 152 actors 13 years ’ll suppress text labels tone edge alpha automatically:

code: ig <- to_igraph(verb_coop)             # list of igraph objects, one per year ig_2010 <- ig[["2010"]] length(igraph::cluster_walktrap(ig_2010)) #> [1] 6 df <- unnetify(subset(verb_coop, time = "2010"), remove_zeros = TRUE) head(df[, c("from", "to", "verbCoop", "matlCoop", "i_polity2_from", "i_polity2_to")]) #>          from         to verbCoop matlCoop i_polity2_from i_polity2_to #> 1 Afghanistan  Argentina        1        0             NA            8 #> 2 Afghanistan    Armenia        7        2             NA            5 #> 3 Afghanistan  Australia      125        0             NA           10 #> 4 Afghanistan    Austria        1        0             NA           10 #> 5 Afghanistan Azerbaijan        7        0             NA           -7 #> 6 Afghanistan    Bahrain        3        0             NA           -5

title: Foundations (lite)
text: long version peacesciencer data full IR walkthrough, see vignette("foundations", package = "netify"). fun!
code: net <- netify(df, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year", weight = "x") summary(net)            # graph-level stats summary_actor(net)      # actor-level stats plot(net)               # ggplot-based network visual homophily(net, attribute = "v")   # do similar actors connect? compare_networks(net)             # how do periods/layers/groups differ? to_amen(net)  # or to_dbn / to_statnet / to_igraph when you're ready to model

what: A note for non-time use cases
title: Foundations (lite)
text: netify’s docs talk “longitudinal” networks ’s bread--butter use case social science. underlying longit_list structure (list matrices, actor set) works per-slice partition, just time. Common non-time examples: Per-subject networks: brain connectivity matrices, one per fMRI subject Per-replicate networks: synthetic networks drawn generative process Per-condition networks: networks observed different experimental conditions Per-document networks: term co-occurrence networks, one per document , build list matrices (one per partition) use either new_netify(list_of_matrices) netify(df, ..., time = "partition_id"). netify’s per-period operations — summary(), summary_actor(), faceted plot(), subset(time = ...) — work identically; variable name just happens time. “time” framing gets confusing context, alias locally: generality package treats longit_list general partition format rather strictly temporal one.
code: # build a per-subject network library subjects <- new_netify(list_of_subject_matrices) # treat the "time" dimension as subject per_subject_stats <- summary(subjects)

what: The netify object: a base R object with attributes
title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: netify object base R matrix / 3D array / list--matrices class = "netify" bundle attributes carrying metadata. netify_type attribute records one three shapes: Multilayer networks insert layer dimension (position 3) store layer names attr(x, "layers"). Mixed-directedness multilayer supported via vector-valued symmetric attribute. dgCMatrix inputs Matrix package accepted densified construction (one-shot cli inform emitted); true sparse storage backend yet implemented. Inspect netify object’s attribute bundle: Key attributes: netify_type — "cross_sec", "longit_array", "longit_list" mode — "unipartite" "bipartite" symmetric — scalar logical (named vector mixed-directedness multilayer) weight — column name (NULL binary) is_binary, detail_weight, diag_to_NA, missing_to_zero, sum_dyads layers — character vector; length > 1 means multilayer actor_pds — data.frame actor, min_time, max_time per actor nodal_data — data.frame actor, optional time, one column per nodal variable dyad_data — nested list: list[[time]][[var]] = matrix. Cross-sec uses "1" time key.
code: net <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2010, ],               actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",               symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop",               nodal_vars = "i_polity2",               dyad_vars  = "matlCoop") #> ℹ `missing_to_zero` is set to "TRUE" (the default). #> ! Missing dyads will be filled with zeros. For latent space or other #>   statistical network models, structural zeros and missing data have different #>   meanings. Set `missing_to_zero = FALSE` to preserve NAs if this distinction #>   matters for your analysis. #> This message is displayed once per session.  class(net) #> [1] "netify" str(attributes(net), max.level = 1) #> List of 17 #>  $ dim               : int [1:2] 152 152 #>  $ dimnames          :List of 2 #>  $ class             : chr "netify" #>  $ netify_type       : chr "cross_sec" #>  $ actor_time_uniform: logi TRUE #>  $ actor_pds         :'data.frame':  152 obs. of  3 variables: #>  $ weight            : chr "verbCoop" #>  $ detail_weight     : chr "Weights from `verbCoop`" #>  $ is_binary         : logi FALSE #>  $ symmetric         : logi FALSE #>  $ mode              : chr "unipartite" #>  $ layers            : chr "verbCoop" #>  $ diag_to_NA        : logi TRUE #>  $ missing_to_zero   : logi TRUE #>  $ sum_dyads         : logi FALSE #>  $ nodal_data        :'data.frame':  152 obs. of  2 variables: #>  $ dyad_data         :List of 1

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: tidy() glance() S3 methods broom generics — ’re registered package load generics (anything imports , like broom, dplyr, tidymodels) installed. hard dependency broom.

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: tibble broom installed, three tidyverse-flavored entry points available. as_tibble.netify registered tibble::as_tibble via .onLoad, tibble must installed use unprefixed call; tidy() glance() likewise registered broom generics. as_tibble(net) tidy(net) share long-format payload unnetify(net) — differ whether zero-weight edges dropped default (tidy() drops , matching broom convention). glance(net) broom-flavored sibling summary(net) — one row graph-level statistics per network (per (time, layer) slice longitudinal / multilayer inputs). as_tibble() also method netify_comparison objects (compare_networks()), returning per-pair $comparisons frame directly can pipe straight filter() / arrange() / pivot_wider():
code: library(tibble) library(broom)  # one row per dyad (long edge frame, wrapped in a tibble) as_tibble(net) #> # A tibble: 22,952 × 6 #>    from        to         verbCoop matlCoop i_polity2_from i_polity2_to #>    <chr>       <chr>         <dbl>    <dbl>          <int>        <int> #>  1 Afghanistan Albania           0        1             NA            9 #>  2 Afghanistan Algeria           0        0             NA            2 #>  3 Afghanistan Angola            0        0             NA           -2 #>  4 Afghanistan Argentina         1        0             NA            8 #>  5 Afghanistan Armenia           7        2             NA            5 #>  6 Afghanistan Australia       125        0             NA           10 #>  7 Afghanistan Austria           1        0             NA           10 #>  8 Afghanistan Azerbaijan        7        0             NA           -7 #>  9 Afghanistan Bahrain           3        0             NA           -5 #> 10 Afghanistan Bangladesh       14        0             NA            5 #> # ℹ 22,942 more rows  # broom-style tidy summary: a tibble with one row per (non-zero) dyad head(tidy(net)) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   from        to         verbCoop matlCoop i_polity2_from i_polity2_to #>   <chr>       <chr>         <dbl>    <dbl>          <int>        <int> #> 1 Afghanistan Argentina         1        0             NA            8 #> 2 Afghanistan Armenia           7        2             NA            5 #> 3 Afghanistan Australia       125        0             NA           10 #> 4 Afghanistan Austria           1        0             NA           10 #> 5 Afghanistan Azerbaijan        7        0             NA           -7 #> 6 Afghanistan Bahrain           3        0             NA           -5  # one-row-per-network model-card summary (one row per time/layer if applicable) glance(net) #> # A tibble: 1 × 18 #>   net   num_actors density num_edges prop_edges_missing mean_edge_weight #>   <chr>      <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>              <dbl>            <dbl> #> 1 1            152   0.435      9976                  0             18.1 #> # ℹ 12 more variables: sd_edge_weight <dbl>, median_edge_weight <dbl>, #> #   min_edge_weight <dbl>, max_edge_weight <dbl>, competition_row <dbl>, #> #   competition_col <dbl>, sd_of_row_means <dbl>, sd_of_col_means <dbl>, #> # title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: validate_netify() developer’s pre-flight check. walks attribute bundle confirms invariants rest package relies : full list checks: netify_type — one "cross_sec", "longit_array", "longit_list", matches underlying R object mode — "unipartite" "bipartite" symmetric_type — scalar logical, named logical length n_layers mixed-directedness multilayer layers_consistent — attr(net, "layers") length matches layer dimension applicable nodal_actors_known — every actor referenced nodal_data exists network’s actor set is_binary_consistent — stored is_binary flag matches actual content symmetric_consistent — stored symmetric, matrix content actually symmetric unipartite_dimnames — row column names agree unipartite networks slice_dimnames_consistent — every slice longitudinal array/list dimnames order recorded actor_pds nodal_time_known — time keys nodal/dyad data subset network’s time axis quick demo clean netlet versus one tampered introduce stray actor: custom exporter internal manipulation breaks one , validate_netify() first place look.
code: validate_netify(net, verbose = TRUE) #> ✔ netify object passes all coherence checks (11 checks). # clean netlet ticks every box all(unlist(validate_netify(net, verbose = FALSE))) #> [1] TRUE  # tamper: inject a stray actor into nodal_data bad <- net nd <- attr(bad, "nodal_data") nd <- rbind(nd, nd[1, , drop = FALSE]) nd$actor[nrow(nd)] <- "ZZZ_not_in_network" attr(bad, "nodal_data") <- nd  validate_netify(bad, verbose = TRUE) #> ! nodal_data references 1 actor not in the netlet: "ZZZ_not_in_network" #> ✖ netify object failed 1 coherence check: "nodal_actors_known".

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: actor roster changes time — entries, exits, attrition, contact-tracing windows — pass actor_time_uniform = FALSE supply actor_pds data.frame giving actor’s [min_time, max_time] window. period’s netlet contains actors whose window covers period; densities, degree counts, per-period actor sets respect entry / exit boundaries.

what: The actor_pds roster, step by step
title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: Three pieces line open-cohort netlet well-formed: roster — one row per actor, actor, min_time, max_time. [min_time, max_time] interval closed (actor alive boundary periods ). edgelist — one row per observed interaction. Edges whose endpoints time fall outside roster’s windows silently dropped construction. netify(actor_time_uniform = FALSE, actor_pds = roster, ...) — tells constructor honor roster instead treating every actor present every period. , actor network periods 1-2 (enters t = 1, exits t = 2), actors d / e arrive t = 3. edgelist deliberately includes tie t = 2 involving ties t = 3 involving d can verify period--period actor sets afterwards. get_actor_time_info() netify object returns stored actor_pds directly (also argument name netify() , round trip exact). raw dyad data.frame function derives roster observed activity — ’s build actor_pds argument first place. standard path open-cohort longitudinal data — panel surveys attrition, contact-tracing chains, organizational membership time, animal co-occurrence births / deaths, similar settings treating every actor present every period distort denominators.
code: set.seed(1)  # roster: actors with closed-interval entry / exit times roster <- data.frame(     actor = c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e"),     min_time = c(1, 1, 1, 3, 3),     max_time = c(2, 5, 4, 5, 5)   # a exits after t = 2 )  # edges (only show up while both endpoints are in the roster) edges <- data.frame(     i = c("a", "a", "b", "c", "d", "c", "d", "e"),     j = c("b", "c", "c", "b", "e", "d", "e", "b"),     t = c(1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 3, 5, 5) )  net_oc <- netify(edges,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "t",     actor_time_uniform = FALSE,     actor_pds = roster )  # read the roster back off the netlet itself get_actor_time_info(net_oc) #>   actor min_time max_time #> 1     a        1        2 #> 2     b        1        5 #> 3     c        1        4 #> 4     d        3        5 #> 5     e        3        5 n_actors(net_oc) #> [1] 4 n_periods(net_oc) #> [1] 5

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: key correctness guarantee: density (every per-period statistic) computed actor set alive period, union actors ever observed. roster : t = 1: actors , b, c alive (3 actors) t = 2: actors , b, c alive (3 actors; ’s last period) t = 3: actors b, c, d, e alive (4 actors; now , d / e enter) t = 4: actors b, c, d, e alive (4 actors) t = 5: actors b, d, e alive (3 actors; c’s last period 4) period-3 denominator 4 × 3 = 12 (directed) 4 × 3 / 2 = 6 (symmetric), 5 × 4 — actor counted max_time 2. load-bearing invariant: density t = 3 excludes actor present periods 1-2, researcher comparing densities across periods comparing apples oranges. accounting flows summary_actor(), homophily(), mixing_matrix(), plot helpers — open-cohort actors never show zero-degree placeholders periods exist.
code: oc_summary <- summary(net_oc) oc_summary[, c("net", "num_actors", "density", "num_edges")] #>   net num_actors   density num_edges #> 1   1          3 0.3333333         1 #> 2   2          3 0.6666667         2 #> 3   3          4 0.3333333         2 #> 4   4          4 0.1666667         1 #> 5   5          3 0.6666667         2

what: NA versus zero in weighted networks
title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: weighted data, distinction 0 (observed, edge / zero-valued interaction) NA (unobserved / --risk) semantically important many domains — epidemiology, animal behavior, sparse survey rosters. default netify(missing_to_zero = TRUE) fills unobserved dyads 0. Pass missing_to_zero = FALSE keep NA, case summary()$prop_edges_missing reports non-trivial missingness fraction downstream centrality / homophily routines propagate NA semantics rather silently treating dyad zero-weight tie. prop_edges_missing prop_unknown_edges use denominator density — number potential edge dyads (-diagonal unipartite + diag_to_NA, halved symmetric, cells bipartite). means density + prop_edges_missing + observed_zero_fraction = 1 identity, property want reading competing fractions. prop_unknown_edges column suppressed missing_to_zero = TRUE every unobserved dyad filled 0 value identically zero every row; present tracks prop_edges_missing serves “netlet carries NA semantics” cue downstream.

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: summary(net, other_stats = list(my_stat = fn)) accepts user-supplied functions. function receives netify object current time period / layer (stripped matrix), can call netify_to_igraph(), peek(), whatever want inside. Return named numeric vector — names become column names output frame. other_stats mechanism available summary_actor(), compare_networks(), homophily(), mixing_matrix(), dyad_correlation(). case function gets called per time period / layer / iteration appropriate; check function’s ? exact contract.
code: # Example: number of weakly connected components with at least 2 nodes n_components_2plus <- function(net) {   g <- netify_to_igraph(net)   c(n_components_2plus = sum(igraph::components(g)$csize >= 2)) }  # Example: edge weight skewness weight_skew <- function(net) {   v <- as.vector(net)   v <- v[!is.na(v) & v != 0]   if (length(v) < 3) return(c(weight_skew = NA_real_))   c(weight_skew = mean((v - mean(v))^3) / (stats::sd(v)^3)) }  summary(net, other_stats = list(   comp = n_components_2plus,   skew = weight_skew )) #>   net num_actors   density num_edges prop_edges_missing mean_edge_weight #> 1   1        152 0.4346462      9976                  0         18.13393 #>   sd_edge_weight median_edge_weight min_edge_weight max_edge_weight #> 1        132.367                  0               0            4937 #>   competition_row competition_col sd_of_row_means sd_of_col_means #> 1      0.04071163      0.03493156         41.4412        37.76971 #>   covar_of_row_col_means reciprocity    mutual transitivity #> 1              0.9934913   0.9823385 0.8402509    0.6458631 #>   comp.n_components_2plus skew.weight_skew #> 1                       1         13.76129

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: summary_actor(net, other_stats = list(my_stat = fn)). function return vector one value per actor (row order):

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: attr(net, "dyad_data") structure gives netify O(1) access per-time dyadic covariates. shape : longitudinal networks top level one entry per period: ’re writing converter (e.g., new modeling format), structure iterate .
code: dd <- attr(net, "dyad_data") names(dd)             # cross-sec: just "1" #> [1] "1" names(dd[["1"]])      # one entry per dyadic variable #> [1] "matlCoop" str(dd[["1"]][["matlCoop"]]) #>  int [1:152, 1:152] 0 4 0 0 0 2 19 0 1 0 ... #>  - attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2 #>   ..$ : chr [1:152] "Afghanistan" "Albania" "Algeria" "Angola" ... #>   ..$ : chr [1:152] "Afghanistan" "Albania" "Algeria" "Angola" ... # pseudo-structure for a 3-period network with 2 dyadic vars: # dd[["2010"]][["matlCoop"]]   -> n×n matrix # dd[["2010"]][["verbConf"]]   -> n×n matrix # dd[["2011"]][["matlCoop"]]   -> n×n matrix # ... etc.

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: existing to_amen(), to_lame(), to_dbn(), to_igraph(), to_statnet() give templates. (to_lame() modern AME / latent-factor handoff emits snippet executable code binding nl; to_amen() historical alias now redirects to_lame().) new exporter generally needs : Validate netify object netify_check(netlet) Branch attr(netlet, "netify_type") (cross_sec / longit_array / longit_list) multilayer, decide whether iterate per layer (use subset_netify(netlet, layers = X) return named list) produce joint structure (4D array, etc.) Pull raw network data get_raw(netlet) Pull nodal data attr(netlet, "nodal_data") align target package’s actor order Pull dyadic data attr(netlet, "dyad_data") reshape target format Handle missing values according target package’s convention (don’t accept NAs) target package expects per-layer outputs takes multilayer netify inputs, standard pattern : to_igraph(), to_statnet(), to_lame() .
code: to_mymodel <- function(netlet, ...) {     netify_check(netlet)     layer_names <- attributes(netlet)$layers     if (length(layer_names) > 1) {         out <- lapply(layer_names, function(lyr) {             to_mymodel(subset_netify(netlet, layers = lyr), ...)         })         names(out) <- layer_names         return(out)     }     # ... single-layer logic ... }

title: Internals: object structure & writing custom extensions
text: networks hundred actors dozen time periods, everything runs seconds. C++ routines netify(), compare_networks() QAP, similarity calculations handle heaviest paths. R-side wrappers (especially plotting attribute handling) optimized. Memory budget rule thumb. netify stores adjacencies densely: N × N double-precision matrix 8·N² bytes. longitudinal stacks cost 8·N²·T (longit_array) sum per-period sizes (longit_list, actor composition varies). Concretely: longit_list netlets cost sum per-period sizes rather T × max(N)², pay less actor composition sparse time. 15,000-node weekly Twitter snapshots year (T = 52), longit_array ~93 GB fit laptop; longit_list whose typical-period N much smaller viable -memory shape. ~10,000 actors, prefer building netlet edgelist data.frame (skips dense intermediate construction) consider exiting edge data.frame via unnetify(net, remove_zeros = TRUE), igraph via to_igraph(net) community detection large-N algorithms. summary_actor(stats = "fast") skips closeness / betweenness / eigen / HITS auto-promotes N exceeds getOption("netify.fast_threshold", 1500L). Sparse-input guard. Passing Matrix::dgCMatrix (sparseMatrix) density < 1% N > 5,000 aborts construction pointer edgelist path. motivating case 15K × 15K follower graph density 0.001: densifying allocates ~1.7 GB mostly zeros, almost never caller wants. Pass force_dense = TRUE override really want dense allocation. Benchmark, three ER networks. Wall-clock dense-matrix path representative laptop (single core, single snapshot, p = 0.01). Re-run locally chunk want numbers machine. Indicative results (single-core, 16 GB laptop): summary() dominated igraph-based global metrics grows fastest; netify() stays seconds even N = 10,000 fed edgelist. full summary_actor() (closeness / betweenness / eigen / HITS) blows roughly quadratically — N = 10,000 takes several minutes versus seconds shown fast path. ’s auto-promote fires default.
code: library(netify) set.seed(1) bench_one <- function(N, p = 0.01) {   # build an ER adjacency directly as an edgelist (skips the dense intermediate)   i <- sample.int(N, size = round(p * N * N), replace = TRUE)   j <- sample.int(N, size = length(i), replace = TRUE)   df <- data.frame(from = i, to = j)    t0 <- Sys.time(); net <- netify(df, actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to"); t_build <- Sys.time() - t0   t0 <- Sys.time(); s   <- summary(net);                                 t_summary <- Sys.time() - t0   t0 <- Sys.time(); sa  <- summary_actor(net, stats = "fast");           t_actor_fast <- Sys.time() - t0   t0 <- Sys.time(); ig  <- to_igraph(net);                               t_igraph <- Sys.time() - t0    data.frame(N = N,              build_s = as.numeric(t_build, units = "secs"),              summary_s = as.numeric(t_summary, units = "secs"),              summary_actor_fast_s = as.numeric(t_actor_fast, units = "secs"),              to_igraph_s = as.numeric(t_igraph, units = "secs")) } do.call(rbind, lapply(c(1000, 5000, 10000), bench_one))

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: plot.netify() handles 90% cases box. vignette 10% — want full control geometry, layout, combinations color scales built-path doesn’t expose. cover extracting node edge data, layering ggplot2, highlighting single actor, layering multiple color scales, applying ggplot theme, exporting ggsave(). fun! vignette provides overview create customizable plots using ggplot2 still using netify prepare data. Let’s load necessary libraries. advanced sections also use dplyr (data manipulation) ggnewscale (multiple color legends). dplyr installed, core plotting workflow still runs; advanced edge-information section skipped.
code: library(netify) library(ggplot2) library(ggnewscale)

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: First let’s create netlet object dyadic data (ICEWS data) using netify package. longitudinal, weighted network nodal dyadic attributes. steps show highlight attributes plot.
code: # load icews data data(icews)  # choose attributes nvars <- c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop") dvars <- c("matlCoop", "verbConf", "matlConf")  # create a netify object netlet <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop",     mode = "unipartite", sum_dyads = FALSE,     actor_time_uniform = TRUE, actor_pds = NULL,     diag_to_NA = TRUE, missing_to_zero = TRUE,     nodal_vars = nvars,     dyad_vars = dvars )  # subset to a few actors actors_to_keep <- c(     "Australia", "Brazil",     "Canada", "Chile", "China",     "Colombia", "Egypt", "Ethiopia",     "France", "Germany", "Ghana",     "Hungary", "India", "Indonesia",     "Iran, Islamic Republic Of",     "Israel", "Italy", "Japan", "Kenya",     "Korea, Democratic People's Republic Of",     "Korea, Republic Of", "Nigeria", "Pakistan",     "Qatar", "Russian Federation", "Saudi Arabia",     "South Africa", "Spain", "Sudan",     "Syrian Arab Republic", "Thailand",     "United Kingdom", "United States",     "Zimbabwe" ) netlet <- subset_netify(     netlet,     actors = actors_to_keep )  # print netlet ## ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! ## • Unipartite ## • Asymmetric ## • Weights from `verbCoop` ## • Longitudinal: 13 Periods ## • # Unique Actors: 34 ## Network Summary Statistics (averaged across time): ##           dens miss    mean recip trans ## verbCoop 0.887    0 179.484 0.978 0.928 ## • Nodal Features: i_polity2, i_log_gdp, i_log_pop ## • Dyad Features: matlCoop, verbConf, matlConf

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: reaching ggplot2 directly, remember fastest path network plot built-plot() method. picks layout, draws edges, points, (small networks) auto-repelling labels, returns ggplot object can keep customizing + layers. Add theme_publication_netify() apply netify theme:  single call usually enough first look. rest vignette shows want finer control underlying data layers — example, swapping layout algorithm, adding edge-level information default plot doesn’t expose, combining multiple color scales ggnewscale.
code: # one-liner -- works for cross-sectional or longitudinal netify objects set.seed(6886) plot(netlet) + theme_publication_netify()

what: Going manual: extract the plot data frames
title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: need finer control, use net_plot_data create data frame ggplot2. net_plot_data extracts sets node edge data netify object according specified plotting arguments. returns list different components important one users net_dfs element. element contains two objects: edge_data nodal_data. data frames can passed ggplot2. x y nodal_data x1, y1, x2, y2 edge_data coordinates nodes edges, respectively. coordinates used plot network.

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: Now data, can create plot using ggplot2. ’ll use geom_segment geom_point (geom_label, geom_text, ggrepel equivalents) plot edges nodes, respectively.  Note scale_color_viridis_c(option = "cividis") — colorblind-safe perceptually-uniform palette. continuous variables almost always better default hand-picked two-color gradient.
code: ggplot() +     geom_segment(         data = net_dfs$edge_data,         aes(             x = x1,             y = y1,             xend = x2,             yend = y2         ),         color = "lightgrey",         alpha = .2     ) +     geom_point(         data = net_dfs$nodal_data,         aes(             x = x,             y = y,             size = i_log_pop,             color = i_polity2         )     ) +     labs(         color = "Polity",         size = "Log(Pop.)"     ) +     scale_color_viridis_c(option = "cividis") +     facet_wrap(~time, scales = "free") +     theme_netify()

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: common task: label visually pop one specific actor plot. built-plot.netify() can directly via highlight = "United States" plus select_text / select_text_display:  assembling plot manually net_plot_data(), effect one extra layer — flag focal actor nodal_data add geom_text (ggrepel::geom_text_repel) layer just . Note net_plot_data() stores actor names column called name:
code: set.seed(6886) plot(netlet,     highlight = "United States",     highlight_color = c("United States" = "#0A3161", "Other" = "grey70"),     select_text = "United States",     select_text_display = "United States",     time_filter = "2010" ) +     theme_publication_netify() ## Warning: Non-positive edge weight found, ignoring all weights during ## graph layout. ## Warning in betweenness_cutoff_impl(graph = graph, vids = v, directed = directed, : Some weights are smaller than epsilon, calculations may suffer from numerical precision issues. ## Source: centrality/betweenness.c:441 focal <- "United States" nodal_focal <- subset(net_dfs$nodal_data, name == focal)  ggplot() +     geom_segment(         data = net_dfs$edge_data,         aes(x = x1, y = y1, xend = x2, yend = y2),         color = "lightgrey", alpha = .2     ) +     geom_point(         data = net_dfs$nodal_data,         aes(x = x, y = y),         color = "grey60", size = 2     ) +     geom_point(         data = nodal_focal,         aes(x = x, y = y),         color = "#0A3161", size = 4     ) +     geom_text(         data = nodal_focal,         aes(x = x, y = y, label = name),         nudge_y = .05, fontface = "bold"     ) +     facet_wrap(~time, scales = "free") +     theme_netify()

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: far, focused using color convey information nodal attributes network (population size polity score). Now, let’s add edge information plot. example, can include information matlConf dyadic attribute. Imagine want highlight edges verbal cooperation occur time higher average levels material conflict occur network. First, let’s create variable edge data. Now new variable data.frame, can plot note now need color aesthetic points segments, even though ggplot2 supports one legend aesthetic default. can get around using new_scale_color function ggnewscale package.
code: library(dplyr) ##  ## Attaching package: 'dplyr' ## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': ##  ##     filter, lag ## The following objects are masked from 'package:base': ##  ##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union # create high_matlConf variable net_dfs$edge_data <- net_dfs$edge_data |>     group_by(time) |>     mutate(         high_matlConf = matlConf > mean(matlConf, na.rm = TRUE)     ) |>     ungroup() |>     as.data.frame()  # check head(net_dfs$edge_data) ##        from       to time verbCoop matlCoop verbConf matlConf          x1 ## 1 Australia   Brazil 2002        3        0        0        0 -0.05761221 ## 2 Australia   Canada 2002       24        1        3        3 -0.05761221 ## 3 Australia    Chile 2002        1        0        0        1 -0.05761221 ## 4 Australia    China 2002      518       15       43        3 -0.05761221 ## 5 Australia Colombia 2002        1        0        6        0 -0.05761221 ## 6 Australia    Egypt 2002       15        0        2        4 -0.05761221 ##          y1            x2          y2 high_matlConf ## 1 0.1316246  0.2567066471  0.06102817         FALSE ## 2 0.1316246  0.0395816516 -0.15478831         FALSE ## 3 0.1316246  0.2208771149  0.27780000         FALSE ## 4 0.1316246 -0.0005098899  0.05176884         FALSE ## 5 0.1316246  0.2581434830 -0.08165964         FALSE ## 6 0.1316246 -0.0063939248 -0.10525516         FALSE # color line segments by this new variable ggplot() +     geom_segment(         data = net_dfs$edge_data,         aes(             x = x1,             y = y1,             xend = x2,             yend = y2,             color = high_matlConf         ),         alpha = .2     ) +     scale_color_manual(         name = "",         values = c("grey", "red"),         labels = c("Below Avg. Matl. Conf", "Above Avg.")     ) +     new_scale_color() +     geom_point(         data = net_dfs$nodal_data,         aes(             x = x,             y = y,             size = i_log_pop,             color = i_polity2         )     ) +     scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Polity", option = "cividis") +     labs(         size = "Log(Pop.)"     ) +     facet_wrap(~time, scales = "free") +     theme_publication_netify() +     theme(legend.position = "right")

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: plot laid way want, export ggsave(). function writes whatever ggplot last printed (whichever ggplot object pass via plot=) disk. vector format (PDF SVG) holds size; web PNG 300 dpi fine.
code: # last plot rendered ggsave("icews_network.pdf", width = 9, height = 8)  # or pass an explicit plot object p <- plot(netlet, time_filter = "2010") + theme_publication_netify() ggsave("icews_2010.png", plot = p, width = 7, height = 6, dpi = 300)

title: Manual Plotting with ggplot2
text: quick reference picking colors survive colorblindness grayscale printing: Continuous variables — use scale_color_viridis_c() / scale_fill_viridis_c(). viridis, magma, plasma, cividis options perceptually uniform colorblind-safe; cividis safest broadest set color vision deficiencies also prints well black & white. Categorical variables (≤8 levels) — use ColorBrewer’s Set2, Dark2, Paired. Pass palette = "Set2" scale_color_brewer(), node_color_palette = "Set2" plot.netify(). Two-class highlights — pair strong color neutral grey, United States highlight example (#0A3161 vs grey70).

what: Why Multilayer Networks Matter for Social Science
title: Multilayer Networks
text: Traditional network analysis forces us choose: study trade conflict, cooperation competition, formal informal ties. political reality multilayered. States trade may also fight. Legislators cosponsor bills may attack social media. Ignoring multiple, simultaneous relationships leads incomplete potentially misleading conclusions. Multilayer networks solve problem allowing us analyze multiple relationship types together, revealing: Substitution effects: actors use one type relationship instead another? Complementarities: certain relationships tend co-occur? Cross-layer influence: position one network affect behavior another? Structural coupling: different relationship types shape ?

previous_headings: Why Multilayer Networks Matter for Social Science
title: Multilayer Networks
text: ’ll use data Integrated Crisis Early Warning System (ICEWS) throughout .  see: Even simple example reveals “cheap talk” problem international relations. verbal cooperation network (left) dense connections, material cooperation (right) sparse. diplomatic promises don’t translate material support.
code: library(netify) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2)  # Load the ICEWS event data data(icews)  # Create a simple multilayer network for 2010 icews_2010 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Verbal cooperation network verbal_net <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Material cooperation network material_net <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlCoop" )  # Combine into multilayer network multi_net <- layer_netify(     list(verbal = verbal_net, material = material_net) )  # Quick visualization plot(multi_net, add_text = FALSE)

what: Building Multilayer Networks: A Substantive Example
title: Multilayer Networks
text: Let’s build complete multilayer network study international cooperation patterns critical period – 2002, shortly 9/11 cooperation dynamics rapidly shifting. Note: ICEWS event counts directed (sends X j). symmetrize layer summing →j j→counts single undirected weight, cross-layer mixing, homophily, regional-clustering analyses naturally undirected. directed analyses (e.g. initiates cooperation), set symmetric = FALSE instead.
code: # Focus on 2002 - post-9/11 cooperation dynamics icews_2002 <- icews[icews$year == 2002, ]  # Create networks with relevant attributes. symmetric = TRUE collapses # i->j and j->i counts into a single undirected weight; appropriate for # the undirected mixing / homophily / multiplexity analyses below. verbal_coop_net <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop", "i_region"),     dyad_vars = c("verbConf", "matlConf") )  material_coop_net <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "matlCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop", "i_region"),     dyad_vars = c("verbConf", "matlConf") )  # Combine into multilayer network multilayer_net <- layer_netify(     netlet_list = list(verbal_coop_net, material_coop_net),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  multilayer_net

title: Multilayer Networks
text: visualization reveals fundamental asymmetry international cooperation. verbal layer shows “diplomatic commons” – nearly countries engage diplomatic discourse. material layer reveals “cooperation core” – much smaller set countries back words resources. Node sizes (GDP) show large economies participate networks, material cooperation isn’t simply function capacity.
code: plot(multilayer_net,     add_text = FALSE,     node_size_by = "i_log_gdp",     node_color_by = "i_region" ) +     theme(         legend.position = "right"     )

title: Multilayer Networks
text: numbers mean: ~3.6x density difference: roughly every material cooperation relationship, three four verbal ones ~3.6x degree difference: average country verbally cooperates 62 others (mean degree ~61.7 symmetrized layer) materially 17 (mean degree ~17.0) Different clustering: Higher transitivity verbal cooperation (0.606 vs 0.311) suggests diplomatic communities form readily material support clusters structural evidence supports “cheap talk” hypothesis also reveals something deeper: material cooperation follows different organizational logics diplomacy.

previous_headings: Testing Political Science Theories with Multilayer Networks
what: Theory 1: Democratic Peace in Different Domains
title: Multilayer Networks
text: democracies cooperate ? vary cooperation type? Democratic homophily registers verbal cooperation (p < 0.001) material cooperation (p = 0.13). verbal correlation small absolute terms (0.04) – magnitude significance flag mostly reflects large dyad count, read “consistent diplomatic-channel mechanism” rather clean effect estimate. Material support, contrast, crosses regime boundaries entirely, plausibly driven strategic rather ideological considerations.
code: # Test homophily across layers multilayer_homophily <- homophily(     multilayer_net,     attribute = "i_polity2",     method = "correlation" )  multilayer_homophily #>   net    layer attribute      method threshold_value homophily_correlation #> 1   1   Verbal i_polity2 correlation               0            0.04162793 #> 2   1 Material i_polity2 correlation               0            0.01444973 #>   mean_similarity_connected mean_similarity_unconnected similarity_difference #> 1                 -6.964294                   -7.444698             0.4804043 #> 2                 -7.016653                   -7.279288             0.2626347 #>   p_value     ci_lower  ci_upper n_connected_pairs n_unconnected_pairs #> 1   0.000  0.023477413 0.0599541              4453                6573 #> 2   0.136 -0.006875631 0.0335228              1201                9825 #>   n_missing n_pairs #> 1         3   11476 #> 2         3   11476

previous_headings: Testing Political Science Theories with Multilayer Networks
title: Multilayer Networks
text: security concerns create regional cooperation clusters? Let’s examine regional patterns: Material cooperation shows stronger regional clustering (assortativity = 0.218) verbal cooperation (0.156), consistent regional security complex theory – countries commit resources, prioritize neighbors. Verbal cooperation, cheaper, globally distributed. One caveat: material events substantially sparser verbal ones, sparse networks mechanically push assortativity (fewer ties means within-region tie carries weight index). Read gap directionally rather precise effect size.
code: # Regional mixing patterns multilayer_mixing <- mixing_matrix(     multilayer_net,     attribute = "i_region" )  multilayer_mixing$summary_stats #>   net    layer attribute assortativity diagonal_proportion  entropy modularity #> 1   1   Verbal  i_region     0.1558717           0.3365323 3.350767   0.122512 #> 2   1 Material  i_region     0.2184878           0.3835404 3.346822   0.172344 #>   n_groups total_ties #> 1        7       9378 #> 2        7       2576

title: Multilayer Networks
text: conflict one domain relate cooperation another? Verbal conflict verbal cooperation moderately correlated (r = 0.456) – countries talk also argue. Material conflict shows lower correlations cooperation layers (~0.27-0.28), suggesting material interactions follow different dynamics diplomatic ones.
code: # Analyze conflict-cooperation relationships cross_layer_corr <- dyad_correlation(     multilayer_net,     dyad_vars = c("verbConf", "matlConf"),     edge_vars = c("Verbal", "Material") )  # Show key correlations cross_layer_corr |>     select(dyad_var, edge_var, correlation, p_value) |>     filter(row_number() <= 4) # Show first 4 unique combinations #>   dyad_var edge_var correlation p_value #> 1 matlConf Material   0.2791647       0 #> 2 matlConf   Verbal   0.2783377       0 #> 3 verbConf Material   0.2692519       0 #> 4 verbConf   Verbal   0.4557314       0

title: Multilayer Networks
text: countries specialize material cooperation despite limited diplomatic engagement: top material brokers 2002 – ranked ratio material verbal betweenness – Spain, India, France, United Kingdom, Netherlands. None countries appreciable verbal betweenness 2002 (close zero), yet accumulates substantial material betweenness, material_bias ratio essentially raw material betweenness. pattern consistent middle-power donor states: Spain Netherlands sit top development-aid pipelines; France UK route post-colonial material flows; India notable south-south aid hub 2002. headline US/China/Russia hegemons drop ranking precisely verbal betweenness high enough anchor ratio’s denominator – filter designed surface countries important material channel, overall biggest players.
code: # Find actor-level patterns actor_stats <- summary_actor(multilayer_net)  # Identify material cooperation specialists centrality_comparison <- actor_stats |>     select(actor, layer, degree, betweenness, strength_sum) |>     pivot_wider(names_from = layer, values_from = c(degree, betweenness, strength_sum))  material_specialists <- centrality_comparison |>     mutate(         material_bias = betweenness_Material / (betweenness_Verbal + 1)     ) |>     filter(betweenness_Material > quantile(betweenness_Material, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)) |>     arrange(desc(material_bias)) |>     head(5)  material_specialists[, c("actor", "betweenness_Verbal", "betweenness_Material", "material_bias")] #> # A tibble: 5 × 4 #>   actor          betweenness_Verbal betweenness_Material material_bias #>   <chr>                       <dbl>                <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 Spain                     0                      2978.         2978. #> 2 India                     0.124                  2215.         1970. #> 3 France                    0.0182                 2004.         1969. #> 4 United Kingdom            0.00377                1776.         1770. #> 5 Netherlands               0.00338                1644.         1639.

title: Multilayer Networks
text: Power Analysis: US dominates layers, confirming hegemonic status Russia shows high verbal lower material engagement – influence diplomacy rather resources China (2002) shows moderate engagement, presaging later rise Japan’s material cooperation exceeds diplomatic footprint Germany balances dimensions, reflecting EU leadership
code: # Compare major powers plot_actor_stats(     actor_stats,     across_actor = FALSE,     specific_actors = c("United States", "China", "Russian Federation", "Japan", "Germany") )

what: Understanding Multiplexity: The Architecture of International Relations
title: Multilayer Networks
text: Multiplexity analysis reveals relationships involve multiple types interaction – key indicator relationship depth stability:  Multiplexity Insights: 26% relationships multiplex (involving verbal material cooperation) 124 “material ” relationships deserve attention – might represent covert cooperation technical assistance diplomatic radar 2,452 multiplex relationships form stable core international cooperation
code: # Analyze edge patterns verbal_melt <- melt(verbal_coop_net, remove_zeros = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE) material_melt <- melt(material_coop_net, remove_zeros = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)  edge_comparison <- merge(     verbal_melt,     material_melt,     by = c("Var1", "Var2"),     suffixes = c("_verbal", "_material") ) |>     filter(value_verbal > 0 | value_material > 0)  # Categorize relationships multiplexity_summary <- edge_comparison |>     mutate(         relationship_type = case_when(             value_verbal > 0 & value_material > 0 ~ "Multiplex",             value_verbal > 0 ~ "Verbal Only",             value_material > 0 ~ "Material Only"         )     )  # Summary and visualization table(multiplexity_summary$relationship_type) #>  #> Material Only     Multiplex   Verbal Only  #>           124          2452          6926  ggplot(multiplexity_summary, aes(x = relationship_type)) +     geom_bar() +     labs(         title = "The Multiplexity Gap in International Cooperation",         subtitle = "Most relationships remain purely verbal",         x = "Relationship Type",         y = "Number of Country Pairs"     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "top"     )

previous_headings: Understanding Multiplexity: The Architecture of International Relations
title: Multilayer Networks
text: US-Russia tops list despite tensions, showing former adversaries maintain multiple channels. US relationships allies (Israel, Japan) strategic competitors (China) also show high multiplexity, indicating relationship importance transcends simple friend/foe categories.
code: # Identify multiplex relationships multiplex_pairs <- multiplexity_summary |>     filter(relationship_type == "Multiplex") |>     mutate(total_coop = value_verbal + value_material) |>     arrange(desc(total_coop)) |>     head(10)  multiplex_pairs[, c("Var1", "Var2", "value_verbal", "value_material")] #>                  Var1               Var2 value_verbal value_material #> 1  Russian Federation      United States         5760             64 #> 2       United States Russian Federation         5760             64 #> 3              Israel      United States         4652             50 #> 4       United States             Israel         4652             50 #> 5               China      United States         4481             16 #> 6       United States              China         4481             16 #> 7               Japan      United States         4334             31 #> 8       United States              Japan         4334             31 #> 9            Pakistan      United States         3252            121 #> 10      United States           Pakistan         3252            121

title: Multilayer Networks
text: Key Metrics Explained: Correlation (0.472): Moderate positive relationship – verbal cooperation somewhat predicts material cooperation Jaccard (0.258): 26% edge overlap – relationships exist just one layer Hamming (0.305): 30% possible edges differ layers QAP test (p < 0.001): correlation statistically significant, due chance downstream filtering plotting, compare_networks() also exposes tidy $comparisons data frame one row per (pair, metric). layout usually want two networks (see multilayer section vignette("tracking_changes") fuller example): Tidy per-pair comparison frame
code: # Comprehensive layer comparison layer_comparison <- compare_networks(multilayer_net, what = "edges", method = "all") layer_comparison #>           comparison correlation jaccard hamming qap_correlation qap_pvalue #> 1 Verbal vs Material       0.472   0.258   0.305           0.472          0 #>   spectral #> 1 9.12e+04 #>             Verbal  Material #> Verbal   1.0000000 0.4718002 #> Material 0.4718002 1.0000000 #>          Verbal Material #> Verbal        0        0 #> Material      0        0 #> attr(,"n_perm") #> [1] 5000 knitr::kable(     layer_comparison$comparisons,     caption = "Tidy per-pair comparison frame",     digits = 3, align = "c" )

title: Multilayer Networks
code: # Cross-layer scatter ggplot(edge_comparison, aes(x = log1p(value_verbal), y = log1p(value_material))) +     geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +     geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +     geom_density_2d(alpha = 0.5) +     labs(         title = "The Verbal-Material Cooperation Gap",         subtitle = "Most relationships cluster along axes, not diagonal",         x = "Log(Verbal Cooperation + 1)",         y = "Log(Material Cooperation + 1)"     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "top"     ) +     annotate("text", x = 7, y = 1, label = "Talk without action", fontface = "italic") +     annotate("text", x = 1, y = 5, label = "Quiet support", fontface = "italic") +     annotate("text", x = 6, y = 5, label = "Full cooperation", fontface = "italic")

title: Multilayer Networks
text: Understanding different layers evolve time reveals changing cooperation dynamics:  post-9/11 period shows network evolution. layers densify 2001 2003, material cooperation grows selectively, suggesting crisis-driven coalition building.
code: # Create temporal multilayer network years_example <- c(2001, 2002, 2003) # Around 9/11 icews_subset <- icews[icews$year %in% years_example, ]  verbal_temporal <- netify(     icews_subset,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  material_temporal <- netify(     icews_subset,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "matlCoop" )  temporal_multilayer <- layer_netify(     list(verbal_temporal, material_temporal),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  # Visualize evolution plot(temporal_multilayer,     add_text = FALSE,     facet_type = "wrap",     facet_ncol = 3 )

what: When to Use Multilayer vs. Separate Analysis
title: Multilayer Networks
text: Use multilayer : Relationships theoretically influence suspect substitution complementarity effects Actor positions might differ across relationship types need identify relationship depth multiplexity Use separate analysis : Relationships theoretically independent Different actor sets across layers Computational constraints exist Initial exploration multilayer modeling

title: Multilayer Networks
text: regime type effects: Use homophily() “correlation” method continuous variables like polity scores regional/categorical patterns: Use mixing_matrix() see full interaction patterns relationship strength: Use compare_networks() ="edges" method="" comprehensive comparison individual actors: Use summary_actor() examine layer-specific centralities

title: Multilayer Networks
text: Multilayer network analysis netify reveals different types political relationships interact, overlap, diverge. moving beyond single-layer analysis, can: Test nuanced theories international behavior Identify actors specialized roles across different domains Understand architecture complex political systems Track relationship portfolios evolve time importantly … fun!

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: examples use ICEWS event data, captures directed interactions 152 countries 2002 2014 across four relation types: verbal cooperation, material cooperation, verbal conflict, material conflict.
code: library(netify) library(ggplot2)  # load the ICEWS event data data(icews)  # preview head(icews[, c("i", "j", "year", "verbCoop", "matlCoop", "verbConf", "matlConf")], 4) #>             i       j year verbCoop matlCoop verbConf matlConf #> 2 Afghanistan Albania 2002        6        1        0        0 #> 3 Afghanistan Albania 2003        1        1        0        0 #> 4 Afghanistan Albania 2004       10        2        0        1 #> 5 Afghanistan Albania 2005        0        0        0        0

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: first step always netify(). create single directed, weighted network representing verbal cooperation time. Key choices: missing_to_zero = FALSE: Preserving NAs distinguishes “observed interaction” “structurally impossible interaction.” matters latent space models missing data structural zeros different likelihoods. output_format = "longit_array": use array format actor set constant across time. nodal_vars dyad_vars: covariates carried amen output automatically.
code: verbal_net = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp"),     dyad_vars = c("matlCoop", "verbConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(FALSE, FALSE),     output_format = "longit_array" )  verbal_net

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: modeling, ’s worth checking basic properties.
code: # quick summary net_summary = summary(verbal_net) head(net_summary[, c("net", "num_actors", "density", "num_edges", "reciprocity")]) #>    net num_actors   density num_edges reciprocity #> 1 2002        152 0.3787034      8692   0.9778217 #> 2 2003        152 0.3871994      8887   0.9632488 #> 3 2004        152 0.4145173      9514   0.9769563 #> 4 2005        152 0.4071976      9346   0.9804325 #> 5 2006        152 0.4108139      9429   0.9771928 #> 6 2007        152 0.4243203      9739   0.9783703 # visualize a single year verbal_2010 = subset(verbal_net, time = "2010") plot(verbal_2010, add_text = FALSE)

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: to_amen() converts single-layer netify object list structure amen::ame() expects. output : Y: [n_actors x n_actors x n_time], adjacency array Xdyad: [n_actors x n_actors x n_dyad_vars x n_time], dyadic covariates Xrow: [n_actors x n_nodal_vars x n_time], sender covariates Xcol: [n_actors x n_nodal_vars x n_time], receiver covariates can plugged directly amen::ame():
code: amen_data = to_amen(verbal_net)  # inspect the resulting array shapes data.frame(     object = c("Y", "Xdyad", "Xrow", "Xcol"),     dims = c(         paste(dim(amen_data$Y), collapse = " x "),         paste(dim(amen_data$Xdyad), collapse = " x "),         paste(dim(amen_data$Xrow), collapse = " x "),         paste(dim(amen_data$Xcol), collapse = " x ")     ) ) #>   object               dims #> 1      Y     152 x 152 x 13 #> 2  Xdyad 152 x 152 x 2 x 13 #> 3   Xrow       152 x 2 x 13 #> 4   Xcol       152 x 2 x 13 library(amen)  # cross-sectional model for a single year ame_fit = ame(     Y = amen_data$Y[, , "2010"],     Xdyad = amen_data$Xdyad[, , , "2010"],     Xrow = amen_data$Xrow[, , "2010"],     Xcol = amen_data$Xcol[, , "2010"],     R = 2,     symmetric = FALSE,     family = "nrm",     nscan = 1000, burn = 500, odens = 1,     plot = FALSE, print = FALSE )

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: longitudinal modeling time-varying actor compositions, use to_lame(). thin specialization to_amen() () auto-pads ragged per-period matrices 3D array via lame::list_to_array(), (b) emits ready--run lame::lame() snippet, (c) provides from_lame_fit() round-tripping posterior predictions back netify plotting.

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: Use to_lame() : Actors enter exit network time (e.g., new states forming, organizations dissolving) want leverage lame’s longitudinal latent space models

what: Create a network with varying actors
title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
code: # use longit_list format for varying actor compositions verbal_list = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     actor_time_uniform = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp"),     dyad_vars = c("matlCoop"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(FALSE) )  verbal_list

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: element list can different set actors. to_lame() also bundles copy-paste-ready snippet pads ragged list 3D array lame::list_to_array() fits lame::lame(): Run snippet fit model. Conceptually:
code: nl = to_lame(verbal_list, lame = TRUE)  # inspect the shape of the returned per-period matrices data.frame(     description = c("number of periods", "first period dims", "last period dims"),     value = c(         length(nl$Y),         paste(dim(nl$Y[[1]]), collapse = " x "),         paste(dim(nl$Y[[length(nl$Y)]]), collapse = " x ")     ) ) #>         description     value #> 1 number of periods        13 #> 2 first period dims 152 x 152 #> 3  last period dims 152 x 152  # check actor counts across time actor_counts = sapply(nl$Y, nrow) actor_counts #> 2002 2003 2004 2005 2006 2007 2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014  #>  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152  152 cat(nl$ame_call) #> U <- unique(unlist(lapply(nl$Y, rownames))) #> padded <- lame::list_to_array(actors = U, Y = nl$Y, Xdyad = nl$Xdyad, Xrow = nl$Xrow, Xcol = nl$Xcol) #> lame::lame(Y = padded$Y, Xdyad = padded$Xdyad, Xrow = padded$Xrow, Xcol = padded$Xcol, family = "normal", method = "mcmc", nscan = 1000, burn = 500) library(lame)  U <- unique(unlist(lapply(nl$Y, rownames))) padded <- lame::list_to_array(actors = U, Y = nl$Y, Xdyad = nl$Xdyad,     Xrow = nl$Xrow, Xcol = nl$Xcol)  lame_fit = lame::lame(     Y = padded$Y, Xdyad = padded$Xdyad,     Xrow = padded$Xrow, Xcol = padded$Xcol,     R = 2, family = "normal",     method = "mcmc", nscan = 1000, burn = 500 )

what: Round-tripping fitted values back to netify
title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: fit hand, from_lame_fit() pulls posterior-mean linear predictor (residual / probability / per-cell quantile) back cross-sectional netify can plotted compared observed network: from_lame_fit() auto-detects link function: probit lame::ame_als() / amen::ame() fits, logit lame::lame() Gibbs fits, explicit link slot taking precedence.
code: # posterior-mean linear predictor pred_net = from_lame_fit(lame_fit, value = "fitted") plot(pred_net, style = "heatmap")  # 90% per-cell credible interval bounds on the probability scale # (only for binary families; here for illustration) lo = from_lame_fit(lame_fit, value = "prob_lower", alpha = 0.05) hi = from_lame_fit(lame_fit, value = "prob_upper", alpha = 0.05)

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: dbn package models multilayer longitudinal networks using Dynamic Bilinear Network models. expects 4D array dimensions [n, n, p, T] p number relation types (layers).

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
code: # verbal cooperation layer (symmetric - mutual diplomatic engagement) verbal_coop = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     output_format = "longit_array",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp"),     dyad_vars = c("verbConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(TRUE) )  # material cooperation layer (directed - trade/aid flows) material_coop = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "matlCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     output_format = "longit_array",     dyad_vars = c("matlConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(FALSE) )

what: Step 2: Combine into a multilayer network
title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: layer_netify() supports mixed directedness, meaning layers can different symmetry settings. common IR applications relations inherently symmetric (alliance status) others directed (trade exports). Notice print output shows mixed directedness across layers.
code: multi_net = layer_netify(     list(verbal_coop, material_coop),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  multi_net

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: to_dbn() extracts multilayer longitudinal netify object exact 4D array format dbn expects. output structure: Y: [n_actors x n_actors x n_layers x n_time], 4D adjacency array Xdyad: [n_actors x n_actors x n_dyad_vars x n_time], dyadic covariates Xrow: [n_actors x n_nodal_vars x n_time], sender covariates Xcol: [n_actors x n_nodal_vars x n_time], receiver covariates
code:
dbn_data = to_dbn(multi_net)  # what did we get? cat("Y dimensions:", paste(dim(dbn_data$Y), collapse = " x "), "
") #> Y dimensions: 152 x 152 x 2 x 13 cat("  Actors:", dim(dbn_data$Y)[1], "
") #>   Actors: 152 cat("  Layers:", paste(dimnames(dbn_data$Y)[[3]], collapse = ", "), "
") #>   Layers: Verbal, Material cat("  Time periods:", dim(dbn_data$Y)[4], "
") #>   Time periods: 13 # fit a dynamic bilinear network model library(dbn)  dbn_fit = dbn(     Y = dbn_data$Y,     R = 2,     nscan = 1000, burn = 500, odens = 1,     plot = FALSE, print = FALSE )

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: to_dbn() also handles single-layer longitudinal networks adding layer dimension size 1:
code: dbn_single = to_dbn(verbal_coop) cat("Single-layer Y dimensions:", paste(dim(dbn_single$Y), collapse = " x "), "\n") #> Single-layer Y dimensions: 152 x 152 x 1 x 13 cat("Layer name:", dimnames(dbn_single$Y)[[3]], "\n") #> Layer name: verbCoop

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: common challenge political science data naturally includes symmetric directed relations. example: Symmetric: UNGA voting agreement, alliance status, shared IGO membership Directed: trade exports, foreign aid, arms transfers netify now handles directly.

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: subset single layer, correct symmetry preserved:
code:
# symmetric layer: average verbal cooperation verbal_symm = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = TRUE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     output_format = "longit_array" )  # directed layer: material cooperation material_dir = netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "matlCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE,     output_format = "longit_array" )  # combine -- mixed directedness is allowed mixed_net = layer_netify(     list(verbal_symm, material_dir),     layer_labels = c("Verbal_Symm", "Material_Dir") )  # check attributes cat("Symmetric attribute:", attr(mixed_net, "symmetric"), "
") #> Symmetric attribute: TRUE FALSE cat("Layer names:", attr(mixed_net, "layers"), "
") #> Layer names: Verbal_Symm Material_Dir # subset to the symmetric layer verbal_only = subset(mixed_net, layers = "Verbal_Symm") cat("Verbal layer symmetric:", attr(verbal_only, "symmetric"), "
") #> Verbal layer symmetric: TRUE  # subset to the directed layer material_only = subset(mixed_net, layers = "Material_Dir") cat("Material layer symmetric:", attr(material_only, "symmetric"), "
") #> Material layer symmetric: FALSE

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: to_dbn() handles mixed-directedness multilayer objects: Note 4D array represents layers uniformly. per-layer symmetry information metadata pass model separately. dbn, typically specify model call.
code: dbn_mixed = to_dbn(mixed_net) cat("Y dimensions:", paste(dim(dbn_mixed$Y), collapse = " x "), "\n") #> Y dimensions: 152 x 152 x 2 x 13

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: missing_to_zero parameter deserves special attention modeling pipelines. default, netify() sets missing_to_zero = TRUE, fills unobserved dyads zeros. fine descriptive analysis can problematic statistical models.

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: latent space models, distinction matters: Zero: “observed dyad interaction,” informs model actors distant latent space NA: “don’t know whether actors interacted,” model marginalizes possible values Recommendation modeling pipelines: Always use missing_to_zero = FALSE unless confident unobserved dyads represent genuine zeros.
code:
# compare the two approaches net_zeros = netify(     icews[icews$year == 2010, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = TRUE )  net_na = netify(     icews[icews$year == 2010, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     missing_to_zero = FALSE )  # count zeros vs NAs raw_zeros = get_raw(net_zeros) raw_na = get_raw(net_na)  cat("With missing_to_zero = TRUE:
") #> With missing_to_zero = TRUE: cat("  Zeros:", sum(raw_zeros == 0, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #>   Zeros: 12976 cat("  NAs:", sum(is.na(raw_zeros)), "
") #>   NAs: 152  cat("
With missing_to_zero = FALSE:
") #>  #> With missing_to_zero = FALSE: cat("  Zeros:", sum(raw_na == 0, na.rm = TRUE), "
") #>   Zeros: 12976 cat("  NAs:", sum(is.na(raw_na)), "
") #>   NAs: 152

title: Pipeline: netify to lame and dbn
text: netify pipeline eliminates manual array construction providing four-step workflow: create network netify() (using missing_to_zero = FALSE modeling applications), combine layers layer_netify() multilayer structure needed, export to_amen() / to_lame() / to_dbn() depending target modeling package, pass resulting output (bundled ame_call snippet) directly model fitting function. fitting, from_lame_fit() round-trips posterior predictions back netify can plotted alongside observed network.

title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: simplest case: one snapshot, one model. to_statnet() carries nodal attributes vertex attributes dyadic attributes edge attributes.
code: # build a single-year netify object with nodal and dyadic attributes icews_2010 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  verb_coop <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight   = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp"),     dyad_vars  = "matlCoop" ) #> ℹ `missing_to_zero` is set to "TRUE" (the default). #> ! Missing dyads will be filled with zeros. For latent space or other #>   statistical network models, structural zeros and missing data have different #>   meanings. Set `missing_to_zero = FALSE` to preserve NAs if this distinction #>   matters for your analysis. #> This message is displayed once per session.  # convert to statnet 'network' object sn_2010 <- to_statnet(verb_coop) #> ! Nodal columns with "NA" detected: "i_polity2" and "i_log_gdp". Ergm terms #>   like `nodecov()`/`nodematch()` will refuse to fit. #> ℹ Use `drop_na_actors(net, cols = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp'))` (or impute) #>   before refitting. #> ℹ Dyad covariates attached as per-edge attributes under "matlCoop_e" and as #>   network-level matrices under their original names ("matlCoop"). #> ℹ For `ergm::edgecov()` use the matrix name (e.g. `edgecov('matlCoop')`); the #>   "_e" per-edge attribute is for descriptive use such as edge styling. #> This message is displayed once per session. sn_2010 #>  Network attributes: #>   vertices = 152  #>   directed = TRUE  #>   hyper = FALSE  #>   loops = FALSE  #>   multiple = FALSE  #>   bipartite = FALSE  #>   verbCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   matlCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   total edges= 9976  #>     missing edges= 0  #>     non-missing edges= 9976  #>  #>  Vertex attribute names:  #>     i_log_gdp i_polity2 vertex.names  #>  #>  Edge attribute names not shown

what: Before you fit: three sanity checks
title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: ERGMs fail cryptic ways underlying network malformed. Three checks catch “invalid output statistic” headaches ever call ergm(): na_cols non-empty, drop affected actors drop_na_actors(verb_coop, cols = na_cols) impute converting. Now fit ergm: One footgun worth flagging: nodecov("i_polity2") refuse fit vertex NA polity score. Either subset actors complete covariates impute passing to_statnet(). netify emits one-shot inform detects NAs nodal attribute stashes offending column names resulting network object can introspect: non-empty, drop impute columns fitting ergm() formulas reference nodecov() / nodematch(). drop_na_actors() helper one call works cross-sectional, longitudinal, bipartite netlets:
code: # 1. NAs in nodal covariates referenced by nodecov / nodematch nd <- attr(verb_coop, "nodal_data") na_cols <- names(nd)[vapply(nd, function(c) any(is.na(c)), logical(1))] na_cols <- setdiff(na_cols, c("actor", "time", "layer")) na_cols #> [1] "i_polity2" "i_log_gdp"  # 2. isolates -- ergm fits but some terms (e.g. gwdegree) degenerate m <- get_raw(verb_coop) bin <- (m != 0) & !is.na(m) deg_total <- rowSums(bin) + colSums(bin) isolates <- rownames(m)[deg_total == 0] length(isolates) #> [1] 0  # 3. symmetry: the netify flag must match how your model treats ties attr(verb_coop, "symmetric") #> [1] FALSE # Note: this chunk is not evaluated by default to keep vignette build fast. # Replace eval = FALSE with eval = TRUE to actually run. set.seed(6886) m <- ergm(     sn_2010 ~ edges +         nodecov("i_polity2") +         nodecov("i_log_gdp") ) summary(m) attr(sn_2010, "netify_na_cols")   # character vector of NA-bearing nodal vars clean <- drop_na_actors(verb_coop, cols = attr(sn_2010, "netify_na_cols")) sn_2010_clean <- to_statnet(clean)

title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: dyadic covariate passed netify() (e.g. dyad_vars = "matlCoop") attached resulting network object two places: network-level attribute original name (full n x n matrix), accessible via network::get.network.attribute(sn, "matlCoop"), per-edge attribute <var>_e (, matlCoop_e), populated edges actually exist. trailing _e disambiguates per-edge edgelist network-level matrix. ergm::edgecov(), pass original (matrix) name — edgecov() resolves argument network-level matrix attribute, _e per-edge alias work : _e per-edge attribute exposed descriptive uses (example, coloring edges covariate network::plot.network). to_statnet() emits one-shot inform listing forms first time attaches dyadic covariates. Goodness--fit, mcmc.diagnostics, postestimation tools live ergm .
code: m <- ergm(sn_2010 ~ edges + edgecov("matlCoop"))

title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: longitudinal netify, to_statnet() returns named list — one network object per time period: Fit separate ergm per period: coevolution models (ties change function past ties), look tergm statnet suite — netify provides data, tergm modeling. typical tergm 4.x call per-period list looks like : tergm 4.x split formulation, Form() models formation new edges periods, Persist() models persistence existing edges one period next; formulas accept ergm terms (edges, nodecov, nodematch, mutual, gwesp, etc.). (Note: i_polity2 missing values country-years — to_statnet() flags stashes offending column names attr(net, "netify_na_cols") can drop impute fitting.)
code: verb_longit <- netify(     icews[icews$year %in% 2010:2012, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = "i_polity2" )  sn_list <- to_statnet(verb_longit) #> ! Nodal columns with "NA" detected: "i_polity2". Ergm terms like #>   `nodecov()`/`nodematch()` will refuse to fit. #> ℹ Use `drop_na_actors(net, cols = c('i_polity2'))` (or impute) before #>   refitting. #> This message is displayed once per session. length(sn_list) #> [1] 3 names(sn_list) #> [1] "2010" "2011" "2012" class(sn_list[[1]]) #> [1] "network" set.seed(6886) fits <- lapply(sn_list, function(n) {     ergm(n ~ edges + nodecov("i_polity2")) }) # Longitudinal ERGM via tergm 4.x library(tergm) set.seed(6886)  nets <- to_statnet(verb_longit)   # named list of network objects fit <- tergm(     nets ~ Form(~ edges + nodecov("i_polity2")) +            Persist(~ edges),     estimate = "CMLE",     times = seq_along(nets) ) summary(fit)

title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: Multilayer ergm modeling research literature. simple pragmatic approach fit one ergm per layer. to_statnet() handles automatically: pass multilayer netify, get back named list keyed layer. element network object can plug straight ergm().
code: verb <- netify(icews_2010, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",                symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") matl <- netify(icews_2010, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",                symmetric = FALSE, weight = "matlCoop") multi <- layer_netify(list(verbal = verb, material = matl))  sn_multi <- to_statnet(multi) length(sn_multi) #> [1] 2 names(sn_multi) #> [1] "verbal"   "material"

what: 4. Round-tripping simulated networks back to netify
title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: useful descriptive check fitting ergm simulate fit, compute descriptives simulated networks, compare observed network. simulate.ergm() returns network objects, means can pipe straight back netify netify’s descriptive tools: gives observed-vs-simulated comparisons density, reciprocity, transitivity, mean degree, etc. — useful assessing whether ergm captured descriptive features care .
code: set.seed(6886) sims <- simulate(m, nsim = 100)  # list of network objects  # convert each simulated network back into a netify for comparison sim_nets <- lapply(sims, function(s) to_netify(s))  # compare observed vs simulated at the structural level all_nets <- c(list(observed = verb_coop), sim_nets) struct_comp <- compare_networks(all_nets, what = "structure")

title: Pipeline: netify to ergm (statnet)
text: modeling beyond ergm: Latent factor / additive-multiplicative: pipeline_lame_dbn vignette Social relations model: netify-dev/srm via to_amen(net) Social influence regression (Minhas & Hoff): netify-dev/sir fun!
code: # build → attach attrs → export → model net <- netify(df, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "x") net <- add_node_vars(net, attrs, actor = "id") sn  <- to_statnet(net)               # single network, longit list, or multilayer list m   <- ergm(sn ~ edges + ...)        # modeling happens in ergm/statnet

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: vignette demonstrates use netify analyze changes international relations time using data Integrated Crisis Early Warning System (ICEWS). ’ll explore fun questions countries interact: Talk vs. Action: countries cooperate verbally (diplomatic statements) also cooperate materially (aid, trade)? Friend Foe: cooperation conflict separate networks, countries cooperate also tend conflicts? Temporal Stability: international relationships persist time fleeting? Structural Evolution: overall patterns international interactions change across years? ’ll use compare_networks() answer questions: compare_networks(): Compares networks across multiple dimensions - edges, structure, nodes, attributes. Think running comprehensive diagnostic two () networks understand ’s similar, ’s different, matters. Function notes: Multi-method flexibility: Choose correlation, Jaccard, Hamming, QAP, spectral distance, “” Four comparison modes: Compare edges (connects ), structure (density, clustering), nodes (actor composition), attributes (actor characteristics) Statistical rigor: Built-permutation tests significance Output: Returns information edge changes, similarity metrics, structural differences
code: library(netify) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(patchwork)

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
