title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: First, let’s create separate networks different types interactions single year:
code: # Create networks for different interaction types in 2002 icews_2002 <- icews[icews$year == 2002, ]  # Verbal cooperation network verb_coop_2002 <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop",     symmetric = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp', 'i_region') )  # Material cooperation network matl_coop_2002 <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlCoop",     symmetric = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp', 'i_region') )  # Verbal conflict network verb_conf_2002 <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbConf",     symmetric = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp', 'i_region') )  # Material conflict network matl_conf_2002 <- netify(     icews_2002,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlConf",     symmetric = FALSE,     nodal_vars = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp', 'i_region') )

what: 1. Comparing Cooperation Networks: Verbal vs. Material
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: countries cooperate verbally also cooperate materially? Let’s use compare_networks() method = "" get comprehensive comparison: Verbal vs Material Cooperation Comparison Metrics Edge Changes Networks
code:
# Compare verbal vs material cooperation coop_comparison <- compare_networks(     list(         verbal = verb_coop_2002,         material = matl_coop_2002     ),     method = "all" )  # Render the summary frame as a knitr table knitr::kable(     coop_comparison$summary,     caption = "Verbal vs Material Cooperation Comparison Metrics",     digits = 3,     align = "c" ) # Display edge changes edge_changes_df <- data.frame(     Change_Type = c("Added", "Removed", "Maintained"),     Count = c(         coop_comparison$edge_changes[[1]]$added,         coop_comparison$edge_changes[[1]]$removed,         coop_comparison$edge_changes[[1]]$maintained     ) ) knitr::kable(     edge_changes_df,     caption = "Edge Changes Between Networks",     align = "c" ) # Interpret results cat("
**INTERPRETATION**
") ##  ## **INTERPRETATION** if (coop_comparison$summary$correlation > 0.7) {     cat("Strong alignment: Countries that cooperate verbally tend to cooperate materially
") } else if (coop_comparison$summary$correlation > 0.4) {     cat("Moderate alignment: Verbal and material cooperation partially overlap
") } else {     cat("Weak alignment: Verbal promises don't strongly translate to material actions
") } ## Moderate alignment: Verbal and material cooperation partially overlap

what: 2. Cooperation vs. Conflict: Testing Network Relationships
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: cooperation conflict networks related? Let’s use QAP (Quadratic Assignment Procedure) method statistical testing:
code:
# Perform QAP test between cooperation and conflict coop_conf_qap <- compare_networks(     list(         cooperation = verb_coop_2002,         conflict = verb_conf_2002     ),     method = "qap",     n_permutations = 500, # Reduced for vignette speed     seed = 12345 # For reproducibility )  # Construct message qap_msg <- paste0(     "**QAP Test Results:**

",     "- Observed correlation: ", round(coop_conf_qap$summary$qap_correlation, 3), "
",     "- P-value: ",     ifelse(         coop_conf_qap$summary$qap_pvalue == 0,         "< 0.002 (1/500 permutations)",         round(coop_conf_qap$summary$qap_pvalue, 3)     ),     "
" )  # Add interpretation based on significance if (coop_conf_qap$summary$qap_pvalue < 0.05) {     if (coop_conf_qap$summary$qap_correlation > 0) {         qap_msg <- paste0(             qap_msg,             "→ Cooperation and conflict networks are positively correlated
",             "  (Countries interact through both cooperation AND conflict)
"         )     } else {         qap_msg <- paste0(             qap_msg,             "→ Cooperation and conflict networks are negatively correlated
",             "  (Different countries engage in cooperation vs conflict)
"         )     } } else {     qap_msg <- paste0(         qap_msg,         "→ No significant relationship between cooperation and conflict patterns
"     ) }  # Print result cat(qap_msg) ## **QAP Test Results:** ##  ## - Observed correlation: 0.506 ## - P-value: < 0.002 (1/500 permutations) ## → Cooperation and conflict networks are positively correlated ##   (Countries interact through both cooperation AND conflict)

previous_headings: 2. Cooperation vs. Conflict: Testing Network Relationships
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: positive correlation (0.506) p-value < 0.002 tells us countries cooperate also tend conflicts. counterintuitive finding reflects multiplex nature international relations: countries appear frequently international news tend cooperative conflictual interactions. High-interaction dyads accumulate cooperative conflictual events; correlation therefore captures activity rather affinity. contrast, countries rarely interact neither cooperation conflict events recorded. pattern well-documented international relations literature “interaction density” effect.

previous_headings: 2. Cooperation vs. Conflict: Testing Network Relationships
what: Advanced QAP Options: Different Permutation Schemes
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: compare_networks() function supports multiple permutation schemes sophisticated hypothesis testing: QAP Results Different Permutation Schemes Understanding Different Permutation Types: Classic: Standard node label permutation. Fast widely used. Freedman-Lane: Controls network autocorrelation residualizing permutation. DSP-MRQAP: Double Semi-Partialling - removes mutual influence networks. Note MRQAP methods (Freedman-Lane DSP) often yield higher p-values account network dependencies. Freedman-Lane DSP residualize matrices permutation, point estimate can shrink occasionally flip sign; matters (non-)significance accounting structural autocorrelation.
code: # Compare different permutation schemes qap_methods <- list(     classic = compare_networks(         list(cooperation = verb_coop_2002, conflict = verb_conf_2002),         method = "qap",         permutation_type = "classic",         n_permutations = 500,         seed = 12345     ),     freedman_lane = compare_networks(         list(cooperation = verb_coop_2002, conflict = verb_conf_2002),         method = "qap",         permutation_type = "freedman_lane",         n_permutations = 500,         seed = 12345     ),     dsp_mrqap = compare_networks(         list(cooperation = verb_coop_2002, conflict = verb_conf_2002),         method = "qap",         permutation_type = "dsp_mrqap",         n_permutations = 500,         seed = 12345     ) )  # Compare results qap_results <- data.frame(     Permutation_Type = c("Classic", "Freedman-Lane", "DSP-MRQAP"),     Correlation = c(         qap_methods$classic$summary$qap_correlation,         qap_methods$freedman_lane$summary$qap_correlation,         qap_methods$dsp_mrqap$summary$qap_correlation     ),     P_Value = c(         qap_methods$classic$summary$qap_pvalue,         qap_methods$freedman_lane$summary$qap_pvalue,         qap_methods$dsp_mrqap$summary$qap_pvalue     ) )  knitr::kable(qap_results,               caption = "QAP Results with Different Permutation Schemes",              digits = 3)

code: # Compare using different correlation types cor_comparison <- data.frame(     Correlation_Type = c("Pearson", "Spearman"),     Cooperation_Networks = c(         compare_networks(             list(verbal = verb_coop_2002, material = matl_coop_2002),             correlation_type = "pearson"         )$summary$correlation,         compare_networks(             list(verbal = verb_coop_2002, material = matl_coop_2002),             correlation_type = "spearman"         )$summary$correlation     ),     Conflict_Networks = c(         compare_networks(             list(verbal = verb_conf_2002, material = matl_conf_2002),             correlation_type = "pearson"         )$summary$correlation,         compare_networks(             list(verbal = verb_conf_2002, material = matl_conf_2002),             correlation_type = "spearman"         )$summary$correlation     ) )  knitr::kable(cor_comparison,              caption = "Pearson vs. Spearman Correlations",              digits = 3)

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: international networks evolve time? pass longitudinal netify object compare_networks(), automatically compares consecutive time periods: Temporal Network Comparison Summary
code: # Create networks for multiple years years_to_compare <- seq(2002, 2014, by = 4)  # Create cooperation networks for each year coop_net_longit <- netify(     icews[icews$year %in% years_to_compare, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     weight = "verbCoop",     symmetric = FALSE,     output_format = "longit_list" )  # Compare across time - automatic pairwise comparisons temporal_comparison <- compare_networks(     coop_net_longit,     method = "all" ) # Render the summary frame as a knitr table knitr::kable(     temporal_comparison$summary,     caption = "Temporal Network Comparison Summary",     digits = 3,     align = "c" )

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: Header Changes: Type: temporal - Indicates ’re comparing time periods longitudinal network Networks compared: 4 - 4 time periods (2002, 2006, 2010, 2014) Summary Statistics Table: Instead single comparison, see summary statistics across pairwise comparisons: mean correlation (0.828): average, networks highly correlated across time sd (0.034): low standard deviation indicates consistent similarity min (0.771) max (0.874): Even least similar pair years correlation > 0.77 high correlation tells us cooperation networks quite stable time - countries tend cooperate across years. Edge Changes Section: Shows pairwise comparisons (just consecutive years): 2002_vs_2006: First comparison 2010_vs_2014: Last consecutive comparison Notice time gaps increase, edges added/removed

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: Notice “Maintained” edges dominate - relationships persist across time periods, confirming stability observed correlation metrics.
code: # Extract edge changes over time edge_change_data <- data.frame(     Comparison = names(temporal_comparison$edge_changes),     Added = sapply(temporal_comparison$edge_changes, function(x) x$added),     Removed = sapply(temporal_comparison$edge_changes, function(x) x$removed),     Maintained = sapply(temporal_comparison$edge_changes, function(x) x$maintained) )  # Reshape for plotting edge_change_long <- edge_change_data |>     pivot_longer(         cols = c(Added, Removed, Maintained),         names_to = "Change_Type",         values_to = "Count"     )  # Plot edge changes ggplot(edge_change_long, aes(x = Comparison, y = Count, fill = Change_Type)) +     geom_col(position = "dodge") +     scale_fill_manual(         values = c(             "Added" = "#A8D5BA",             "Removed" = "#E8B4B8",             "Maintained" = "#95A99C"         )     ) +     labs(         title = "Edge Changes Between Years",         subtitle = "Tracking relationship dynamics over time",         x = "Year Comparison",         y = "Number of Edges"     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "top",         axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)     )

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: comparing multiple networks, ’s important adjust p-values multiple comparisons. p_adjust parameter offers several correction methods: QAP P-values (Adjusted using BH method) Available p-value adjustment methods: "none": adjustment (default) "holm": Holm’s method (controls family-wise error rate) "BH": Benjamini-Hochberg (controls false discovery rate) "": Benjamini-Yekutieli (conservative FDR control) Use p-value adjustment making many comparisons avoid inflated Type error rates.
code:
# Demonstrate multiple testing correction # Compare all years pairwise with QAP multi_year_comparison <- compare_networks(     coop_net_longit,     method = "qap",     n_permutations = 500,  # Lower for vignette     p_adjust = "BH",  # Benjamini-Hochberg correction     seed = 123 )  # Show adjusted p-values cat("Number of pairwise comparisons:",      choose(length(names(coop_net_longit)), 2), "
") ## Number of pairwise comparisons: 6 cat("P-value adjustment method: Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR)

") ## P-value adjustment method: Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR) # Display the p-value matrix knitr::kable(     round(multi_year_comparison$significance_tests$qap_pvalues, 4),     caption = "QAP P-values (Adjusted using BH method)",     align = "c" )

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: table shows network-wide properties evolve: n_nodes (152): Number countries remains constant - actors throughout n_edges: Increases 8,692 (2002) 9,774 (2014) - connections time density: Increases 0.379 0.426 - network becomes denser reciprocity: Stays high (0.97-0.98) — correlation directed tie reverse nearly perfect, meaning cooperation B closely tracks cooperation B magnitude. (Note: correlation-based measure, proportion mutual edges.) transitivity: Around 0.61-0.64 - moderate clustering (friend friend often friend) mean_degree: Increases 57.2 64.3 - average country partners time increasing density mean degree suggest growing interconnectedness international cooperation.

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: heatmap reveals density, number edges, mean degree increase 14-15% 2002 2010 (remain ~12-13% baseline 2014), reciprocity stays essentially flat (within 1% either direction) transitivity shows modest growth (~3-5%). Many ways visualize structural changes … also just line plot:
code: # Prepare data for visualization # struct_comparison$summary contains a # data.frame with structural metrics over time struct_data <- struct_comparison$summary  # Calculate percent changes from baseline baseline_year <- struct_data$network[1] struct_long <- struct_data |>     select(-num_actors) |> # Remove num_actors since it doesn't change much     pivot_longer(cols = -network, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>     group_by(metric) |>     mutate(         first_value = value[1],         percent_change = (value - first_value) / first_value * 100     ) |>     ungroup()  # Create heatmap of changes ggplot(     filter(struct_long, network != baseline_year),     aes(x = network, y = metric, fill = percent_change) ) +     geom_tile(color = "white") +     geom_text(aes(label = round(percent_change, 1)), color = "black", size = 4) +     scale_fill_gradient2(         low = "#d73027", mid = "white", high = "#1a9850",         midpoint = 0, name = "% Change"     ) +     labs(         title = "Structural Changes Heatmap",         subtitle = paste("Percent change from", baseline_year),         x = "", y = ""     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "top",         axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0)     ) # Reshape data for visualization struct_long <- struct_data |>     select(-num_actors) |> # Remove num_actors since it doesn't change     pivot_longer(cols = -network, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>     group_by(metric) |>     mutate(         # Calculate percent change from first year         first_value = value[1],         percent_change = (value - first_value) / first_value * 100,         # Also calculate year-to-year change         yoy_change = (value - lag(value)) / lag(value) * 100     ) |>     ungroup()  # Visualize structural changes over time ggplot(struct_long, aes(x = network, y = value, group = metric)) +     geom_line(size = 1.2) +     geom_point(size = 3) +     facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +     labs(         title = "Structural Properties Over Time",         subtitle = "Evolution of network characteristics",         x = "", y = ""     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "none",         strip.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = "black"),         strip.text = element_text(color = "white", hjust = 0)     )

what: 6. Deep Dive: Using return_details for Comprehensive Analysis
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: return_details = TRUE option provides access full comparison matrices: Relationship Dynamics 2002-2014: Stable relationships: 6633 New relationships: 3141 Ended relationships: 2059 Total change rate: 43.9% Weight correlation maintained edges: 0.762 → Stable cooperation intensities continuing relationships
code:
# Compare 2002 and 2014 cooperation networks with full details early_late_comp <- compare_networks(     # note subset here is     # subset.netify() function     subset(         coop_net_longit,         time = c("2002", "2014")     ),     method = "all",     return_details = TRUE,     n_permutations = 1000 # Lower for vignette speed )  # Access detailed comparison matrices names(early_late_comp$details) ## [1] "correlation_matrix" "jaccard_matrix"     "hamming_matrix"     ## [4] "spectral_matrix" # Get edge changes changes <- early_late_comp$edge_changes[[1]]  # Summary of relationship dynamics changes_summary <- paste0(     "**Relationship Dynamics 2002-2014:**

",     "- Stable relationships: ", changes$maintained, "
",     "- New relationships: ", changes$added, "
",     "- Ended relationships: ", changes$removed, "
",     "- Total change rate: ",     round((changes$added + changes$removed) /         (changes$maintained + changes$added + changes$removed) * 100, 1), "%
" )  if (!is.na(changes$weight_correlation)) {     changes_summary <- paste0(         changes_summary,         "- Weight correlation for maintained edges: ",         round(changes$weight_correlation, 3), "
"     )      if (changes$weight_correlation > 0.7) {         changes_summary <- paste0(             changes_summary,             "  → Stable cooperation intensities for continuing relationships
"         )     } else {         changes_summary <- paste0(             changes_summary,             "  → Cooperation intensities vary even for maintained relationships
"         )     } }

what: 7. Spectral Distance: Comparing Network Structure Through Eigenvalues
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: spectral distance approach provides way compare networks based fundamental structural properties captured eigenvalues (See Shimada et al 2016 useful introduction): Spectral Distance Results
code:
# Compare networks using spectral distance spectral_comp <- compare_networks(     list(         "2002" = coop_net_longit[["2002"]],         "2014" = coop_net_longit[["2014"]]     ),     method = "spectral" )  # Render the spectral summary as a knitr table knitr::kable(     spectral_comp$summary,     caption = "Spectral Distance Results",     digits = 2,     align = "c" ) # Interpretation # Build spectral interpretation message spec_dist <- spectral_comp$summary$spectral  spec_msg <- paste0(     "**SPECTRAL DISTANCE INTERPRETATION**

",     "- Spectral distance: ", round(spec_dist, 2), "
" )  # Add interpretation # Note: Spectral distance should be interpreted relative to other comparisons # in your study, as the scale depends on network size interpretation <- paste0(     "→ The spectral distance of ", round(spec_dist, 0),      " should be interpreted relative to other network comparisons in your study.
",     "  Larger values indicate greater structural differences." )  # Combine and print cat(paste0(spec_msg, "→ ", interpretation)) ## **SPECTRAL DISTANCE INTERPRETATION** ##  ## - Spectral distance: 14369.14 ## → → The spectral distance of 14369 should be interpreted relative to other network comparisons in your study. ##   Larger values indicate greater structural differences.

previous_headings: 7. Spectral Distance: Comparing Network Structure Through Eigenvalues
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: Spectral distance measures different two networks comparing eigenvalue spectra. captures global structural properties metrics might miss: measures: distance sorted eigenvalues two network Laplacian matrices Scale: Ranges 0 (identical spectra) potentially large values depending network size Captures global network structure Sensitive community structure clustering patterns Robust node relabeling Detecting fundamental structural changes time Comparing networks different connectivity patterns Identifying networks similar spectral properties (useful network classification)

previous_headings: 7. Spectral Distance: Comparing Network Structure Through Eigenvalues
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: large networks (>1000 nodes), computing eigenvalues can computationally expensive. spectral_rank parameter allows use top-k eigenvalues. Example: adapt code large network objects run . top eigenvalues capture significant structural features. good rule thumb use round(sqrt(n)) eigenvalues, n number nodes.
code: # For large networks, use spectral_rank to improve performance spectral_comp_fast <- compare_networks(     list(large_net1, large_net2),     method = "spectral",     spectral_rank = 50  # Use only top 50 eigenvalues (rule of thumb: round(sqrt(n))) )

previous_headings: 7. Spectral Distance: Comparing Network Structure Through Eigenvalues
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: Network Similarity Metrics: 2002 vs 2014  Note spectral distance different scale metrics (typically 0-1), ’s best interpreted relative spectral distance values rather absolute terms.
code: # Compare all methods including spectral full_comp <- compare_networks(     list(         early = coop_net_longit[["2002"]],         late = coop_net_longit[["2014"]]     ),     method = "all",     return_details = TRUE )  # Extract similarity metrics (0-1 scale) and spectral distance separately similarity_metrics <- data.frame(     Method = c("Correlation", "Jaccard", "Hamming"),     Value = c(         full_comp$summary$correlation,         full_comp$summary$jaccard,         full_comp$summary$hamming     ) )  spectral_distance <- full_comp$summary$spectral  # Display similarity metrics table knitr::kable(     rbind(         similarity_metrics,         data.frame(Method = "Spectral Distance", Value = spectral_distance)     ),     caption = "Network Similarity Metrics: 2002 vs 2014",     digits = 3,     align = "c" ) # Create visualization for similarity metrics p_similarity <- ggplot(similarity_metrics, aes(x = Method, y = Value)) +     geom_col(width = 0.7) +     geom_text(aes(label = round(Value, 3)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +     scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +     labs(         title = "Similarity Metrics (0-1 Scale)",         x = NULL, y = "Similarity Score"     ) +     theme_bw() +     theme(         panel.border = element_blank(),         axis.ticks = element_blank(),         legend.position = "top"     ) +     coord_flip()  # Create separate note for spectral distance spectral_note <- paste("Spectral Distance:", round(spectral_distance, 2))  # Combine the plot with spectral distance information library(patchwork) p_similarity + plot_annotation(     title = "Network Comparison: Multiple Metrics",     subtitle = paste("2002 vs 2014 Cooperation Networks |", spectral_note),     theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")) )

what: 8. Multilayer Networks: Comparing Different Relationship Types
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: compare_networks() function automatically handles multilayer networks created layer_netify(). allows compare different types relationships (layers) within set actors: Multilayer Network Comparison Summary
code: # Create multilayer network for 2010 icews_2010 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create individual networks for each layer verb_coop_2010 <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop",     symmetric = FALSE )  matl_coop_2010 <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlCoop",     symmetric = FALSE )  verb_conf_2010 <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbConf",     symmetric = FALSE )  matl_conf_2010 <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlConf",     symmetric = FALSE )  # Combine into multilayer network multilayer_2010 <- layer_netify(     list(         verbal_coop = verb_coop_2010,         material_coop = matl_coop_2010,         verbal_conf = verb_conf_2010,         material_conf = matl_conf_2010     ) )  # Compare layers automatically layer_comparison <- compare_networks(multilayer_2010, method = "all") # Render the layer-comparison summary as a knitr table knitr::kable(     layer_comparison$summary,     caption = "Multilayer Network Comparison Summary",     digits = 3,     align = "c" )

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: compare_networks() function go-tool understanding networks differ change: Basic usage: Key parameters remember: method: “correlation” (default), “jaccard”, “hamming”, “qap”, “spectral”, “” : “edges” (default), “structure”, “nodes”, “attributes” return_details: Set TRUE get full comparison matrices n_permutations: QAP significance testing (default 5000) permutation_type: “classic” (default), “degree_preserving”, “freedman_lane”, “dsp_mrqap” correlation_type: “pearson” (default) “spearman” seed: Set reproducible permutation tests p_adjust: “none” (default), “holm”, “BH”, “” multiple testing correction spectral_rank: Number eigenvalues spectral distance (0 = , default). Rule thumb: round(sqrt(n)) networks many nodes get: Summary statistics: Correlation, Jaccard similarity, Hamming distance, spectral distance Edge changes: Detailed counts added, removed, maintained edges Statistical tests: Permutation tests significance Flexible comparisons: Works number networks, automatically handles longitudinal data Use method = "" initial exploration Use = "structure" compare network-level properties Set return_details = TRUE need full comparison matrices longitudinal data, just pass netify object - handles rest Use permutation_type = "freedman_lane" "dsp_mrqap" comparing networks may autocorrelation Set correlation_type = "spearman" networks skewed weight distributions Use p_adjust making multiple comparisons control false discovery rate Set spectral_rank = round(sqrt(n)) large networks improve performance
code: # Compare two networks comparison <- compare_networks(list(net1, net2))  # Compare longitudinal networks automatically comparison <- compare_networks(longitudinal_netify_object)

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: compare_networks() function includes several advanced parameters help tailor analysis specific network characteristics: Permutation Types: classic (default): Standard node label permutation. Use general network comparisons. degree_preserving: Maintains degree sequences edge swaps. Essential sparse, binary networks high degree heterogeneity. freedman_lane: Residualizes permutation. Use suspect network autocorrelation (e.g., geographic networks, social influence). dsp_mrqap: Double semi-partialling. conservative option; use multiple regression-style comparisons networks strong mutual dependencies. Correlation Types: pearson (default): Standard linear correlation. Appropriate normally distributed edge weights. spearman: Rank-based correlation. Use edge weights skewed, zero-inflated, contain outliers. P-value Adjustments: none (default): adjustment. Fine single comparisons. holm: Controls family-wise error rate. Use small number planned comparisons. BH (Benjamini-Hochberg): Controls false discovery rate. Best many comparisons (e.g., comparing time periods). (Benjamini-Yekutieli): conservative FDR control. Use comparisons strongly dependent. Binary Metrics (binary networks): phi: Phi coefficient. Standard choice, sensitive marginal imbalance. simple_matching: Proportion matching edges. Use absence edges meaningful presence. mean_centered: Centers matrices correlation. Reduces bias density differences.

Informs SAOM rate functions ERGM temporal windows Track actor turnover Choose relational event models vs. panel frameworks Identify density, reciprocity, transitivity trends Suggests candidate ERGM terms SAOM effects Detect regime-change style discontinuities May require sample breaks change-point models Test correlations spurious network constraints Guides controls must enter model

previous_headings: Using compare_networks() in Your Research Workflow
title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: ERGMs: High transitivity → include GWESP terms Degree heterogeneity → add degree-based terms (e.g., gwidegree) Reciprocity changes → include mutual edge terms Spectral distance jumps → consider time-varying parameters SAOMs: Edge turnover rates → set appropriate rate functions Structural stability → choose evaluation vs. creation effects Actor composition changes → include composition change effects Latent Space Models: Spectral distance magnitude → suggests latent dimensionality Community structure (eigenvalues) → number latent positions Temporal stability → fixed vs. dynamic latent positions

title: Tracking Changes in Cooperation and Conflict: Comparing Networks
text: Example: adapt template networks run .
code: # Comprehensive diagnostic before ERGM fitting diagnostic_results <- compare_networks(     network_list,     method = "all",     what = "edges",     permutation_type = "degree_preserving",  # For sparse networks     correlation_type = "spearman",           # For skewed weights     n_permutations = 10000,                  # For final analysis     p_adjust = "BH",                         # Multiple comparisons     spectral_rank = 75,                      # Large network optimization     return_details = TRUE,                   # For custom analysis     seed = 12345                             # Reproducibility )  # Extract insights for model specification if (diagnostic_results$summary$transitivity > 0.3) {     # High clustering suggests including triadic closure terms     ergm_formula <- formula(net ~ edges + gwesp(0.5, fixed=TRUE)) }  if (diagnostic_results$spectral > 100) {     # Large spectral distance suggests structural regime change     # Consider separate models for different periods }

text: Transform relational data network object just one function:
code: library(netify) data(icews)  # Create a network from dyadic data icews_conflict <- netify(   icews,   actor1 = 'i',    actor2 = 'j',   time = 'year',   symmetric = FALSE,    weight = 'matlConf',   nodal_vars = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp', 'i_region') )  # Print the netify object print(icews_conflict) ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! • Unipartite • Asymmetric • Weights from `matlConf` • Longitudinal: 13 Periods • # Unique Actors: 152 Network Summary Statistics (averaged across time):           dens miss  mean recip trans matlConf 0.113    0 1.471 0.594 0.387 • Nodal Features: None • Dyad Features: None

title: Network Tools
text: netify’s plotting system highly customizable. ’s can create sophisticated visualization:
code: # Create democracy indicator icews$i_democ <- factor(   ifelse(icews$i_polity2 >= 6, 1, 0),    levels = c(0, 1),    labels = c("Non-democracy", "Democracy") )  # Add it to the network icews_conflict <- add_node_vars(   icews_conflict, icews,   actor = 'i', time = 'year',   node_vars = 'i_democ' )  plot(   icews_conflict,   # Log transform weights   mutate_weight = log1p,    # Map node attributes to aesthetics   node_color_by = 'i_region',    node_size_by = 'i_log_gdp',    node_shape_by = 'i_democ',   # set global node alpha   node_alpha = .7,   # set global edge alpha   edge_linewidth = .1,   # Filter data   node_filter = ~ !is.na(i_democ),   time_filter = c('2002', '2004', '2008', '2014'),   # clean up plot labels   edge_alpha_label = 'Log(Matl.\n Conf.)',   node_color_label = '',   node_size_label = 'Log(GDP)',   node_shape_label = ''   ) +   theme(legend.position = 'right') +    scale_color_brewer(palette = 'Set1')

title: Network Tools
text: netify stores adjacencies dense matrices/arrays, keeps API uniform makes memory binding constraint large N (single dense N x N slice costs 8 * N^2 bytes; e.g. ~7.6 MB N=1K, ~191 MB N=5K, ~1.7 GB N=15K). knobs benchmarks keep mind: Sparse matrix guard. Passing Matrix::sparseMatrix (e.g. dgCMatrix) netify() densifies internally. N > 5000 density 1%, netify() aborts guidance message rather silently allocating gigabytes. Override force_dense = TRUE really want dense object, build edgelist data.frame skip matrix intermediate entirely. Fast actor stats. summary_actor() defaults stats = "" (degree + closeness + betweenness + eigenvector + HITS). closeness/betweenness paths dominate wall-clock large N, N >= getOption("netify.fast_threshold", 1500L) netify auto-promotes default call stats = "fast", returns degree- strength-style columns. Pass stats = "" explicitly force centralities, raise threshold via options(netify.fast_threshold = ...). Indicative timings (single laptop core, directed weighted toy nets): 10K+ actor / weekly-slice workflows, prefer edgelist inputs, set stats = "fast" explicitly, consider to_igraph() heavy centrality work.

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: add_dyad_vars (also available add_edge_attributes) merges additional dyadic (edge-level) variables data.frame existing netify object. function allows incrementally build dyadic attributes network initial creation, useful variables come different sources need different preprocessing.

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
code: add_dyad_vars(   netlet,   dyad_data,   actor1 = NULL,   actor2 = NULL,   time = NULL,   dyad_vars = NULL,   dyad_vars_symmetric = NULL,   replace_existing = FALSE )  add_edge_attributes(   netlet,   dyad_data,   actor1 = NULL,   actor2 = NULL,   time = NULL,   dyad_vars = NULL,   dyad_vars_symmetric = NULL,   replace_existing = FALSE )

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") dyadic variables added. dyad_data data.frame object containing dyadic variables add. Must include columns matching actor1, actor2, time specifications used original netify object. coerced data.frame tibble data.table provided. actor1 Character string specifying column name dyad_data first actor dyad. match actor1 specification used creating netify object. actor2 Character string specifying column name dyad_data second actor dyad. match actor2 specification used creating netify object. time Character string specifying column name dyad_data time periods. Required longitudinal netify objects. match time specification used creating netify object. Set NULL cross-sectional networks. dyad_vars Character vector column names dyad_data add dyadic variables. NULL (default), columns except actor1, actor2, time added. dyad_vars_symmetric Logical vector indicating whether dyadic variable represents symmetric relationships. Must length dyad_vars. NULL, defaults symmetry setting netify object, warning issued recommending explicit specification. replace_existing Logical scalar. TRUE, existing dyadic variables names replaced. FALSE (default), attempting add variables already exist result error.

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: netify object (class "netify") additional dyadic variables stored 'dyad_data' attribute. structure nested list : First level: named list time periods names ("1" cross-sectional data) Second level: named list variable names names Values: matrix objects actors rows/columns numeric, integer, logical, character values

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: Dyadic variables stored matrix objects rows represent first actor (sender directed networks) columns represent second actor (receiver directed networks). symmetric variables undirected networks, function ensures matrix[,j] equals matrix[j,]. function optimizes storage automatically detecting data type variable using appropriate matrix storage mode: logical vectors → logical matrices integer vectors → integer matrices numeric vectors integer values → integer matrices numeric vectors decimals → double matrices character vectors → character matrices longitudinal networks, function handles time-varying actor sets appropriately, creating matrices include actors present time point. Missing dyadic observations (NA values) input data.frame set missing resulting matrices well.

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: input dyad_data must data.frame object can coerced data.frame (e.g., tibble data.table). Inputs matrices arrays supported. adding dyadic variables bipartite networks, variables automatically treated asymmetric regardless dyad_vars_symmetric specification. large networks, consider memory implications adding many dyadic variables, variable requires full adjacency matrix storage.

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
text: Cassy Dorff, Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Add dyadic variables to a netify object — add_dyad_vars
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Cross-sectional example icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create initial netify object with just the main weight verbCoop_net <- netify(     icews_10, # data.frame input     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" ) #> ℹ `missing_to_zero` is set to "TRUE" (the default). #> ! Missing dyads will be filled with zeros. For latent space or other #>   statistical network models, structural zeros and missing data have different #>   meanings. Set `missing_to_zero = FALSE` to preserve NAs if this distinction #>   matters for your analysis. #> This message is displayed once per session.  # Check class class(verbCoop_net) # "netify" #> [1] "netify"  # Add additional dyadic variables verbCoop_net <- add_dyad_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_net, # netify object     dyad_data = icews_10, # data.frame with variables to add     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     dyad_vars = c("matlCoop", "verbConf", "matlConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = rep(FALSE, 3) )  # Access the dyadic data structure (returns list) dyad_data_structure <- attr(verbCoop_net, "dyad_data") class(dyad_data_structure) # "list" #> [1] "list" names(dyad_data_structure) # Time periods #> [1] "1" names(dyad_data_structure[["1"]]) # Variables at time 1 #> [1] "matlCoop" "verbConf" "matlConf"  # Access specific variable matrix matlCoop_matrix <- dyad_data_structure[["1"]][["matlCoop"]] class(matlCoop_matrix) # "matrix" "array" #> [1] "matrix" "array"  dim(matlCoop_matrix) #> [1] 152 152 matlCoop_matrix[1:5, 1:5] # View subset #>             Afghanistan Albania Algeria Angola Argentina #> Afghanistan           0       1       0      0         0 #> Albania               4       0       0      0         0 #> Algeria               0       0       0      2         0 #> Angola                0       0       0      0         0 #> Argentina             0       0       1      0         0  # Longitudinal example verbCoop_longit_net <- netify(     icews, # data.frame input     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Add dyadic variables across all time periods verbCoop_longit_net <- add_dyad_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_longit_net, # netify object     dyad_data = icews, # data.frame with longitudinal data     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     dyad_vars = c("matlCoop", "verbConf", "matlConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = rep(FALSE, 3) )  # Access data for specific year (returns list) year_2002_data <- attr(verbCoop_longit_net, "dyad_data")[["2002"]] class(year_2002_data) # "list" #> [1] "list" names(year_2002_data) # Available variables #> [1] "matlCoop" "verbConf" "matlConf"  # Each variable is stored as a matrix matlCoop_2002 <- year_2002_data[["matlCoop"]] class(matlCoop_2002) # "matrix" "array" #> [1] "matrix" "array"   # Example: Add variables from a different source # \donttest{ # Create a new data.frame with trade information trade_data <- data.frame(     i = icews_10$i,     j = icews_10$j,     trade_volume = runif(nrow(icews_10), 0, 1000),     trade_balance = rnorm(nrow(icews_10)) ) class(trade_data) # "data.frame" #> [1] "data.frame"  verbCoop_net <- add_dyad_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_net,     dyad_data = trade_data,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     dyad_vars = c("trade_volume", "trade_balance"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(FALSE, FALSE) ) # }

title: Add nodal variables to a netify object — add_node_vars
text: Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Add nodal variables to a netify object — add_node_vars
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Cross-sectional example icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create initial netify object verbCoop_net <- netify(     icews_10, # data.frame input     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Prepare nodal data - one row per unique actor nvars <- c("i_polity2", "i_gdp", "i_log_gdp", "i_pop", "i_log_pop") nodeData <- unique(icews_10[, c("i", nvars)]) class(nodeData) # "data.frame" #> [1] "data.frame" nrow(nodeData) # Number of unique actors #> [1] 152 head(nodeData) #>                 i i_polity2        i_gdp i_log_gdp    i_pop i_log_pop #> 10    Afghanistan        NA  16047892927  23.49884 28189672  17.15447 #> 2250      Albania         9  10420206418  23.06701  2913021  14.88470 #> 4490      Algeria         2 140977153156  25.67186 35856344  17.39503 #> 6730       Angola        -2  69938841426  24.97089 23364185  16.96671 #> 8970    Argentina         8 552738161802  27.03815 40788453  17.52391 #> 11210     Armenia         5   8513508876  22.86492  2946293  14.89606  # Add nodal variables verbCoop_net <- add_node_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_net, # netify object     node_data = nodeData, # data.frame with actor attributes     actor = "i", # column identifying actors     node_vars = nvars # variables to add )  # Access nodal data (returns data.frame) node_data_stored <- attr(verbCoop_net, "nodal_data") class(node_data_stored) # "data.frame" #> [1] "data.frame" head(node_data_stored) #>         actor i_polity2        i_gdp i_log_gdp    i_pop i_log_pop #> 1 Afghanistan        NA  16047892927  23.49884 28189672  17.15447 #> 2     Albania         9  10420206418  23.06701  2913021  14.88470 #> 3     Algeria         2 140977153156  25.67186 35856344  17.39503 #> 4      Angola        -2  69938841426  24.97089 23364185  16.96671 #> 5   Argentina         8 552738161802  27.03815 40788453  17.52391 #> 6     Armenia         5   8513508876  22.86492  2946293  14.89606 names(node_data_stored) # "actor" plus variable names #> [1] "actor"     "i_polity2" "i_gdp"     "i_log_gdp" "i_pop"     "i_log_pop"  # \donttest{ # Longitudinal example verbCoop_longit_net <- netify(     icews, # data.frame input     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Prepare longitudinal nodal data - one row per actor-time combination nodeData_longit <- unique(icews[, c("i", "year", nvars)])  # Add nodal variables with time dimension verbCoop_longit_net <- add_node_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_longit_net, # netify object     node_data = nodeData_longit, # data.frame with longitudinal data     actor = "i", # column identifying actors     time = "year", # column identifying time     node_vars = nvars # variables to add ) # }  # \donttest{ # Add variables from external source # Suppose you have additional actor data external_data <- data.frame(     i = unique(icews_10$i),     democracy_score = runif(length(unique(icews_10$i)), 0, 10),     trade_openness = runif(length(unique(icews_10$i)), 0, 100) )  verbCoop_net <- add_node_vars(     netlet = verbCoop_net,     node_data = external_data,     actor = "i",     node_vars = c("democracy_score", "trade_openness") ) # }

what: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: aggregate_dyad designed use dyadic event datasets ACLED ICEWS. datasets often contain multiple interactions pair actors—e.g., protest events, material cooperation, verbal conflict—recorded high frequency. function aggregates repeated observations single summary value per dyad, optionally within specified time periods. particularly useful preparing network inputs collapsing daily monthly event-level data actor--actor matrices.

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
code: aggregate_dyad(   dyad_data,   actor1,   actor2,   time = NULL,   weight,   symmetric,   ignore_missing = TRUE )

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: dyad_data data.frame containing dyadic observations. Must include columns two actors weight variable. coerced data.frame tibble data.table provided. actor1 Character string specifying column name first actor dyad. actor2 Character string specifying column name second actor dyad. time Character string specifying column name time periods. NULL (default), aggregation performed across time periods. weight Character string specifying column name containing values aggregated (summed) unique actor pair. symmetric Logical. TRUE, treats dyads undirected (.e., dyad -B treated identical B-). FALSE, treats dyads directed (.e., -B distinct B-). ignore_missing Logical. TRUE (default), missing values weight variable ignored aggregation. FALSE, dyad containing missing value result NA aggregated dyad.

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: data.frame unique actor pairs (time periods specified) aggregated weight values. output contains columns: actor1: First actor dyad (using original column name) actor2: Second actor dyad (using original column name) time: Time period time parameter specified (using original column name) weight: Aggregated (summed) values unique dyad (using original column name)

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: function handles directed undirected dyadic aggregation: symmetric (undirected) networks: function uses efficient aggregation method : Creates symmetric identifiers dyad using gen_symm_id (-B = B-) Aggregates values symmetric identifiers Expands results back directed format consistency netify functions ensures interactions actors B combined regardless direction, useful undirected relationships like friendships alliances. asymmetric (directed) networks: Standard aggregation performed treating directed dyad separately. maintains distinction →B B→relationships, important directed interactions like exports/imports sender/receiver communications. Missing value handling: ignore_missing parameter controls NA values treated: TRUE: Missing values excluded sum (e.g., sum(10, NA, 20) = 30) FALSE: missing value results NA dyad (e.g., sum(10, NA, 20) = NA)

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: function preserves original column names input data.frame output, making easy chain operations merge results. symmetric = TRUE, output still maintains directed format (separate rows -B B-) identical values directions. ensures compatibility netify functions expect directed dyadic data.

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
text: Shahryar Minhas

title: Aggregate dyadic event data by actor pairs — aggregate_dyad
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Example 1: Aggregate multiple events between countries # The icews data contains multiple events per country pair icews_2010 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Aggregate directed cooperation events agg_coop <- aggregate_dyad(     dyad_data = icews_2010,     actor1 = "i",     actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop",     symmetric = FALSE )  # Check reduction in observations nrow(icews_2010) # Original observations #> [1] 22952 nrow(agg_coop) # Unique directed dyads #> [1] 22952  # Example 2: Create symmetric trade volumes trade_data <- data.frame(     exporter = c("USA", "USA", "China", "China", "USA", "China"),     importer = c("China", "China", "USA", "USA", "UK", "UK"),     year = c(2020, 2020, 2020, 2021, 2021, 2021),     trade_value = c(100, 50, 75, 80, 120, 90) )  # Aggregate as total trade between countries (undirected) total_trade <- aggregate_dyad(     dyad_data = trade_data,     actor1 = "exporter",     actor2 = "importer",     time = "year",     weight = "trade_value",     symmetric = TRUE )  # USA-China trade in 2020: 100+50+75 = 225 (appears in both directions) total_trade[total_trade$year == 2020, ] #>   exporter importer year trade_value #> 2    China      USA 2020         225 #> 6      USA    China 2020         225  # Example 3: Aggregate across all time periods all_time_trade <- aggregate_dyad(     dyad_data = trade_data,     actor1 = "exporter",     actor2 = "importer",     time = NULL, # Aggregate across all years     weight = "trade_value",     symmetric = FALSE )  # USA total exports to China: 100+50 = 150 all_time_trade #>   exporter importer trade_value #> 1      USA    China         150 #> 2    China       UK          90 #> 3      USA       UK         120 #> 4    China      USA         155  # Example 4: Handle missing values trade_data_na <- trade_data trade_data_na$trade_value[2] <- NA  # Ignore missing values (default) agg_ignore_na <- aggregate_dyad(     dyad_data = trade_data_na,     actor1 = "exporter",     actor2 = "importer",     time = "year",     weight = "trade_value",     symmetric = FALSE,     ignore_missing = TRUE )  # Include missing values agg_with_na <- aggregate_dyad(     dyad_data = trade_data_na,     actor1 = "exporter",     actor2 = "importer",     time = "year",     weight = "trade_value",     symmetric = FALSE,     ignore_missing = FALSE )  # Compare results for USA->China in 2020 agg_ignore_na[agg_ignore_na$exporter == "USA" &     agg_ignore_na$importer == "China" &     agg_ignore_na$year == 2020, ] # 100 (ignored NA) #>   exporter importer year trade_value #> 1      USA    China 2020         100  agg_with_na[agg_with_na$exporter == "USA" &     agg_with_na$importer == "China" &     agg_with_na$year == 2020, ] # NA #>   exporter importer year trade_value #> 1      USA    China 2020          NA

what: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
text: Provides comprehensive analysis nodal dyadic attributes relate network structure. Combines multiple analytical approaches including homophily analysis, mixing patterns, dyadic correlations, network position-based attribute summaries.

title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
code: attribute_report(   netlet,   node_vars = NULL,   dyad_vars = NULL,   include_centrality = TRUE,   include_homophily = TRUE,   include_mixing = TRUE,   include_dyadic_correlations = TRUE,   centrality_measures = c("degree", "betweenness"),   categorical_threshold = 10,   significance_test = TRUE,   other_stats = NULL,   ... )

title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
text: netlet netify object containing network data. node_vars Character vector nodal attributes analyze. NULL, analyzes available nodal variables except actor time. dyad_vars Character vector dyadic attributes analyze. NULL, analyzes available dyadic variables. include_centrality Logical. Whether calculate attribute-centrality relationships. Default TRUE. include_homophily Logical. Whether perform homophily analysis. Default TRUE. include_mixing Logical. Whether create mixing matrices categorical attributes. Default TRUE. include_dyadic_correlations Logical. Whether calculate dyadic correlations. Default TRUE. centrality_measures Character vector centrality measures calculate. Options: "degree", "betweenness", "closeness", "eigenvector". Default c("degree", "betweenness"). categorical_threshold Maximum number unique values categorical treatment. Default 10. significance_test Logical. Whether perform significance tests. Default TRUE. other_stats Named list custom functions additional statistics. ... Additional arguments passed component functions.

title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
text: List containing: homophily_analysis Results homophily analysis nodal attributes mixing_analysis Results mixing matrix analysis categorical attributes dyadic_correlations Results dyadic correlation analysis centrality_correlations Correlations nodal attributes centrality attribute_summaries Descriptive statistics attributes overall_summary High-level summary key findings

title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
text: Serves comprehensive wrapper around exploratory analysis functions. Automatically determines appropriate analysis methods based attribute types. large networks many attributes, consider setting components FALSE faster computation. Centrality measures use igraph functions.

title: Comprehensive summary of network-attribute relationships — attribute_report
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
text: Thin wrapper around mutate_weights() common case "dichotomize weighted network cut-point." Returns new netify edge values coerced 0/1 based supplied threshold (threshold function).

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
code: binarize(netlet, threshold = 0, strict = FALSE, abs = FALSE, new_name = NULL)

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
text: netlet weighted netify object. threshold Numeric scalar (default 0 — nonzero edge becomes 1) function f(x) takes vector edge weights returns single numeric threshold (e.g., function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE) function(x) quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)). strict Logical. TRUE, edges weight strictly greater threshold become 1; FALSE (default), threshold included (>=). threshold = 0, default gives "nonzero edge counts" semantics matches rest package (signed-weight density, homophily-default-threshold, etc.). abs Logical. TRUE, compare |x| threshold negative-magnitude ties also count toward binarization. Defaults FALSE. network contains positive negative weights abs = FALSE, binarize() informs negative ties dropped. new_name Optional character. New name binarized weight column (default keeps original name).

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
text: binarized netify object is_binary = TRUE.

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
text: NA cells (e.g., diagonal diag_to_NA = TRUE) propagate NA output rather becoming 0. Use na.rm = TRUE summing edges want treated 0. Structural zeros stay zero every branch — negative threshold promote empty cells 1, regardless strict abs.

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Binarize a netify object at a threshold — binarize
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(icews) net <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2010, ], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") # any-nonzero-edge dichotomization (#' bin0 <- binarize(net) # 75th-percentile of nonzero weights bin75 <- binarize(net, threshold = function(x) { nz <- x[x > 0] quantile(nz, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE) }) } # }

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
text: bind_netifies() concatenates two netify objects along time axis (combining cross-sec → longit, stacking two longit panels longer one). inputs must share mode (unipartite / bipartite), symmetry, layers, (cross-sec inputs) actor set. Actor sets across periods may differ — result longit_list.

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
code: bind_netifies(   ...,   names = NULL,   align_actors = c("none", "union", "intersection") )

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
text: ... Two netify objects, single list netify objects. names Optional character vector name resulting periods. NULL, periods auto-named inputs' existing period labels (deduplication collisions). align_actors One "none" (default), "union", "intersection". Controls per-period actor sets reconciled inputs differ: "none": keep period's actor set supplied; resulting longit_list periods may different dimensions (matches prior behavior). "union": take union actor sets across inputs pad period NA rows/columns actors originally present. "intersection": take intersection actor sets across inputs subset period actors.

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
text: longit_list netify object.

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
text: combining different layers time slice, use layer_netify(). layer_netify(): bind_netifies() joins along TIME. layer_netify() joins along RELATION (layer). Nodal dyadic attributes concatenated per-period; two inputs supply conflicting values (actor, time), later input wins (one-shot inform). downstream models (e.g., tergm CMLE) require uniform actor composition across periods, use align_actors = "union" "intersection".

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Combine multiple netify objects — bind_netifies
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(icews) n1 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2010, ], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") n2 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2011, ], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") combined <- bind_netifies(n1, n2, names = c("2010", "2011")) summary(combined) } # }

what: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: Resamples actors replacement (Snijders & Borgatti 1999 vertex bootstrap) per panel, rebuilds netlet draw, applies user-supplied fn, returns per-draw values plus percentile confidence intervals.

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
code: bootstrap_netlet(   netlet,   fn,   n_boot = 200L,   alpha = 0.05,   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: netlet netify object. fn Function. Takes netlet, returns single numeric value named numeric vector. Called per bootstrap draw. n_boot Integer. Number bootstrap replicates (default 200). alpha Numeric (0, 1). Two-sided percentile-CI alpha (default 0.05 → 95% intervals). seed Optional integer. supplied, sets local RNG seed without leaking user's global stream. verbose Logical. TRUE (default), print progress ticker every 50 draws.

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: data.frame one row per element fn(netlet) columns: metric Name output element ("value" scalar fn). point Point estimate fn(netlet) original (un-resampled) netlet. mean Bootstrap mean. sd Bootstrap standard deviation. lower, upper Lower / upper percentile CI bounds. full per-draw matrix stashed attr(, "bootstrap_draws") callers want empirical distribution (e.g., kernel-density plots).

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: generalizes per-graph-stat bootstrap summary(net, bootstrap = TRUE) runs internally: scalar / named numeric vector summary netify can wrapped. Useful homophily / mixing-matrix / centrality stats expose bootstrap, downstream fit summaries (e.g., coefficient to_igraph -> igraph::cluster_* -> modularity pipeline). Resampling vertex-level, edge-level: actors sampled replacement netlet's adjacency sliced accordingly. longitudinal netlets, resampled index set applied every period (preserves within-actor dependence). Multilayer netlets unsupported — bootstrap layer independently via subset_netify(layers = ...) first.

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: bootstrap_netlet runs serially. n_boot > 50 netlet large enough draw non-trivial (rule thumb: N > ~1000 default centrality-heavy fn), parallelism helps. built-parallel = argument; instead drive loop future.apply::future_lapply(), respects whatever future::plan() caller set: summarize draws exactly bootstrap_netlet internally. Wrapping inner body bootstrap_netlet future-aware loop small refactor recommended path ~15K-node weekly snapshots serial pass tie single core hours.
code: library(future); library(future.apply) plan(multisession)        # or multicore on Linux/macOS draws <- future_lapply(1:n_boot, function(b) {   idx <- sample(actors, length(actors), replace = TRUE)   fn(subset_netify(net, actors = idx)) }, future.seed = TRUE)

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Bootstrap any user-supplied function of a netify object — bootstrap_netlet

what: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
text: Performs comprehensive comparison networks identify similarities differences. Supports comparison edge patterns, structural properties, node compositions across multiple networks within subgroups.

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
code: compare_networks(   nets,   method = "correlation",   by = NULL,   what = "edges",   test = TRUE,   n_permutations = 5000,   include_diagonal = FALSE,   return_details = FALSE,   edge_threshold = 0,   permutation_type = c("classic", "degree_preserving", "freedman_lane", "dsp_mrqap"),   correlation_type = c("pearson", "spearman"),   binary_metric = c("phi", "simple_matching", "mean_centered"),   seed = NULL,   p_adjust = c("none", "holm", "BH", "BY"),   adaptive_stop = FALSE,   alpha = 0.05,   max_permutations = 20000,   spectral_rank = 0,   attr_metric = c("ecdf_cor", "wasserstein"),   other_stats = NULL )

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
text: nets Either list netify objects compare, single netify object (longitudinal, multilayer, -group comparisons). method Character string specifying comparison method: "correlation" Pearson correlation edge weights (default) "jaccard" Jaccard similarity binary networks "hamming" Hamming distance (proportion differing edges) "qap" Quadratic Assignment Procedure permutation test "spectral" Spectral distance based eigenvalue spectra. Measures global structural differences comparing sorted eigenvalues network Laplacian matrices. Useful detecting fundamental structural changes. "" Applies applicable methods Character vector nodal attributes. specified, compares networks within attribute groups rather across input networks. (Note: Currently fully implemented) Character string specifying compare: "edges" Edge-level comparison (default) "structure" Structural properties (density, clustering, etc.) "nodes" Node composition attributes "attributes" Compare networks based node attribute distributions test Logical. Whether perform significance testing. Default TRUE. n_permutations Integer. Number permutations QAP test. Default 5000. include_diagonal Logical. Whether include diagonal comparison. Default FALSE. return_details Logical. Whether return detailed comparison matrices. Default FALSE. edge_threshold Numeric function. weighted networks, threshold determine edge presence. Default 0 (positive weight). permutation_type Character string specifying permutation scheme: "classic" Standard label permutation QAP (default) "degree_preserving" Preserves degree sequence (binary networks ) "freedman_lane" Freeman-Lane MRQAP controlling autocorrelation "dsp_mrqap" Double-Semi-Partialling MRQAP correlation_type Character string. "pearson" (default) "spearman" rank-based correlation. binary_metric Character string binary network correlation: "phi" Standard phi coefficient (default) "simple_matching" Proportion matching edges "mean_centered" Mean-centered phi coefficient seed Integer. Random seed reproducible permutations. Default NULL. p_adjust Character string multiple testing correction: "none" (default), "holm", "BH" (Benjamini-Hochberg), "". adaptive_stop Logical. Whether use adaptive stopping permutations. Default FALSE. (Currently implemented) alpha Numeric. Significance level adaptive stopping. Default 0.05. max_permutations Integer. Maximum permutations adaptive stopping. Default 20000. spectral_rank Integer. Number eigenvalues use spectral distance. Default 0 (use ). Set smaller value (e.g., 50-100) large networks improve performance maintaining accuracy. attr_metric Character string continuous attribute comparison: "ecdf_cor" Correlation empirical CDFs (default) "wasserstein" Wasserstein-1 (Earth Mover's) distance other_stats Named list custom functions calculate additional comparison statistics. function accept netify object (matrix edge comparisons) return named vector scalar values. specific input depends parameter: = "edges" Functions receive adjacency matrices = "structure" Functions receive netify objects = "nodes" Functions receive netify objects = "attributes" Functions receive netify objects Example: list(connectivity = function(net) { g <- to_igraph(net);   c(vertex_conn = igraph::vertex_connectivity(g)) })

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
text: list class "netify_comparison" containing: comparison_type Character string: "cross_network", "temporal", "multilayer", "by_group" method Comparison method(s) used n_networks Number networks compared summary Data frame comparison statistics edge_changes List detailing added, removed, maintained edges (edge comparisons) node_changes List detailing added, removed, maintained nodes (node comparisons) significance_tests QAP test results test = TRUE details Detailed comparison matrices return_details = TRUE comparisons Long-format data frame = "edges" one row per (network pair, metric) triple. Columns: net_i, net_j (two network names), metric (e.g. "correlation", "jaccard", "hamming", "qap_correlation", "spectral", "weight_correlation"), value (scalar metric), p_value (populated qap_correlation; NA otherwise). Coerce tibble tibble::as_tibble(comp) (as_tibble S3 method returns frame directly).

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
text: function supports four types comparisons: Edge comparison (= "edges"): Compares edge patterns networks using correlation, Jaccard similarity, Hamming distance, spectral distance, QAP permutation tests. Returns detailed edge changes showing edges added, removed, maintained networks. Structure comparison (= "structure"): Compares network-level properties like density, reciprocity, transitivity, mean degree. two networks, also provides percent change calculations. Node comparison (= "nodes"): Analyzes changes actor composition networks, tracking actors enter exit network. Attribute comparison (= "attributes"): Compares distributions node attributes across networks using appropriate statistical tests (KS test continuous, total variation distance categorical). Automatic handling longitudinal data: passed single longitudinal netify object, function automatically extracts time periods performs pairwise comparisons . Automatic handling multilayer networks: passed single multilayer netify object (created layer_netify()), function automatically extracts layers performs pairwise comparisons . works cross-sectional multilayer (3D arrays), longitudinal multilayer (4D arrays), longitudinal list multilayer formats. Summary statistics interpretation: Correlation: Ranges -1 1, measuring linear relationship edge weights Jaccard: Ranges 0 1, proportion edges present networks Hamming: Ranges 0 1, proportion differing edges QAP p-value: Significance observed correlation random permutation Permutation methods: permutation_type = "degree_preserving", first network must binary (0/1). method preserves degree sequence permutations, important degree heterogeneity inflate Type error rates.

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Compare networks across time, layers, or attributes — compare_networks
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create networks for different years net_2002 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2002, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlConf" ) net_2003 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2003, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlConf" )  # Basic edge comparison comp <- compare_networks(list("2002" = net_2002, "2003" = net_2003)) print(comp) #>  #> ── Network Comparison Results ────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Comparison type: cross_network #> Number of networks: 2 #> Comparison focus: edges #> Method: correlation #> Algorithm: correlation #> Permutation type: classic #> Correlation type: pearson #> Random seed: 551581484 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Edge Comparison Summary ── #>  #>     comparison correlation #> 1 2002 vs 2003       0.943 #> ── Edge Changes  #> 2002_vs_2003: #> Added: 1366 | Removed: 1198 | Maintained: 1866  # Structural comparison struct_comp <- compare_networks(     list(net_2002, net_2003),     what = "structure" )  # \donttest{ # Create longitudinal network for automatic temporal comparison longit_net <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     weight = "verbCoop",     output_format = "longit_list" )  # Automatic temporal comparison temporal_comp <- compare_networks(longit_net, method = "all")  # Create multilayer network example verbal_coop <- netify(     icews[icews$year == 2010, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop" )  material_coop <- netify(     icews[icews$year == 2010, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "matlCoop" )  # Combine into multilayer network multilayer <- layer_netify(     list(verbal = verbal_coop, material = material_coop) )  # Automatic multilayer comparison layer_comp <- compare_networks(multilayer, method = "all") print(layer_comp) #>  #> ── Network Comparison Results ────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Comparison type: multilayer #> Number of networks: 2 #> Comparison focus: edges #> Method: all #> Algorithm: all #> Permutation type: classic #> Correlation type: pearson #> Random seed: 1040204134 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Edge Comparison Summary ── #>  #>           comparison correlation jaccard hamming qap_correlation qap_pvalue #> 1 verbal vs material       0.609   0.264   0.351           0.609          0 #>   spectral #> 1  8.4e+04 #> ── Edge Changes  #> verbal_vs_material: #> Added: 188 | Removed: 7930 | Maintained: 2912 #>  #> ── Statistical Tests ── #>  #> Permutations: 5000 #>  #> ── qap_correlations  #>             verbal  material #> verbal   1.0000000 0.6090541 #> material 0.6090541 1.0000000 #>  #> ── qap_pvalues  #>          verbal material #> verbal        0        0 #> material      0        0 #> attr(,"n_perm") #> [1] 5000  # Get detailed matrices detailed_comp <- compare_networks(     list(net_2002, net_2003),     return_details = TRUE ) names(detailed_comp$details) # Shows available matrices #> [1] "correlation_matrix" "jaccard_matrix"     "hamming_matrix"     #> [4] "spectral_matrix"    # }  # Compare with custom statistics if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(igraph)  # Define custom connectivity function connectivity_stats <- function(net) {     g <- to_igraph(net)     c(vertex_connectivity = vertex_connectivity(g),       edge_connectivity = edge_connectivity(g),       diameter = diameter(g, directed = FALSE)) }  # Apply to structural comparison struct_comp_custom <- compare_networks(     list("2002" = net_2002, "2003" = net_2003),     what = "structure",     other_stats = list(connectivity = connectivity_stats) )  # Custom stats will appear in the summary print(struct_comp_custom$summary) } # }

what: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: Simulates n_sim networks null model (default Erdős-Rényi observed density), applies user-supplied statistic , reports observed statistic compares — point estimate, percentile null distribution, two-sided permutation p-value.

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
code: compare_to_null(   netlet,   fn,   n_sim = 200L,   model = c("erdos_renyi", "configuration", "dyad_permutation"),   alpha = 0.05,   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: netlet observed netify object. fn Function. Takes netify object (igraph matrix) returns single numeric value named numeric vector. Called observed netlet n_sim times simulated draws. Wrap igraph helpers to_igraph() inside fn, e.g. fn = function(net) igraph::transitivity(to_igraph(net)). n_sim Integer. Number null-model draws (default 200). model Character. Null-model family; passed simulate.netify(). One "erdos_renyi" (default), "configuration", "dyad_permutation". Picking null model. ERGM-trained users: erdos_renyi Random graph observed density. Use null hypothesis "structure beyond density." configuration Random graph preserving observed degree sequence. Use want ask "observed structure surprising given degree distribution?" — already conditions degree. dyad_permutation Vertex-label permutation. Preserves full degree distribution weight values; use comparing attribute-aware structure (homophily, mixing) actor relabelings. weighted netlets, simulator draws weights empirical non-zero weight distribution observed-vs-null stats computed comparable scales (dyad_permutation exact — preserves weights via relabel). alpha Numeric (0, 1). Two-sided alpha percentile CI null distribution (default 0.05 → 95%). seed Optional integer. Local RNG seed (leak user's global stream). verbose Logical. Progress ticker every 50 draws.

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: data.frame (also class "netify_null_test") one row per stat columns: metric Name statistic. observed Observed value netlet. null_mean, null_sd Moments null distribution. null_lower, null_upper Percentile CI bounds. p_value Two-sided permutation p-value. extreme Logical: observed outside [null_lower, null_upper]?

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: Combines simulate.netify() + vectorized fn loop single-call entry users actually want: "observed transitivity surprising vs. random graph?"

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: compare_to_null runs serially. n_sim > 50 large N, per-draw fn() cost (igraph community detection, full summary_actor) dominates wall-clock. built-parallel = argument; drive loop manually future.apply::future_lapply(): Summarize null_draws compare_to_null internally (rowMeans, quantiles, two-sided p). 15K-node weekly snapshots n_sim = 500, 4-core multisession plan cuts wall-clock roughly 3.5x.
code: library(future); library(future.apply) plan(multisession) sims <- simulate(net, nsim = n_sim, model = "erdos_renyi", seed = 1) null_draws <- future_lapply(sims, fn, future.seed = TRUE)

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Test an observed network statistic against a null distribution — compare_to_null
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(icews) net <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2010, ], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") my_stat <- function(net) { s = suppressMessages(summary(net)) c(transitivity = s$transitivity, reciprocity = s$reciprocity) } compare_to_null(net, my_stat, n_sim = 200, seed = 1) } # }

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
code: decompose_igraph(grph, weight = NULL)

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
text: grph igraph object decomposed. weight Character string specifying edge attribute use weights adjacency matrix. NULL (default), unweighted adjacency matrix returned 1s edges 0s non-edges.

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
text: list containing four elements: adj_mat: adjacency matrix extracted igraph object unipartite graphs: Square matrix dimension n×n bipartite graphs: Rectangular matrix dimension n₁×n₂ Values edge weights specified, otherwise 0/1 ndata: data frame vertex attributes, NULL none exist Always includes 'actor' column vertex names Additional columns vertex attribute ddata: data frame edge attributes, NULL none exist Columns '' '' specify edge endpoints Additional columns edge attribute weight: edge attribute name used weights, provided

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
text: function handles unipartite bipartite graphs appropriately: Graph type detection: Bipartite graphs must logical 'type' vertex attribute 'type' attribute exists logical, graph treated unipartite warning Vertex naming: graph lacks vertex names, default names assigned: Unipartite graphs: "a1", "a2", ..., "" Bipartite graphs: "r1", "r2", ... type 1; "c1", "c2", ... type 2 Existing vertex names always preserved used output. Matrix extraction: Unipartite: Uses as_adjacency_matrix() get n×n matrix Bipartite: Uses as_biadjacency_matrix() get n₁×n₂ matrix rows correspond type=FALSE vertices columns type=TRUE vertices Attribute handling: vertex edge attributes preserved output data frames. System attributes (like 'name' 'type') included alongside user-defined attributes.

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
text: longitudinal networks changing actor composition, explicitly set vertex names decomposition ensure consistent actor identification across time periods. adjacency matrix always returned standard R matrix (sparse), may memory implications large graphs. edge attributes used weights, ensure contain numeric values. Non-numeric edge attributes cause error.

title: Decompose an igraph object into base R components — decompose_igraph
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas
