title: Decompose a netify object into edge and node data frames — decompose_netify
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") decomposed. remove_zeros Logical. TRUE (default), edges zero weight values removed edge data frame. FALSE, zero-weight edges retained.

title: Decompose a netify object into edge and node data frames — decompose_netify
text: list containing two data frames: edge_data: data frame row represents edge columns: : Source node identifier : Target node identifier time: Time period (character; "1" cross-sectional networks) weight: Edge weight values (using original weight variable name specified) Additional columns dyadic variables stored netify object nodal_data: data frame row represents node-time combination columns: name: Node identifier time: Time period (character; "1" cross-sectional networks) Additional columns nodal variables stored netify object

title: Decompose a netify object into edge and node data frames — decompose_netify
text: function helpful : Edge data processing: Extracts adjacency matrix (array longitudinal networks) netify object Optionally removes zero-weight edges based remove_zeros parameter Merges dyadic variables stored netify object Renames columns standardized names (, , time) Node data processing: Extracts nodal attributes present, constructs actor_pds information Ensures consistent time variable across node edge data Renames columns standardized names (name, time) Time handling: cross-sectional networks: Sets time "1" data frames longitudinal networks: Preserves original time periods character values ego networks: Extracts time ego-time concatenated identifiers Variable preservation: dyadic nodal variables stored netify object preserved output data frames. Dyadic variables merged edge data, nodal variables remain nodal data frame.

title: Decompose a netify object into edge and node data frames — decompose_netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Decompose a netify object into edge and node data frames — decompose_netify
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Example 1: Cross-sectional network icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create netify object net_cs <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Decompose to data frames decomposed_cs <- decompose_netify(net_cs)  # Examine structure str(decomposed_cs) #> List of 2 #>  $ edge_data :'data.frame':	9976 obs. of  4 variables: #>   ..$ from    : chr [1:9976] "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" "Afghanistan" ... #>   ..$ to      : chr [1:9976] "Argentina" "Armenia" "Australia" "Austria" ... #>   ..$ time    : chr [1:9976] "1" "1" "1" "1" ... #>   ..$ verbCoop: num [1:9976] 1 7 125 1 7 3 14 3 2 16 ... #>  $ nodal_data:'data.frame':	152 obs. of  2 variables: #>   ..$ name: chr [1:152] "Afghanistan" "Albania" "Algeria" "Angola" ... #>   ..$ time: chr [1:152] "1" "1" "1" "1" ... head(decomposed_cs$edge_data) #>          from         to time verbCoop #> 1 Afghanistan  Argentina    1        1 #> 2 Afghanistan    Armenia    1        7 #> 3 Afghanistan  Australia    1      125 #> 4 Afghanistan    Austria    1        1 #> 5 Afghanistan Azerbaijan    1        7 #> 6 Afghanistan    Bahrain    1        3 head(decomposed_cs$nodal_data) #>          name time #> 1 Afghanistan    1 #> 2     Albania    1 #> 3     Algeria    1 #> 4      Angola    1 #> 5   Argentina    1 #> 6     Armenia    1  # Example 2: Keep zero-weight edges decomposed_with_zeros <- decompose_netify(net_cs, remove_zeros = FALSE)  # Compare edge counts nrow(decomposed_cs$edge_data) # Without zeros #> [1] 9976 nrow(decomposed_with_zeros$edge_data) # With zeros #> [1] 22952  # Example 4: Use for visualization prep if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Decompose for use with ggplot2 plot_data <- decompose_netify(net_cs)  # Can now use edge_data and nodal_data separately # for network visualization } # }

title: Decompose a network object into base R components — decompose_statnet
text: list containing four elements: adj_mat: adjacency matrix extracted network object unipartite networks: Square matrix dimension n×n bipartite networks: Full square matrix dimension (n₁+n₂)×(n₁+n₂) Values edge weights specified, otherwise 0/1 ndata: data frame vertex attributes, NULL none exist Always includes 'actor' column vertex names Additional columns vertex attribute (excluding system attributes) ddata: data frame edge attributes, NULL none exist Columns '' '' specify edge endpoints using vertex names Additional columns edge attribute weight: edge attribute name used weights, provided

title: Decompose a network object into base R components — decompose_statnet
text: function handles unipartite bipartite networks appropriately: Network type detection: Bipartite networks identified using .bipartite() bipartite partition size retrieved 'bipartite' network attribute Vertex naming: function checks existing vertex names 'vertex.names' attribute. names just default numeric sequence (1, 2, 3, ...), treated missing. Default names assigned needed: Unipartite networks: "a1", "a2", ..., "" Bipartite networks: "r1", "r2", ... first partition; "c1", "c2", ... second partition Matrix extraction: Unlike igraph's bipartite handling, network package returns full adjacency matrix even bipartite networks. function: Extracts full matrix using .matrix.network.adjacency() bipartite networks, matrix dimension (n₁+n₂)×(n₁+n₂) first n₁ rows/columns first partition Attribute handling: System attributes ('vertex.names' 'na') excluded vertex attribute data frame. user-defined vertex edge attributes preserved.

text: longitudinal networks changing actor composition, explicitly set vertex names decomposition ensure consistent actor identification across time periods. adjacency matrix format differs function decompose_igraph bipartite networks: function returns full square matrix decompose_igraph returns rectangular bipartite portion. Edge directions preserved adjacency matrix according network's directed/undirected property.

what: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
text: Removes actors whose nodal_data carries NA one covariate columns. ERGM terms like nodecov() nodematch() reject NA-bearing vertex attributes, helper handy upstream netify_to_statnet. Works cross-sectional, longitudinal, bipartite netlets.

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
code: drop_na_actors(netlet, cols = NULL)

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
text: netlet netify object nodal_data attribute. cols Character vector column names nodal_data check NA. NULL (default) checks every non-bookkeeping column (everything except actor, time, layer).

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
text: netify object equivalent subset_netify(netlet, actors = clean_actors) dropping actor whose nodal rows contain NA inspected columns. NAs found input returned unchanged.

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
text: longitudinal netlets actor dropped every period rows nodal_data carry NA inspected columns. bipartite netlets actors mode nodal table covers filtered; mode passes untouched. Corner cases: cols references name nodal_data, call aborts clear message listing missing columns. actor carries NA inspected columns, input netlet returned unchanged (inform). every actor carries NA (cleaned netlet zero actors), call aborts rather silently returning empty netify, break downstream to_statnet() / ergm() pipelines. netlet nodal_data attribute attached, input returned unchanged.

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
text: Shahryar Minhas

title: Drop actors with NA covariates from a netify object — drop_na_actors
code: # \donttest{ data(icews) net <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop") ) clean <- drop_na_actors(net, cols = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp")) #> ℹ Dropped 8 of 152 actors with NA covariates: "Afghanistan", "Bosnia And #>   Herzegovina", "Djibouti", "Iraq", "Korea, Democratic People's Republic Of", #>   "Lebanon", "Solomon Islands", and "Somalia". #> This message is displayed once per session. # }

what: Analyze correlations between dyadic attributes and network ties — dyad_correlation
title: Analyze correlations between dyadic attributes and network ties — dyad_correlation

what: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: ego_netify extracts ego network netify object. ego network consists focal node (ego) immediate neighbors (alters). weighted networks, users can define neighborhoods using edge weight thresholds. function returns netify object representing ego network.

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
code: ego_netify(   netlet,   ego,   threshold = NULL,   ngbd_direction = "any",   include_ego = TRUE )

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") extract ego network. ego Character string specifying name ego create ego network. Must match actor name netify object. threshold Numeric value vector specifying threshold including alters ego network based edge weights. longitudinal networks, can vector length equal number time periods apply different thresholds time. NULL (default), uses 0 unweighted networks mean edge weight weighted networks. ngbd_direction Character string specifying neighbors include directed networks. Options : "": Include alters ego outgoing ties "": Include alters ego incoming ties "": Include alters tie /ego (default) include_ego Logical. TRUE (default), ego node included ego network. FALSE, alters included.

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: netify object representing ego network. longitudinal networks, returns list netify objects one ego network per time period. returned netify object includes additional attributes: ego_netify: TRUE (indicator ego network) ego_id: Identifier ego threshold: Threshold value(s) used ngbd_direction: Direction specification used include_ego: Whether ego included

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: function extracts ego network identifying nodes connected specified ego based given criteria: Neighborhood definition: unweighted networks: nodes edges /ego (threshold = 0) weighted networks: nodes edge weights exceeding threshold Direction matters directed networks (controlled ngbd_direction) Threshold behavior: specified, defaults 0 unweighted networks specified weighted networks, uses mean edge weight longitudinal networks, can vary time period vector provided Edges weights > threshold included (≥) Output structure: function preserves attributes original netify object, including nodal dyadic variables, subsets include ego neighbors. longitudinal networks, ego networks may vary composition across time periods relationships change. Limitations: Currently support multilayer networks Currently support bipartite networks

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: create ego networks multiple egos, use lapply loop call function ego separately.

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Create ego network from a netify object — ego_netify
code: # cross-sectional ego network from the bundled classroom data data(classroom_edges) data(classroom_nodes) net <- netify(     classroom_edges,     actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to",     symmetric = TRUE,     nodal_data = classroom_nodes ) s07_ego <- ego_netify(net, ego = "s07") print(s07_ego) #> ✔ Hello, you have created a neighborhood network for ego(s) (s07), yay! #> • Type: Ego Network #> • Ego: s07 #> • Direction: Any ties (in or out) #> • Ego included: Yes #> • Unipartite #> • Symmetric #> • Binary Weights #> • Cross-Sectional #> • # Unique Egos: 1 | # Unique Alters: 5 #> Neighborhood Network Summary Statistics: #>          dens miss trans #> weight1 0.333    0     0 #> • Nodal Features: gender, grade, gpa #> • Dyad Features: None  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # longitudinal ego network with a weighted, directed netlet data(icews) netlet <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     weight = "verbCoop" ) pakistan_ego <- ego_netify(netlet, ego = "Pakistan") summary(pakistan_ego) } # }

what: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
text: Takes posterior-mean (point-estimate) prediction matrix lame::ame() / lame::ame_als() / amen::ame() fit wraps netify predictions can summarized, plotted, compared observed netlet via compare_networks().

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
code: from_lame_fit(   fit,   value = c("fitted", "residual", "prob", "prob_lower", "prob_upper", "fitted_lower",     "fitted_upper"),   symmetric = NULL,   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
text: fit fitted object lame::ame(), lame::ame_als(), lame::lame(), amen::ame(). Must expose fitted-value matrix (e.g., fit$EZ, fit$ZpostMean, fitted(fit)). value "prob_lower" / "prob_upper" / "fitted_lower" / "fitted_upper" fit must additionally expose per-draw array fitted values. slots searched, order, : fit$BOOT$EZ fit$BOOT$Y_hat (lame ALS parametric / block bootstrap), fit$EZ_draws, fit$EZps, fit$Z_draws (Gibbs posterior draws), shape [n, n, B] [n, n, T, B]. value One "fitted" (default — posterior-mean linear predictor EZ/ZpostMean), "residual" (observed - fitted), "prob" (logistic / probit -> probability scale, family supports ), "prob_lower" / "prob_upper" / "fitted_lower" / "fitted_upper" (per-cell alpha/1-alpha quantiles across bootstrap posterior draws). lame exposes fitted() method object, preferred. value = "prob" binary family, link detection follows priority: (1) explicit link slot fit (fit$control$link); (2) ALS class (token containing "als") fit$fit_method = "als" -> probit, matching lame::ame_als(); (3) lame class -> logit, matching lame::lame() Gibbs default; (4) ame / amen class (fit_method = "logit" override) -> probit, matching amen::ame() Gibbs convention; (5) otherwise logit fallback. symmetric Logical. Override inferred symmetry; default reads fit's stored mode/Y. alpha Numeric (0, 0.5). Tail probability *_lower / *_upper quantiles. Default 0.05 -> 90% interval (lower = 0.025, upper = 0.975 interpreted two-sided CI; use lower = alpha/2, upper = 1 - alpha/2).

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
text: cross-sectional netify object whose underlying matrix fitted-value (residual) matrix actor ordering.

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
text: Useful posterior-predictive checks: build observed netlet, run AME fit, from_lame_fit(fit) |> plot(style = "heatmap") visualize fitted intensity matrix.

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert a fitted lame/amen AME object back into a netify — from_lame_fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lm_in <- to_lame(net, fit_method = "als", bootstrap = 200) fit <- lame::ame_als(Y = lm_in$Y, mode = "bipartite", family = "binary", bootstrap = 200) # round-trip predictions back into a netify for plotting pred_net <- from_lame_fit(fit, value = "prob") plot(pred_net, style = "heatmap") } # }

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
code: gen_symm_id(dyad_data, actor1, actor2, time = NULL)

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
text: dyad_data data.frame containing dyadic data least two columns representing actors dyad. coerced data.frame tibble data.table provided. actor1 Character string specifying column name first actor dyad. actor2 Character string specifying column name second actor dyad. time Character string specifying column name time periods. provided, time value appended symmetric ID create unique identifiers time period. Set NULL (default) cross-sectional data.

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
text: character vector symmetric identifiers length number rows dyad_data. ID formatted : Without time: "actor1_actor2" (alphabetically sorted) time: "actor1_actor2_time" (actors alphabetically sorted)

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
text: function ensures symmetry alphabetically sorting actor names creating identifier. guarantees : dyad "USA-China" receives ID "China-USA" dyad "Brazil-Argentina" receives ID "Argentina-Brazil" Actor pairs consistently ordered regardless input order time column specified, appended symmetric ID maintain unique identifiers across different time periods. allows proper aggregation longitudinal dyadic data preserving temporal variation.

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
text: actor values converted character strings creating IDs ensure consistent sorting behavior across different data types. underscore character ("_") used separator IDs. actor names contain underscores, IDs still unique may harder parse visually. function primarily used internally aggregate_dyad efficient symmetric aggregation, can used independently creating symmetric dyad identifiers.

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
text: Shahryar Minhas

title: Generate symmetric identifiers for dyadic data — gen_symm_id
code: # Create example dyadic data trade_df <- data.frame(     from = c("USA", "China", "Russia", "USA", "Brazil", "Argentina"),     to = c("China", "USA", "USA", "Russia", "Argentina", "Brazil"),     trade_value = c(100, 100, 50, 75, 30, 25),     year = c(2020, 2020, 2021, 2021, 2021, 2021) )  # Generate symmetric IDs without time trade_df$symm_id <- gen_symm_id(trade_df, "from", "to") print(trade_df[, c("from", "to", "symm_id")]) #>        from        to          symm_id #> 1       USA     China        China_USA #> 2     China       USA        China_USA #> 3    Russia       USA       Russia_USA #> 4       USA    Russia       Russia_USA #> 5    Brazil Argentina Argentina_Brazil #> 6 Argentina    Brazil Argentina_Brazil # Note: USA-China and China-USA both get "China_USA"  # Generate symmetric IDs with time trade_df$symm_id_time <- gen_symm_id(trade_df, "from", "to", "year") print(trade_df[, c("from", "to", "year", "symm_id_time")]) #>        from        to year          symm_id_time #> 1       USA     China 2020        China_USA_2020 #> 2     China       USA 2020        China_USA_2020 #> 3    Russia       USA 2021       Russia_USA_2021 #> 4       USA    Russia 2021       Russia_USA_2021 #> 5    Brazil Argentina 2021 Argentina_Brazil_2021 #> 6 Argentina    Brazil 2021 Argentina_Brazil_2021 # Note: USA-China in 2020 gets "China_USA_2020"  # Use for aggregation of undirected relationships trade_df$total_trade <- ave(     trade_df$trade_value,     trade_df$symm_id_time,     FUN = sum ) print(unique(trade_df[, c("symm_id_time", "total_trade")])) #>            symm_id_time total_trade #> 1        China_USA_2020         200 #> 3       Russia_USA_2021         125 #> 5 Argentina_Brazil_2021          55  # Example with longitudinal data library(netify) data(icews) icews_sample <- icews[1:100, ]  # Create symmetric IDs for conflict events icews_sample$symm_dyad <- gen_symm_id(     icews_sample,     actor1 = "i",     actor2 = "j",     time = "year" )  # Check that symmetric pairs get same ID icews_sample[icews_sample$i == "United States" & icews_sample$j == "Israel", "symm_dyad"] #> character(0) icews_sample[icews_sample$i == "Israel" & icews_sample$j == "United States", "symm_dyad"] #> character(0)

what: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: get_actor_time_info returns per-actor data.frame entry exit times. dispatches first argument:

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
code: get_actor_time_info(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netify' get_actor_time_info(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' get_actor_time_info(x, actor1, actor2, time, ...)  # Default S3 method get_actor_time_info(x, actor1, actor2, time, ...)

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: x netify object, data.frame dyadic observations. ... Unused; reserved future methods. actor1 Character string specifying column name first actor dyad (data.frame method ). actor2 Character string specifying column name second actor dyad (data.frame method ). time Character string specifying column name time periods (data.frame method ).

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: data.frame three columns: actor: Character vector unique actor identifiers. min_time: Earliest time period actor network (entry point). max_time: Latest time period actor network (exit point). netify method, verbatim copy attr(x, "actor_pds"). data.frame method, actors ordered appear aggregation, alphabetically time.

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: x netify object, returns stored actor_pds attribute directly (one row per actor min_time / max_time). open-cohort roster netlet built — roster every per-period statistic (density, degree, homophily) computed . x data.frame dyadic observations, computes entry / exit times data. Entry defined first time period actor appears interaction (either sender receiver), exit last time period. Use form prepare actor_pds argument netify(). Use cases: dyad data.frame: build actor_pds argument netify(..., actor_time_uniform = FALSE, actor_pds = ...) open-cohort panels (panel surveys attrition, contact-tracing chains, organizational membership time, etc.). netify object: inspect entry / exit roster netlet currently using — useful debugging density denominators, writing custom exporters, verifying open-cohort netlet actor windows expect. Assumptions (data.frame method): actor considered "present" time period appear data, regardless role (sender/receiver). Missing values time ignored calculating min/max. Actors must appear least one non-missing time period.

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: data.frame method assumes presence data indicates network participation. actors can temporarily absent network still considered members, method capture gaps — supply explicit actor_pds roster netify() instead.

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
text: Shahryar Minhas, Ha Eun Choi Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Extract actor time range information — get_actor_time_info
code: # data.frame input: derive the roster df <- data.frame(     i = c("a", "a", "b", "c"),     j = c("b", "c", "c", "a"),     t = c(1, 2, 2, 3) ) get_actor_time_info(df, "i", "j", "t") #>   actor min_time max_time #> 1     a        1        3 #> 2     b        1        2 #> 3     c        2        3  # netify input: read back the stored roster if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ roster <- data.frame(actor = c("a", "b"), min_time = c(1, 1), max_time = c(3, 4)) net <- netify(df, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "t",               actor_time_uniform = FALSE, actor_pds = roster) get_actor_time_info(net) } # }

title: Create a netify list from longitudinal dyadic data — get_adjacency_list
text: Cassy Dorff, Ha Eun Choi, Shahryar Minhas

title: Create a netify list from longitudinal dyadic data — get_adjacency_list
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create a netify list with constant actor composition icews_list <- get_adjacency_list(     dyad_data = icews,     actor1 = "i",     actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     actor_time_uniform = TRUE,     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbConf" )  # Verify it's a netify object class(icews_list) # "netify" #> [1] "netify"  # Check structure length(icews_list) # Number of time periods #> [1] 13 names(icews_list) # Time period labels #>  [1] "2002" "2003" "2004" "2005" "2006" "2007" "2008" "2009" "2010" "2011" #> [11] "2012" "2013" "2014"  # Access specific time period icews_2010 <- icews_list[["2010"]] dim(icews_2010) #> [1] 152 152

what: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
code: get_edge_layout(netlet, nodes_layout, ig_netlet = NULL)

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") containing network structure edges extracted. nodes_layout data.frame matrix containing node positions, list objects longitudinal networks. element must include columns: actor: Character string identifying node x: Numeric x-coordinate node position y: Numeric y-coordinate node position longitudinal networks, provide named list : Names correspond time periods netify object element follows structure described Time period names must match netify object ig_netlet optional pre-converted igraph object. provided, function use directly instead converting netify object .

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
text: Depending input netify object: Cross-sectional: list containing one data.frame columns: : Source node name : Target node name x1, y1: Coordinates source node x2, y2: Coordinates target node Longitudinal: named list data.frames (one per time period) structure output maintains temporal structure input netify object.

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
text: function performs following operations: Edge extraction: Converts netify object igraph format internally Extracts edge list preserving edge directions Handles cross-sectional longitudinal networks Coordinate mapping: Matches edge endpoint corresponding node position Creates complete set coordinates drawing edges Preserves temporal structure longitudinal networks Use visualization: function typically used part visualization pipeline: Create node layout using get_node_layout() custom layout algorithm Generate edge coordinates using function Pass visualization functions plotting

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
text: nodes_layout structure must exactly match actors time periods netify object. Missing actors layout result NA coordinates associated edges. longitudinal networks, ensure names nodes_layout list match time period labels netify object (e.g., "2008", "2009"). function always returns list structure consistency, even cross-sectional networks list contains one element.

title: Generate edge layout coordinates for netify visualization — get_edge_layout
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: get_ego_layout computes node positions ego network visualization using ego-centric layout algorithms. layouts place ego center arrange alters around (radially, concentric ring, etc.) ego's relationships organized around focal node.

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
code: get_ego_layout(   netlet,   layout = "star",   group_by = NULL,   order_by = NULL,   weight_to_distance = FALSE,   ring_gap = 0.3,   ego_size = 0.1,   seed = 6886 )

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") ego network, created using ego_netify. object must ego_netify = TRUE attribute. layout Character string specifying ego-centric layout algorithm. Options: "radial": Places ego center alters arranged circle around . Distance center can encode relationship strength. "concentric": Places ego center alters concentric circles based grouping variable relationship strength. "star": Simple star layout ego center alters equally spaced around (default). group_by Character string specifying nodal attribute use grouping alters layout. "radial" layout, groups arranged sectors. "concentric" layout, groups determine ring alters appear . NULL (default), grouping applied. order_by Character string specifying nodal attribute use ordering alters within groups around ego. Common options include network statistics like "degree_total" custom attributes. NULL (default), alters arranged alphabetically. weight_to_distance Logical. TRUE network weighted, use edge weights determine distance ego (higher weights = closer). "radial" layout . Default FALSE. ring_gap Numeric value 0 1 specifying gap concentric rings proportion total radius. used "concentric" layout. Default 0.3. ego_size Numeric value specifying relative size central area reserved ego. Larger values create space ego alters. Default 0.1. seed Integer random number generation ensure reproducible layouts ties ordering. Default 6886.

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: list data frames (one per time period) data frame contains: index: Integer node index actor: Character string actor name x: Numeric x-coordinate node position y: Numeric y-coordinate node position cross-sectional networks, returns list one element. longitudinal networks, returns named list time periods names.

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: function provides specialized layouts ego networks emphasize ego-alter structure: Layout algorithms: Radial: Places ego origin arranges alters circle around . grouping specified, alters arranged sectors related nodes near . weight_to_distance TRUE, alters stronger ties ego placed closer center. Concentric: Places ego origin arranges alters concentric circles. ring assignment can based grouping variable (categorical) continuous variable (discretized rings). Star: simple star layout places ego center distributes alters evenly around single circle. equivalent radial layout without grouping weighting. Visual encoding: layouts allow encoding network properties spatial arrangement: Distance ego: Can represent tie strength, frequency interaction, dyadic measures Angular position: Can group similar alters together (e.g., family, friends, colleagues) Ring assignment: Can represent categories, levels importance, discretized continuous variables Longitudinal networks: longitudinal ego networks, function maintains consistent angular positions alters across time periods possible, making easier track changes ego's network time.

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: function designed specifically ego networks created ego_netify. general network layouts, use get_node_layout. function issue warning used non-ego networks attempt proceed treating first node ego.

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Calculate ego-centric layout positions for network visualization — get_ego_layout
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Create an ego network ego_net <- ego_netify(my_network, ego = "Alice")  # Get radial layout with alters grouped by attribute layout_radial <- get_ego_layout(ego_net,                                 layout = "radial",                                group_by = "department")  # Get concentric layout with rings based on degree layout_circles <- get_ego_layout(ego_net,                                 layout = "concentric",                                  group_by = "degree_total")  # Use with plot plot(ego_net, point_layout = layout_radial) } # }

what: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
text: get_node_layout computes node positions network visualization using various layout algorithms igraph. function converts netify object igraph format, applies specified layout algorithm, returns node coordinates suitable plotting.

title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
code: get_node_layout(   netlet,   layout = NULL,   static_actor_positions = FALSE,   which_static = NULL,   seed = 6886,   ig_netlet = NULL,   ... )

title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") compute layout positions. layout Character string specifying layout algorithm use. Options include: "nicely": Automatic selection appropriate layout (default unipartite) "bipartite": Two-column layout bipartite networks (default bipartite) "fruchtermanreingold" "fr": Force-directed layout "kamadakawai" "kk": Another force-directed layout "circle": Nodes arranged circle "star": Star-shaped layout "grid": Nodes grid "tree": Hierarchical tree layout "random" "randomly": Random positions Additional options: "graphopt", "sugiyama", "drl", "lgl", "dh", "gem", "mds" NULL, defaults "nicely" unipartite "bipartite" bipartite networks. static_actor_positions Logical. TRUE, maintains consistent node positions across time periods longitudinal networks. FALSE (default), time period gets optimized layout. which_static Integer specifying time period's layout use static template static_actor_positions TRUE. NULL (default), creates static layout based union edges across time periods, giving weight persistent edges. seed Integer random number generation ensure reproducible layouts. Default 6886. ig_netlet optional pre-converted igraph object. provided, function use directly instead converting netify object . ... Additional arguments passed ego-specific layout functions layout "radial" "concentric". See get_ego_layout available options (ego_group_by, ego_order_by, ego_weight_to_distance, etc.).

title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
text: list data frames (one per time period) data frame contains: index: Integer node index actor: Character string actor name x: Numeric x-coordinate node position y: Numeric y-coordinate node position cross-sectional networks, returns list one element. longitudinal networks, returns named list time periods names.

title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
text: function handles layout generation cross-sectional longitudinal networks several key features: Layout algorithms: function provides access major igraph layout algorithms. default "nicely" option automatically selects appropriate algorithm based network structure. ego networks, specialized layouts ("radial" "concentric") available emphasize ego-alter structure. Longitudinal layouts: longitudinal networks, two approaches available: Dynamic layouts: time period gets optimized layout, may better reveal structural changes makes visual comparison harder Static layouts: time periods use node positions, facilitating visual comparison network evolution using static layouts which_static = NULL, function creates composite layout based union edges across time periods. Edges appear frequently given higher weight, producing layouts emphasize stable core structure network. Bipartite networks: bipartite networks, default layout arranges two node sets separate columns.

title: Calculate node layout positions for netify visualization — get_node_layout
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
text: Tests whether connected actors similar attributes (homophily). Calculates correlation attribute similarity tie presence, support multiple similarity metrics significance testing.

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
code: homophily(   netlet,   attribute,   method = "correlation",   threshold = 0,   signed_handling = c("abs", "drop_negative", "preserve_sign"),   significance_test = TRUE,   n_permutations = 1000,   alpha = 0.05,   other_stats = NULL,   ... )

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
text: netlet netify object containing network data. attribute Character string specifying nodal attribute analyze. method Character string specifying similarity metric: "correlation" Negative absolute difference continuous data (default) "euclidean" Negative euclidean distance continuous data "manhattan" Negative Manhattan/city-block distance continuous data "cosine" Cosine similarity continuous data "categorical" Binary similarity (0/1) categorical data "jaccard" Jaccard similarity binary/presence-absence data "hamming" Negative Hamming distance categorical data threshold Numeric value function determine tie presence weighted networks. numeric, edges weights > threshold considered ties. function, take network matrix return logical matrix. Default 0 (positive weight tie). Common values: 0 (default), mean(weights), median(weights), quantile-based thresholds. pre-binarized networks, consider using mutate_weights() first. signed_handling Character. Strategy signed (negative-weight) edges: "abs" (default) Take absolute values thresholding tie magnitude — positive negative — can become connection. "drop_negative" Set negative weights zero thresholding (positive ties ). "preserve_sign" Treat non-zero entry (positive negative) connection; threshold ignored sign decisions. Ignored network negative weights. significance_test Logical. Whether perform permutation test. Default TRUE. n_permutations Number permutations significance testing. Default 1000. alpha Significance level confidence intervals. Default 0.05. other_stats Named list custom functions additional statistics. ... Additional arguments passed custom functions.

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
text: Data frame homophily statistics per network/time period: net Network/time identifier layer Layer name attribute Analyzed attribute name method Similarity method used threshold_value Threshold used determining ties (NA binary networks) homophily_correlation Correlation similarity tie presence (binary: tie/tie) mean_similarity_connected Mean similarity among connected pairs (weight > threshold) mean_similarity_unconnected Mean similarity among unconnected pairs (weight <= threshold missing) similarity_difference Difference connected unconnected mean similarities p_value Permutation test p-value ci_lower, ci_upper Confidence interval bounds n_connected_pairs Number connected pairs n_unconnected_pairs Number unconnected pairs

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
text: Auto-promotion categorical: leave method default pass character, factor, logical attribute, homophily() switch method = "categorical" automatically inform per attribute. avoids C++-level error otherwise come feeding non-numeric data correlation-based similarity routine. Pass method = "categorical" (explicit choice) silence message. Similarity Metrics: continuous attributes: correlation: Based absolute difference, good general purpose metric euclidean: Similar correlation single attributes manhattan: Less sensitive outliers euclidean cosine: Useful normalized data sign matters categorical/binary attributes: categorical: Simple matching (1 , 0 different) jaccard: binary data, emphasizes shared presence shared absence hamming: Counts positions values differ (negated similarity) Threshold Parameter: weighted networks, threshold parameter determines edge weights constitute "connection". can specify: numeric value: edges weight > threshold ties function: take matrix return single numeric threshold Common threshold functions: function(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) - mean weight function(x) median(x, na.rm = TRUE) - median weight function(x) quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE) - 75th percentile complex binarization needs (e.g., different thresholds time period), consider using mutate_weights() pre-process network.

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Analyze homophily in network data — homophily
code: # quick homophily check on the bundled classroom friendship data data(classroom_edges) data(classroom_nodes) net <- netify(     classroom_edges,     actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to",     symmetric = TRUE,     nodal_data = classroom_nodes ) # do students cluster by gender? homophily(net, attribute = "gender", method = "categorical") #>   net   layer attribute      method threshold_value homophily_correlation #> 1   1 weight1    gender categorical              NA            0.07436588 #>   mean_similarity_connected mean_similarity_unconnected similarity_difference #> 1                 0.6666667                   0.5520833             0.1145833 #>   p_value    ci_lower  ci_upper n_connected_pairs n_unconnected_pairs n_missing #> 1   0.127 -0.02056023 0.1595074                51                 384         0 #>   n_pairs #> 1     435  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Basic homophily analysis with default threshold (> 0) homophily_default <- homophily(net, attribute = "group")  # Using different similarity metrics for continuous data homophily_manhattan <- homophily(     net,     attribute = "age",     method = "manhattan" # Less sensitive to outliers )  # For binary attributes (e.g., gender, membership) homophily_jaccard <- homophily(     net,     attribute = "member",     method = "jaccard" # Better for binary data than correlation )  # For categorical attributes homophily_categorical <- homophily(     net,     attribute = "department",     method = "categorical" )  # Combining method and threshold homophily_combined <- homophily(     net,     attribute = "score",     method = "manhattan",     threshold = function(x) quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE) ) } # }

what: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: layer_netify combines multiple netify objects single multilayer network structure. input network becomes layer resulting multilayer network, enabling analysis multiple relationship types network views simultaneously.

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
code: layer_netify(netlet_list, ..., layer_labels = NULL)

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: netlet_list list netify objects layer together, first netify object passing multiple objects separate arguments via .... objects must compatible dimensions attributes (see Details). ... Additional netify objects. provided, netlet_list single netify object arguments collected list. allows calling layer_netify(net1, net2, net3) shorthand layer_netify(list(net1, net2, net3)). layer_labels Character vector specifying names layer. NULL (default), uses names netlet_list generates generic labels ("layer1", "layer2", etc.). Length must match number netify objects.

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: multilayer netify object structure depending input type: Cross-sectional input: 3D array [actors × actors × layers] Longitudinal array input: 4D array [actors × actors × layers × time] Longitudinal list input: List 3D arrays, one per time period returned object maintains netify class includes: Combined nodal attributes (compatible) Combined dyadic attributes (compatible) Layer information accessible via attr(obj, 'layers') standard netify attributes

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: Compatibility requirements: netify objects netlet_list must identical: Network type (cross-sectional longitudinal) Dimensions (actors time periods) Mode (unipartite bipartite) Actor composition (actors order) Mixed directedness: Layers allowed different symmetry settings (e.g., symmetric others directed). layers mixed directedness, symmetric attribute stored named logical vector one element per layer. Attribute handling: Nodal dyadic attributes combined possible: attributes identical across layers, first layer's attributes used attributes differ compatible structure, merged attributes incompatible, warning issued attributes must added manually using add_node_vars() add_dyad_vars()

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: Memory usage increases number layers. large networks many layers, consider whether layers necessary analysis.

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Create multilayer networks from multiple netify objects — layer_netify
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Example 1: Cross-sectional multilayer network icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create separate networks for different interaction types verbal_coop <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop"),     dyad_vars = "verbConf" )  material_coop <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "matlCoop",     nodal_vars = "i_polity2",     dyad_vars = "matlConf" )  # Layer them together coop_multilayer <- layer_netify(     netlet_list = list(verbal_coop, material_coop),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  # Check structure dim(get_raw(coop_multilayer)) # [actors × actors × 2] #> [1] 152 152   2 attr(coop_multilayer, "layers") # "Verbal" "Material" #> [1] "Verbal"   "Material"  # \donttest{ # Example 2: Longitudinal multilayer (array format) verbal_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     output_format = "longit_array" )  material_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "matlCoop",     output_format = "longit_array" )  # Create longitudinal multilayer longit_multilayer <- layer_netify(     list(verbal_longit, material_longit),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  dim(get_raw(longit_multilayer)) # [actors × actors × 2 × years] #> [1] 152 152   2  13 # }

Var1 / Var2 names inherited base R's .data.frame.table / reshape2::melt heritage used internal helpers (decompose_helpers, plot_homophily, etc.); rename downstream need snake_case (e.g. rlang::set_names(, c("", "", "value", ...))). fully snake_case, dyad-attribute-merged edge frame, use unnetify() as_tibble.netify() instead.

title: Create attribute mixing matrices for network data — mixing_matrix
code: # who tends to befriend whom, by gender, in the bundled classroom data data(classroom_edges) data(classroom_nodes) net <- netify(     classroom_edges,     actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to",     symmetric = TRUE,     nodal_data = classroom_nodes ) mm <- mixing_matrix(net, attribute = "gender") round(mm$mixing_matrices[[1]], 3) #>       F     M #> F 0.078 0.167 #> M 0.167 0.588 mm$summary_stats #>   net   layer attribute assortativity diagonal_proportion  entropy modularity #> 1   1 weight1    gender    0.09922078           0.6666667 1.109037 0.03671665 #>   n_groups total_ties #> 1        2        102

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
code: mutate_weights(   netlet,   transform_fn = NULL,   add_constant = 0,   new_name = NULL,   keep_original = TRUE )

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
text: netlet netify object edge weights transform transform_fn function apply weights. Can function takes matrix returns matrix (e.g., log, sqrt, function(x) x^2). NULL, add_constant operation performed. add_constant Numeric value add weights applying transform_fn. Useful log transformations (e.g., add_constant = 1 log(x + 1)) shifting distributions. new_name Optional new name weight variable. provided, updates weight attribute descriptive labels. NULL, keeps original name. keep_original Logical. TRUE (default), preserves original weights dyadic variable. FALSE, discards original weights save memory.

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
text: netify object transformed weights. original weights optionally preserved dyadic variable named "original_weight".

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
text: function handles netify object types: Cross-sectional: Transforms single network matrix Longitudinal arrays: Transforms time slice Longitudinal lists: Transforms time period matrix function automatically updates network attributes: Updates is_binary transformation results 0/1 values Updates detail_weight transformation description Preserves network nodal attributes longitudinal arrays, original weight preservation yet implemented show informational message.

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Mutate edge weights in a netify object — mutate_weights
code: # Load example data data(icews) icews_2010 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create a weighted network net <- netify(     icews_2010,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Example 1: Log transformation (common for skewed data) net_log <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = log,     add_constant = 1, # log(x + 1) to handle zeros     new_name = "log_verbCoop" ) print(net_log) #> ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! #> • Unipartite #> • Asymmetric #> • log_verbCoop (transformed) #> • Cross-Sectional #> • # Unique Actors: 152 #> Network Summary Statistics: #>           dens miss  mean recip trans #> verbCoop 0.435    0 0.975 0.966 0.639 #> • Nodal Features: None #> • Dyad Features: original_weight  # Example 2: Square root transformation (moderate skewness) net_sqrt <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = sqrt,     new_name = "sqrt_verbCoop" )  # Example 3: Binarization (convert to presence/absence) net_binary <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) ifelse(x > 0, 1, 0),     new_name = "verbCoop_binary" ) #> ℹ network has been binarized through transformation  # Example 4: Standardization (z-scores) net_std <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) {         mean_x <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)         sd_x <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)         return((x - mean_x) / sd_x)     },     new_name = "verbCoop_standardized" )  # Example 5: Rank transformation net_rank <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) rank(x, na.last = "keep"),     new_name = "verbCoop_ranked" )  # Example 6: Power transformation net_power <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) x^0.5, # Square root as power     new_name = "verbCoop_power" )  # Example 7: Min-max normalization (scale to 0-1) net_norm <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) {         min_x <- min(x, na.rm = TRUE)         max_x <- max(x, na.rm = TRUE)         return((x - min_x) / (max_x - min_x))     },     new_name = "verbCoop_normalized" )  # Example 8: Winsorization (cap extreme values) net_winsor <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) {         q95 <- quantile(x, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)         return(pmin(x, q95)) # Cap at 95th percentile     },     new_name = "verbCoop_winsorized" )  # Example 9: Only add constant (no transformation function) net_shifted <- mutate_weights(     net,     add_constant = 10,     new_name = "verbCoop_shifted" )  # Example 10: Don't keep original weights to save memory net_log_compact <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = log1p, # log(1 + x), handles zeros automatically     new_name = "log1p_verbCoop",     keep_original = FALSE )  # Example 11: Longitudinal network transformation # \donttest{ # Create longitudinal network net_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     actor_time_uniform = FALSE )  # Transform across all time periods net_longit_log <- mutate_weights(     net_longit,     transform_fn = log1p,     new_name = "log_verbCoop" ) # }  # Example 12: Custom transformation with multiple operations net_custom <- mutate_weights(     net,     transform_fn = function(x) {         # Complex transformation: log, then standardize         x_log <- log(x + 1)         x_std <- (x_log - mean(x_log, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x_log, na.rm = TRUE)         return(x_std)     },     new_name = "verbCoop_log_std" )

what: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: net_plot_data processes netify object generates necessary components network visualization. function handles layout computation, aesthetic parameter organization, data structuring subsequent plotting ggplot2 visualization tools.

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
code: net_plot_data(netlet, plot_args = list())

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") containing network visualized. Must single-layer network (multilayer networks currently supported). plot_args list plotting arguments controlling visualization appearance behavior. Can include: Layout parameters: point_layout: Pre-computed node positions data.frame list data.frames (longitudinal networks). provided, overrides layout algorithm selection layout: Character string specifying igraph layout algorithm. Options: "nicely" (default), "fr" (Fruchterman-Reingold), "kk" (Kamada-Kawai), "circle", "star", "grid", "tree", "bipartite", others. See get_node_layout full list static_actor_positions: Logical. TRUE, maintains consistent node positions across time periods longitudinal networks which_static: Integer specifying time period use template static positions seed: Integer reproducible random layouts Display options: remove_isolates: Logical. Remove unconnected nodes (default: TRUE) add_edges: Logical. Include edges visualization (default: TRUE) curve_edges: Logical. Use curved edges instead straight (default: FALSE) add_points: Logical. Display nodes points (default: TRUE) add_text: Logical. Add text labels nodes (default: FALSE) add_label: Logical. Add boxed labels nodes (default: FALSE) Selective labeling: select_text: Character vector node names label text select_label: Character vector node names label boxes Additional aesthetic parameters processed adjust_plot_args gg_params.

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: list three components creating network visualizations: plot_args: Processed plotting arguments defaults applied parameters validated. Includes layout display settings ggnet_params: Organized aesthetic parameters ggplot2 mapping. Contains separate specifications nodes, edges, text, labels static dynamic (data-mapped) aesthetics net_dfs: Data frames ready plotting: nodal_data: Node information including positions (x, y), attributes, additional variables edge_data: Edge information including endpoint coordinates (x1, y1, x2, y2) edge attributes

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: function serves data preparation layer netify visualization, performing several operations: Data validation: Ensures input valid netify object Checks single-layer networks (multilayer supported) Validates ego networks contain one ego Layout computation: Generates node positions using specified algorithm provided Calculates edge endpoint coordinates based node positions Handles cross-sectional longitudinal layouts Data organization: Merges layout information network attributes Processes plotting arguments applies defaults Organizes aesthetic parameters ggplot2 compatibility Removes isolates requested Output structure: returned data structured direct use ggplot2 can customized. separation layout, aesthetics, data allows flexible visualization workflows.

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: function primarily designed use netify's plot method can called directly custom visualization workflows. multilayer networks, use subset_netify extract individual layers visualization. ego networks multiple egos, create separate visualizations combine using packages like patchwork.

title: Prepare netify data for network visualization — net_plot_data
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Create network object from various data types — netify
title: Create network object from various data types — netify
text: function takes various types network data (dyadic datasets, matrices, arrays, lists, igraph objects, network objects) outputs netify object.

title: Create network object from various data types — netify
code: netify(   input,   actor1 = NULL,   actor2 = NULL,   time = NULL,   symmetric = TRUE,   mode = "unipartite",   weight = NULL,   sum_dyads = FALSE,   actor_time_uniform = TRUE,   actor_pds = NULL,   diag_to_NA = TRUE,   missing_to_zero = TRUE,   output_format = NULL,   nodal_vars = NULL,   dyad_vars = NULL,   dyad_vars_symmetric = rep(symmetric, length(dyad_vars)),   input_type = c("auto", "dyad_df", "netify_obj"),   nodelist = NULL,   force_dense = FALSE,   ... )

title: Create network object from various data types — netify
text: input data object netify. Can : data.frame (tibble/data.table) dyadic data matrix representing adjacency matrix 3D array representing longitudinal networks list matrices representing longitudinal networks igraph object network object (network package) list igraph network objects actor1 character: name actor 1 variable data (required data.frame inputs) actor2 character: name actor 2 variable data (required data.frame inputs) time character: name time variable data. Can contain numeric, Date, POSIXct/POSIXlt, character values. Non-numeric types converted numeric indices preserving original labels. time provided cross-sectional network created. symmetric logical: whether ties symmetric, default TRUE. matrix, array, list, igraph, network inputs default ignored unless explicitly set caller; symmetry instead detected input . default applies data.frame inputs. mode character: whether network unipartite bipartite, default unipartite. symmetric, default applies data.frame inputs auto-detected matrix / array / list / igraph / network inputs unless explicitly set. weight character: name weighted edge variable data, default NULL sum_dyads logical: whether sum weight value exists repeating dyads actor_time_uniform logical: whether assume actors across full time series observed data TRUE means actors across full time series observed data outputted netify object array format. FALSE means actors come observed data "existence" determined data, meaning first year existence determined time point first event last year existence time point last event. Outputted netify object list format. actor_pds data.frame indicating start end time point every actor, can created using get_actor_time_info, unless provided estimated user based choice actor_time_uniform diag_to_NA logical: whether diagonals set NA, default TRUE. matrix / array / list inputs, default ignored unless explicitly set: instead, diag_to_NA auto-detected inspecting whether diagonal supplied matrix already NA. Pass explicit value override auto-detection. missing_to_zero logical: whether missing values set zero, default TRUE. diag_to_NA, auto-detected matrix / array / list inputs based whether supplied data already contains -diagonal NAs. output_format character: "cross_sec", "longit_array", "longit_list". specified time NULL output_format "cross_sec" time specified output_format default "longit_list". applies data.frame inputs. nodal_vars character vector: names nodal variables input added attributes netify object (data.frame inputs) dyad_vars character vector: names dyadic variables input added attributes netify object (data.frame inputs) dyad_vars_symmetric logical vector: whether ties symmetric, default use choice symmetric argument input_type character: force specific input type interpretation. Options "auto" (default), "dyad_df", "netify_obj". Use "dyad_df" force data.frame interpretation "netify_obj" force matrix/array/ igraph/network interpretation. nodelist character vector: optional list actors (nodes) included network. ensures isolates (nodes edges) properly represented. Particularly useful working edgelists contain active dyads. force_dense logical: Matrix::sparseMatrix input densify large allocation (N > 5000 density < 1%), netify() aborts guidance message. Set force_dense = TRUE override guard proceed densification. ... additional arguments passed to_netify processing network objects

title: Create network object from various data types — netify

title: Create network object from various data types — netify
text: Ha Eun Choi, Cassy Dorff, Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas

title: Create network object from various data types — netify
code: # load example directed event data from ICEWS # this data comes in the form of a dyadic # dataframe where all dyad pairs are listed data(icews)  # From a data.frame: generate a longitudional, directed and weighted network # where the weights are matlConf icews_matlConf <- netify(     input = icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE, weight = "matlConf" )  # From a matrix adj_matrix <- matrix(rbinom(100, 1, 0.3), 10, 10) net_from_matrix <- netify(adj_matrix)  # From an igraph object if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(igraph) g <- sample_gnp(10, 0.3) net_from_igraph <- netify(g) } # }

text: is_binary() returns single logical: TRUE every -diagonal cell underlying adjacency 0, 1, NA. Reads cached "is_binary" attribute available falls back probing raw matrix / array / list. nodal_data() returns nodal-attribute data.frame stored netify object ("nodal_data" attribute), NULL nodal attributes attached. Convenience wrapper users remember attr() call. is_bipartite() returns single logical. igraph loaded netify, bare is_bipartite() may masked igraph::is_bipartite() (accept netify). Use is_bipartite_netify() (alias) call netify::is_bipartite() avoid collision. is_bipartite_netify() alias is_bipartite() collide igraph::is_bipartite() packages attached. is_directed_netify() returns single logical. Convenience alias !isTRUE(attr(x, "symmetric")) collide igraph::is_directed(). is_longitudinal() returns single logical. is_multilayer() returns single logical. is_symmetric_netify() returns single logical (, mixed-directedness multilayer, per-layer vector). n_actors() returns single integer (number unique actors across periods / modes; bipartite length-2 integer vector c(row, col)). n_periods() returns single integer (1 cross-sectional). n_layers() returns single integer (1 single-layer).
