what: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: netify_to_amen (also available to_amen) transforms netify network objects data structure required amen package advanced network modeling. enables use Social Relations Models (SRM), Additive Multiplicative Effects (AME) models, network regression approaches implemented amen.

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
code: netify_to_amen(netlet, lame = FALSE)  to_amen(netlet, lame = FALSE)

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") containing network data. Must single-layer network. multilayer networks, first extract individual layers using subset_netify. lame Logical. Controls output format longitudinal data: FALSE (default): Formats output compatibility standard version amen package, uses array structures. Y returned 3D array [n_actors x n_actors x n_time], Xdyad 4D array [n_actors x n_actors x n_covariates x n_time], Xrow/Xcol 3D arrays [n_actors x n_attributes x n_time]. requires constant actor composition across time. TRUE: Formats output compatibility netify-verse version called lame, supports longitudinal network modeling time-varying actor compositions. Y returned list T matrices (one per time period), Xdyad list T 3D arrays, Xrow/Xcol lists T matrices. Actor sets can vary across time periods, making suitable panels countries enter/exit. parameter ignored cross-sectional data.

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: structure returned list depends data type lame parameter: cross-sectional data longitudinal lame = FALSE (standard amen): Y Network adjacency data numeric matrix array: Cross-sectional: Matrix dimensions [n_actors × n_actors] Longitudinal: Array dimensions [n_actors × n_actors × n_time] Contains edge weights binary indicators. Missing edges preserved NA. Xdyad Dyadic covariates array, NULL none exist: Cross-sectional: [n_actors × n_actors × n_covariates] Longitudinal: [n_actors × n_actors × n_covariates × n_time] slice contains one dyadic covariate across actor pairs. Xrow Sender/row actor attributes matrix array, NULL none exist: Cross-sectional: [n_actors × n_attributes] Longitudinal: [n_actors × n_attributes × n_time] Contains numeric attributes actors act senders. Xcol Receiver/column actor attributes, structured identically Xrow. symmetric networks, Xcol identical Xrow. bipartite networks, contains attributes second mode. longitudinal data lame = TRUE (lame package): Y list length T (time periods), element n × n relational matrix. Actor sets can vary across time periods. Xdyad list length T, element n × n × pd array dyadic covariates, NULL none exist Xrow list length T, element n × pr matrix nodal row covariates, NULL none exist Xcol list length T, element n × pc matrix nodal column covariates, NULL none exist

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: Variable requirements: nodal attributes must numeric (integer double) Character factor variables must converted using function Missing values (NA) preserved can handled amen's models use format: Use lame = FALSE : Actor composition constant across time Use lame = TRUE : Actors enter/exit network time Want access features lame

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: function performs several validation checks: Ensures single-layer networks (multilayer supported). multilayer networks, first extract individual layers using subset_netify (e.g., subset(net, layers = "trade")). Verifies nodal attributes numeric Maintains actor ordering original netify object multilayer longitudinal models require 4D array [n, n, p, T], see netify_to_dbn instead. Bipartite networks. amen::ame() accept rectangular Y matrices; passing output to_amen() bipartite netify amen::ame() fail. Use netify_to_lame (sets mode = "bipartite" targets lame::ame()) bipartite networks instead.

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
text: Ha Eun Choi, Cassy Dorff, Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert netify objects to amen format — netify_to_amen
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create a netify object net <- netify(     icews[icews$year == 2010, ],     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Convert to amen format (standard) amen_data <- netify_to_amen(net) names(amen_data) # Y, Xdyad, Xrow, Xcol #> [1] "Y"     "Xdyad" "Xrow"  "Xcol"   if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # For longitudinal data with time-varying composition longit_net <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Convert to lame format lame_data <- netify_to_amen(longit_net, lame = TRUE) } # }

what: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
text: netify_to_dbn (also available to_dbn) transforms netify network objects array format required dbn package Dynamic Bilinear Network models. enables use longitudinal latent space models multilayer single-layer networks.

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
code: netify_to_dbn(netlet)  to_dbn(netlet)

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
text: netlet netify object (class "netify") containing longitudinal network data. Must type longit_array longit_list. Supports single-layer multilayer networks.

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
text: list containing: Y Network adjacency data 4D array dimensions [n_actors, n_actors, n_layers, n_time]. single-layer networks, n_layers = 1 third dimension labeled weight variable name ("edge_value" binary networks). Missing edges preserved NA. Xdyad Dyadic covariates 4D array dimensions [n_actors, n_actors, n_covariates, n_time], NULL none exist. Xrow Sender/row actor attributes 3D array dimensions [n_actors, n_attributes, n_time], NULL none exist. Xcol Receiver/column actor attributes 3D array dimensions [n_actors, n_attributes, n_time], NULL none exist. symmetric networks, Xcol identical Xrow.

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
text: dbn package expects 4D array dimensions [n, n, p, T] n number actors, p number relation types (layers), T number time periods. Supported netify types: longit_array: Directly extracts reshapes underlying array longit_list: Converts array format first (actors unioned across time, missing entries become NA) Cross-sectional networks supported since dbn designed longitudinal data. Variable requirements: nodal attributes must numeric (integer double) Character factor variables must converted using function

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
text: Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert netify objects to dbn format — netify_to_dbn
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create two longitudinal networks verbal_net <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     output_format = "longit_array" )  material_net <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "matlCoop",     output_format = "longit_array" )  # Create multilayer network multi_net <- layer_netify(     list(verbal_net, material_net),     layer_labels = c("Verbal", "Material") )  # Convert to dbn format dbn_data <- netify_to_dbn(multi_net) dim(dbn_data$Y) # [n_actors, n_actors, 2, n_years] #> [1] 152 152   2  13  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Single-layer also works dbn_single <- netify_to_dbn(verbal_net) dim(dbn_single$Y) # [n_actors, n_actors, 1, n_years] } # }

what: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: Transforms netify network objects igraph objects (also available netify_to_igraph), preserving network structure optionally including nodal dyadic attributes vertex edge attributes.

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
code: netify_to_igraph(   netlet,   add_nodal_attribs = TRUE,   add_dyad_attribs = TRUE,   .quiet_na = FALSE )  to_igraph(   netlet,   add_nodal_attribs = TRUE,   add_dyad_attribs = TRUE,   .quiet_na = FALSE )

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: netlet netify object containing network data. Single-layer networks return single igraph (list igraphs longitudinal input); multilayer networks return named list keyed layer, element igraph list igraphs. add_nodal_attribs Logical. TRUE (default), includes nodal attributes netify object vertex attributes igraph object. Set FALSE create network structure . add_dyad_attribs Logical. TRUE (default), includes dyadic attributes netify object edge attributes igraph object. Set FALSE exclude edge covariates. .quiet_na Logical. Internal flag suppress one-shot NA--zero cli alert called inside netify's summary path. Default FALSE; set user code.

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: igraph object list igraph objects: Cross-sectional networks Returns single igraph object Longitudinal networks Returns named list igraph objects, names corresponding time periods Multilayer networks Returns named list keyed layer; element igraph (longitudinal) list igraphs resulting igraph object(s) : Vertices named according actors netify object Edge weights netify weight variable (present) Vertex attributes nodal variable (add_nodal_attribs = TRUE) Edge attributes dyadic variable (add_dyad_attribs = TRUE)

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: conversion process handles different netify structures: Cross-sectional: Direct conversion single igraph object Longitudinal arrays: Internally converted list format, time slice becomes separate igraph object Longitudinal lists: time period converted separate igraph object directed networks, resulting igraph object directed. undirected networks, igraph object undirected. Edge weights netify object become edge weights igraph. longitudinal data includes actors appear disappear time, time period's igraph object contain actors present period.

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: function requires igraph package installed.

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph
text: Ha Eun Choi, Cassy Dorff, Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert netify objects to igraph format — netify_to_igraph

what: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
text: netify_to_lame() (also available to_lame()) thin specialization \link{netify_to_amen} sibling lame package, CRAN. Install GitHub: remotes::install_github("netify-dev/lame"). lame::ame() accepts Y / Xdyad / Xrow / Xcol skeleton amen::ame() adds two things netify users care :

title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
code: netify_to_lame(   netlet,   lame = FALSE,   family = NULL,   pad = TRUE,   fit_method = c("gibbs", "als"),   bootstrap = 0L )  to_lame(   netlet,   lame = FALSE,   family = NULL,   pad = TRUE,   fit_method = c("gibbs", "als"),   bootstrap = 0L )

title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
text: netlet netify object. lame Logical. netify_to_amen: pass TRUE actor composition varies time. Default FALSE. family Optional character. AME family use. NULL (default), inferred netify: "binary" binary networks, "normal" weighted, -per-session info message naming choice. pad Logical. lame = TRUE per-period list returned (function never actually pads place; always returns list), controls whether -per-session info message points users lame::list_to_array() + lame::lame() snippet baked nl$ame_call. Default TRUE (emit message); set FALSE silence . fit_method One "gibbs" (default — MCMC posterior via lame::ame() / lame::lame()) "als" (fast alternating- least-squares point estimate via lame::ame_als()). bipartite + binary networks MCMC slow, ALS bootstrap > 0 gives fast point estimate plus parametric/block bootstrap uncertainty intervals single call. choice affects generated ame_call snippet — Y/Xdyad/ Xrow/Xcol payload identical. bootstrap Integer. Number bootstrap replicates ALS uncertainty intervals. Ignored fit_method = "gibbs" (MCMC draws uncertainty representation ). Default 0 (bootstrap). Pass 200 reasonable interval estimate.

title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
text: list shape netify_to_amen output, plus two helpful extras: mode Character: "unipartite" "bipartite". Pass directly lame::ame(mode = .). family Character: suggested family ("binary" "normal"). Pass lame::ame(family = .). ame_call Character: literal lame::ame() call string user can copy-paste.

title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
text: rectangular Y bipartite networks via mode = "bipartite" — standard amen::ame() rejects . ragged longitudinal panels via lame::list_to_array() → lame::ame_rep_* — amen requires constant actor composition. function wraps \link{netify_to_amen} : picks family default appropriate netify ("binary" binary nets, "normal" weighted), suggests correct mode argument lame::ame() ("unipartite" / "bipartite"), lame = TRUE longitudinal output, emits copy-paste lame::list_to_array() + lame::lame() snippet returned ame_call slot. function pad list — run snippet (literal call) step . Bipartite + binary: case to_amen() fit. bipartite weighted-binary netify (e.g., person × event attendance, 0/1), exact pipeline: Ragged longitudinal panels. lame = TRUE actors enter / exit network, function: builds per-period list via netify_to_amen(lame=TRUE), bakes lame::list_to_array(actors = U, Y = nl$Y, ...) pad--fit snippet nl$ame_call user runs materialize 3D [n, n, T] array fit lame::lame(). returned Y / Xdyad / Xrow / Xcol always per-period list — pad controls whether -per- session info message reminding user snippet fires.
code: bp <- netify(df, actor1 = "person", actor2 = "event", mode = "bipartite", weight = "attended") nl <- to_lame(bp) # auto-detects binary fit <- lame::ame( Y = nl$Y, Xrow = nl$Xrow, Xcol = nl$Xcol, mode = nl$mode, family = nl$family, nscan = 1000, burn = 500 ) # uncertainty: posterior intervals are in fit$BETA / fit$VC

title: Convert a netify object to the format expected by lame::ame() — netify_to_lame
text: Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: Transforms netify network objects statnet's network objects (also available netify_to_network, to_statnet, to_network), providing access extensive statistical modeling capabilities statnet suite, including ERGMs (Exponential Random Graph Models), descriptive statistics, network visualization tools.

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
code: netify_to_statnet(netlet, add_nodal_attribs = TRUE, add_dyad_attribs = TRUE)  netify_to_network(netlet, add_nodal_attribs = TRUE, add_dyad_attribs = TRUE)  to_statnet(netlet, add_nodal_attribs = TRUE, add_dyad_attribs = TRUE)  to_network(netlet, add_nodal_attribs = TRUE, add_dyad_attribs = TRUE)

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: netlet netify object containing network data. Cross-sectional, longitudinal, multilayer netlets supported; multilayer inputs dispatched layer--layer layer results returned named list keyed layer. add_nodal_attribs Logical. TRUE (default), includes nodal attributes netify object vertex attributes network object. Set FALSE create network structure . add_dyad_attribs Logical. TRUE (default), includes dyadic attributes netify object edge attributes network object. dyad variable attached two places resulting network: network-level attribute original name (full \(n\times n\) matrix) per-edge attribute <var>_e. trailing _e disambiguates per-edge edgelist network-level matrix. ergm::edgecov() pass original (matrix) name, _e alias, since edgecov() reads network-level matrix attribute. _e per-edge attribute exposed descriptive use (e.g. coloring edges network::plot.network).

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: network object list network objects: Cross-sectional networks Returns single network object Longitudinal networks Returns named list network objects class c("network.list", "list"), names corresponding time periods. network.list class format tergm CMLE (tergm(nl ~ Form(.) + Persist(.), estimate = "CMLE",       times = seq_along(nl))) stergm expect directly, output plug--play longitudinal ERGMs. Multilayer networks Returns named list (per-time) network objects keyed layer name resulting network object(s) : Vertices named according actors netify object Edge weights netify weight variable stored "weight" attribute Vertex attributes nodal variable (add_nodal_attribs = TRUE) Edge attributes dyadic variable (add_dyad_attribs = TRUE); per-edge attributes carry _e suffix, network-level attributes keep original name Proper directedness based symmetric parameter netify object netify_na_cols attribute (character vector) listing nodal columns carry NAs; pass directly drop_na_actors() drop offending actors fitting ergm formulas reference columns

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: conversion process handles different netify structures: Cross-sectional: Direct conversion single network object Longitudinal arrays: Internally converted list format, time slice becomes separate network object Longitudinal lists: time period converted separate network object statnet network format stores networks edgelist attributes, making memory-efficient sparse networks. nodal dyadic attributes netify object preserved can used subsequent ERGM modeling network analysis. longitudinal data, time period results independent network object. format suitable discrete-time network analysis pooled ERGM estimation across time periods.

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: function requires network package (part statnet) installed. ERGM modeling, ergm package (also part statnet) loaded creating network objects.

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
text: Ha Eun Choi, Cassy Dorff, Colin Henry, Shahryar Minhas Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert netify objects to statnet network format — netify_to_statnet
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Cross-sectional example icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  # Create netify object with attributes dvars <- c("matlCoop", "verbConf", "matlConf") nvars <- c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop")  verbCoop_net <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     dyad_vars = dvars,     dyad_vars_symmetric = rep(FALSE, length(dvars)),     nodal_vars = nvars )  # Convert to statnet network object ntwk <- netify_to_statnet(verbCoop_net) #> ! Nodal columns with "NA" detected: "i_polity2" and "i_log_gdp". Ergm terms #>   like `nodecov()`/`nodematch()` will refuse to fit. #> ℹ Use `drop_na_actors(net, cols = c('i_polity2', 'i_log_gdp'))` (or impute) #>   before refitting. #> This message is displayed once per session. #> ℹ Dyad covariates attached as per-edge attributes under "matlCoop_e", #>   "verbConf_e", and "matlConf_e" and as network-level matrices under their #>   original names ("matlCoop", "verbConf", and "matlConf"). #> ℹ For `ergm::edgecov()` use the matrix name (e.g. `edgecov('matlCoop')`); the #>   "_e" per-edge attribute is for descriptive use such as edge styling. #> This message is displayed once per session.  # Examine the result ntwk #>  Network attributes: #>   vertices = 152  #>   directed = TRUE  #>   hyper = FALSE  #>   loops = FALSE  #>   multiple = FALSE  #>   bipartite = FALSE  #>   verbCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   matlCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   verbConf: 152x152 matrix #>   matlConf: 152x152 matrix #>   total edges= 9976  #>     missing edges= 0  #>     non-missing edges= 9976  #>  #>  Vertex attribute names:  #>     i_log_gdp i_log_pop i_polity2 vertex.names  #>  #>  Edge attribute names not shown  network::network.size(ntwk) # number of vertices #> [1] 152 network::network.edgecount(ntwk) # number of edges #> [1] 9976 network::list.vertex.attributes(ntwk) # nodal attributes #> [1] "i_log_gdp"    "i_log_pop"    "i_polity2"    "na"           "vertex.names" network::list.edge.attributes(ntwk) # edge attributes #> [1] "matlConf_e" "matlCoop_e" "na"         "verbConf_e" "verbCoop"    # Access specific attributes network::get.vertex.attribute(ntwk, "i_polity2") # polity scores #>   [1]  NA   9   2  -2   8   5  10  10  -7  -5   5  -7   8   7   7  NA   8   9 #>  [19]   0   6   2  -4  10  10  -1  -2  10  -7   7   9  -4   5  10   0   9  -7 #>  [37]  10  10   2   8   5  -3   8  -6   9  -3  -4  10  10   3  -5   6  10   8 #>  [55]  10   8   1   6   6   0   7  10   9   8  -7   3  10   6  10   9  10  -3 #>  [73]  -6   8 -10   8  -7   4  -7   8   6   6  -7  10  10   9   0   6   6   7 #>  [91]  -2  10   8   9  10  -6   5  -6   6  10  10   9   3   4  10  -8   6   9 #> [109]   4   8   9   8  10  10 -10   9   4  -4 -10   7   7  -2  10  10   8   0 #> [127]   9  10   3  -2   5  10  10  -7  -3  -1   4  -2  10  -4  -9  -1   6  -8 #> [145]  10  10  10  -9  -7  -2   7   1 network::get.edge.attribute(ntwk, "matlCoop") # material cooperation #> NULL  # Check network properties network::is.directed(ntwk) # TRUE for this example #> [1] TRUE network::has.loops(ntwk) # FALSE (no self-ties) #> [1] FALSE  # \donttest{ # Longitudinal example verbCoop_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     dyad_vars = dvars,     dyad_vars_symmetric = rep(FALSE, length(dvars)),     nodal_vars = nvars )  # Convert to list of network objects ntwk_list <- netify_to_statnet(verbCoop_longit)  # Examine structure length(ntwk_list) # number of time periods #> [1] 13 names(ntwk_list) # time period labels #>  [1] "2002" "2003" "2004" "2005" "2006" "2007" "2008" "2009" "2010" "2011" #> [11] "2012" "2013" "2014"  # Access specific time period ntwk_2002 <- ntwk_list[["2002"]] ntwk_2002 #>  Network attributes: #>   vertices = 152  #>   directed = TRUE  #>   hyper = FALSE  #>   loops = FALSE  #>   multiple = FALSE  #>   bipartite = FALSE  #>   verbCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   matlCoop: 152x152 matrix #>   verbConf: 152x152 matrix #>   matlConf: 152x152 matrix #>   total edges= 8692  #>     missing edges= 0  #>     non-missing edges= 8692  #>  #>  Vertex attribute names:  #>     i_log_gdp i_log_pop i_polity2 vertex.names  #>  #>  Edge attribute names not shown  # }  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Use with ergm for modeling (requires ergm package) library(ergm) model <- ergm(ntwk ~ edges + mutual + nodematch("i_polity2")) } # }

what: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
text: documentation-topic ("?netify_workflows") indexes standard pipelines netify users actually run, function calls order. New users opening ?netify see constructor; opening topic sees recipes.

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
text: ready--run worked example ships package:
code: library(netify) net <- netify(friends, actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to", symmetric = TRUE, nodal_data = people) summary(net) plot(net, node_color_by = "gender") data(classroom_edges); data(classroom_nodes) net <- netify(classroom_edges, actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to", symmetric = TRUE, nodal_data = classroom_nodes) summary(net) plot(net, node_color_by = "gender", node_size_by = "gpa")

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
text: bipartite edgelist links two different kinds things (e.g. companies jurisdictions, movies actors, students classes). Build mode = "bipartite" pass row-mode / col-mode covariates separately.
code: net <- netify(filings, actor1 = "company", actor2 = "jurisdiction", mode = "bipartite", weight = "n_filings") net <- add_node_vars(net, company_meta, actor = "company", mode = "row", node_vars = c("industry", "hq")) net <- add_node_vars(net, jurisdiction_meta, actor = "jurisdiction", mode = "col", node_vars = "tax_haven") plot(net, style = "heatmap")

what: From a longitudinal dyadic event dataset (ICEWS-style)
title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: net <- netify(icews, actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year", symmetric = FALSE, weight = "verbCoop") net <- add_node_vars(net, country_panel, actor = "actor", time = "year", node_vars = c("polity", "gdp_log")) summary(net, tidy = TRUE) |> ggplot(aes(time, value)) + geom_line() + facet_wrap(~ metric)

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: # build as a single multilayer netify by splitting on `channel` ml <- netify(slack_long, actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to", time = "month", weight = "n_messages", layer = "channel") # or build per-layer + combine with layer_netify() nets <- lapply(channels, function(ch) { netify(slack_long[slack_long$channel == ch, ], ...) }) ml <- layer_netify(setNames(nets, channels))

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: ntwk <- to_statnet(net) library(ergm) fit <- ergm(ntwk ~ edges + nodematch("gender") + nodecov("gpa")) summary(fit)

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: nl <- to_statnet(longit_net) # network.list library(tergm) fit <- tergm(nl ~ Form(~ edges + nodematch("gender")) + Persist(~ edges + mutual), estimate = "CMLE", times = seq_along(nl))

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: lm_in <- to_lame(net) # auto-picks family from binary/weighted library(lame) # copy-paste the printed `lm_in$ame_call`: fit <- lame::ame(Y = lm_in$Y, Xdyad = lm_in$Xdyad, Xrow = lm_in$Xrow, Xcol = lm_in$Xcol, family = "binary", nscan = 1000, burn = 500)

what: To AME (binary bipartite + ALS + bootstrap uncertainty)
title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: # fast point estimate + parametric bootstrap CIs in one call lm_in <- to_lame(net_bp, fit_method = "als", bootstrap = 200) library(lame) fit <- lame::ame_als(Y = lm_in$Y, mode = "bipartite", family = "binary", bootstrap = 200L) confint(fit) # bootstrap CIs from_lame_fit(fit, value = "prob") |> plot(style = "heatmap")

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: # is observed transitivity surprising vs Erdős-Rényi at this density? compare_to_null(net, fn = function(n) c(transitivity = summary(n)$transitivity), n_sim = 200, model = "erdos_renyi", seed = 1)

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: my_stat <- function(net) { sa <- summary_actor(net, stats = "betweenness") c(max_betw = max(sa$betweenness, na.rm = TRUE)) } bootstrap_netlet(net, fn = my_stat, n_boot = 200, seed = 1)

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
text: netify stores adjacencies densely, N x N matrix consumes 8 * N^2 bytes (1.7 GB N = 15K, 20 GB N = 50K) per snapshot. recipes avoid dense bottleneck possible.
code: # 1. build directly from an edgelist data.frame -- skip any #    pre-built sparse / dense matrix intermediate. net <- netify(edges, actor1 = "from", actor2 = "to",               weight = "n", symmetric = FALSE)  # 2. use the fast actor-summary path -- skips closeness / #    betweenness / eigen / HITS, which dominate wall-clock at #    large N. netify auto-promotes at N >= netify.fast_threshold #    (default 1500); tune via options(netify.fast_threshold = ...). sa <- summary_actor(net)            # auto-promotes  # 3. for community detection / shortest paths / clustering, hand #    off to igraph. its C backend handles 50K+ nodes comfortably. ig <- to_igraph(net) cl <- igraph::cluster_louvain(ig)  # 4. when you need a sparse object for downstream code (e.g. #    spectral methods, irlba, glmnet), exit via to_igraph() and #    grab the sparse adjacency from there. ig <- to_igraph(net) M  <- igraph::as_adjacency_matrix(ig, sparse = TRUE)  # requires Matrix  # 5. if you must accept a pre-built sparse matrix, density < 1 #    with N > 5,000 aborts to protect against accidental #    gigabyte allocations -- pass force_dense = TRUE to override. net <- netify(m_sparse, force_dense = TRUE)

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: # to igraph and back ig <- to_igraph(net) back <- netify(ig) # preserves directedness + weight  # to long data frame edges <- unnetify(net, remove_zeros = TRUE) tb <- tibble::as_tibble(net) # same thing, tibble class  # to base matrix M <- as.matrix(net) # strips netify class

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows
code: # combine two cross-sec into a 2-period longit combined <- bind_netifies(n_2020, n_2021, names = c("2020", "2021"))

title: Common netify workflows — index of patterns — netify_workflows

what: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: new_netify (also available new_netlet) low-level constructor creates netify objects raw matrix, array, list data structures. function automatically detects network properties sets appropriate attributes, making useful converting existing network data netify format.

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
code: new_netify(data, ...)

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: data network data structure convert: Matrix: Creates cross-sectional netify object 3D array: Creates longitudinal array netify object (dimensions: actors × actors × time) List matrices: Creates longitudinal list netify object (useful time-varying actor composition) ... Additional parameters set attributes netify object. Common parameters include: symmetric: Logical indicating network undirected mode: "unipartite" "bipartite" weight: Name edge weight variable diag_to_NA: Whether set diagonal NA missing_to_zero: Whether treat missing edges zeros nodal_data: Data frame node attributes dyad_data: Dyadic attributes (see netify documentation) provided, properties automatically detected data.

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: netify object class "netify" appropriate structure: matrices: single netify matrix netify_type = "cross_sec" arrays: netify array netify_type = "longit_array" lists: netify list netify_type = "longit_list", element netify object netify objects include automatically detected user-specified attributes network properties, making ready use netify functions.

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: Automatic property detection: properties explicitly provided, new_netify intelligently detects: Symmetry: Checks matrix equals transpose Mode: Infers unipartite/bipartite dimensions actor names Edge weights: Detects binary (0/1) vs. weighted networks Diagonal treatment: Checks diagonal contains NAs Missing values: Determines NAs exist -diagonal Actor composition: longitudinal data, detects actors remain constant vary time Naming conventions: row/column names provided: Unipartite networks: Actors named "a1", "a2", ... Bipartite networks: Row actors "r1", "r2", ...; column actors "c1", "c2", ... Time periods: Named "1", "2", ... specified Longitudinal data handling: longitudinal networks: Arrays assume constant actor composition across time Lists allow time-varying actor composition time slice list becomes separate cross-sectional netify object Properties detected across time periods (e.g., symmetric time slices symmetric)

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: low-level constructor primarily intended package developers advanced users. users use higher-level netify() function, provides comprehensive data validation preprocessing. function support multilayer networks directly. create multilayer networks, create separate netify objects combine layer_netify(). function attempts detect network properties automatically, explicitly providing parameters recommended clarity.

title: Low-level constructor for netify objects — new_netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

what: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: peek provides convenient way inspect portions network data stored netify objects. Rather displaying entire networks (can overwhelming large datasets), function allows examine specific actors, time periods, layers data exploration, verification, debugging.

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
code: peek(netlet, actors = NULL, from = 3, to = 3, time = 1, layers = NULL)

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: netlet netify object preview. Can cross-sectional, longitudinal (array list format), /multilayer. actors Character vector actor names numeric indices subset. Selects actors senders receivers (extracts subgraph among actors). Overridden specified. Specifies actors display senders ties (row actors adjacency matrix). Can : Single number: Shows first n actors (e.g., = 5 displays actors 1-5) Numeric vector: Shows specific positions (e.g., = c(1,3,5) displays 1st, 3rd, 5th actors) Character vector: Shows named actors (e.g., = c("USA", "China") displays specific countries) NULL: Shows row actors (default 3) bipartite networks, represents actors first mode. Specifies actors display receivers ties (column actors adjacency matrix). Accepts input types . bipartite networks, columns represent actors second mode. Default 3. time longitudinal networks, specifies time periods display. Can : Single number: Shows nth time period (e.g., time = 1 shows first time period) Numeric vector: Shows specific time indices (e.g., time = c(1,5,10)) Character vector: Shows named time periods (e.g., time = c("2002", "2006") displays specific years) NULL: Shows time periods Default 1 (first time period ). Ignored cross-sectional networks. layers multilayer networks, specifies layer(s) display. Must character vector matching layer names netify object (e.g., layers = c("trade", "alliance")). single-layer networks, parameter ignored. Default NULL (shows layers).

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: Returns subset raw network data (without netify attributes): Cross-sectional networks Single layer: Returns matrix selected rows columns Multilayer: Returns 3D array (rows × columns × layers) Longitudinal networks Array format: Returns array dimensions depending selection List format: Returns list matrices, one per selected time period returned objects preserve dimension names (actor names, time labels, layer names) easy interpretation. Single dimensions automatically dropped.

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: Purpose Design peek designed lightweight data exploration tool. Unlike subset_netify, creates new netify objects attributes preserved, peek returns raw network data quick inspection. makes ideal : Verifying data structure content Checking specific relationships Debugging data issues Quick visual inspection network patterns Understanding Network Directions directed networks: represents actors sending ties (-ties) represents actors receiving ties (-ties) Cell [,j] contains tie actor actor j example, cell ["USA", "China"] = 5, means USA sends tie strength 5 China. Smart Selection Behavior function includes several convenience features: Single numbers expanded ranges (e.g., = 5 becomes first 5 actors) --bounds indices silently ignored (errors exploration) Character names matched actor labels Dimension reduction: one time period layer selected, dimension dropped output

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: Important distinctions: Use peek quick data inspection (returns raw matrices) Use subset_netify create new netify objects attributes Use get_raw extract raw data netify object multiple layers present layer selection specified, layers returned warning message remind multilayer structure.

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Preview subsets of network data from netify objects — peek
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Example 1: Basic usage with cross-sectional network icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ] net <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Default: see first 3 actors (both sending and receiving) peek(net) #>             Afghanistan Albania Algeria #> Afghanistan          NA       5       5 #> Albania what: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
text: pivot_dyad_to_network swaps dyadic attribute main network netify object. useful want analyze different relationship type stored dyadic attribute. example, network represents trade relationships FDI stored dyadic attribute, can pivot make FDI main network.

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
code: pivot_dyad_to_network(   netlet,   dyad_var,   make_network_dyad_var = TRUE,   network_var_name = NULL,   symmetric = NULL,   weight_type = NULL,   diag_to_NA = NULL,   missing_to_zero = NULL )

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
text: netlet netify object containing network dyadic attributes dyad_var Character string naming dyadic variable become new network. Must exist netlet's dyad_data attribute. make_network_dyad_var Logical. TRUE (default), current network preserved dyadic attribute. network_var_name Character string specifying name old network converted dyadic attribute. NULL (default), uses current weight attribute name available, otherwise "old_network". symmetric Logical NULL. Specifies whether new network treated symmetric. NULL (default), attempts detect dyadic variable's symmetry setting data structure. weight_type Character string describing type weight new network (e.g., "trade_volume", "fdi_amount"). NULL (default), uses dyad_var name. diag_to_NA Logical. Whether set diagonal values NA new network. NULL (default), inherits original netlet. missing_to_zero Logical. Whether treat missing values zeros new network. NULL (default), inherits original netlet.

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
text: netify object dyadic variable main network (optionally) old network preserved dyadic attribute. attributes dyadic variables preserved.

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
text: function handles different netify types appropriately: cross-sectional networks: performs simple matrix swap longitudinal arrays: swaps matrices across time periods longitudinal lists: swaps matrices time period multilayer networks: swaps within layer new network different properties original (e.g., different symmetry weight type), function updates netify attributes accordingly. bipartite networks, new network always treated asymmetric. dyadic variable originally specified symmetry information via add_dyad_vars(), information used unless overridden symmetric parameter.

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
text: Shahryar Minhas

title: Pivot a dyadic variable to become the network — pivot_dyad_to_network
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create a netify object with verbal cooperation as the main network # and material cooperation as a dyadic attribute icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  net <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  net <- add_dyad_vars(     net,     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     dyad_vars = "matlCoop",     dyad_vars_symmetric = FALSE )  # Check the current network print(net) #> ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! #> • Unipartite #> • Asymmetric #> • Weights from `verbCoop` #> • Cross-Sectional #> • # Unique Actors: 152 #> Network Summary Statistics: #>           dens miss   mean recip trans #> verbCoop 0.435    0 18.134 0.982 0.646 #> • Nodal Features: None #> • Dyad Features: matlCoop  # Pivot to make material cooperation the main network net_pivoted <- pivot_dyad_to_network(     net,     dyad_var = "matlCoop",     network_var_name = "verbCoop" ) #> ℹ Auto-detected symmetry for 'matlCoop': asymmetric #> ✔ Successfully pivoted 'matlCoop' to be the main network #> ℹ Previous network preserved as dyadic variable 'verbCoop'  # The main network is now material cooperation print(net_pivoted) #> ✔ Hello, you have created network data, yay! #> • Unipartite #> • Asymmetric #> • Weighted: matlCoop #> • Cross-Sectional #> • # Unique Actors: 152 #> Network Summary Statistics: #>           dens miss  mean recip trans #> verbCoop 0.093    0 0.467 0.442 0.646 #> • Nodal Features: None #> • Dyad Features: verbCoop  # The old network (verbal cooperation) is preserved as a dyadic attribute attr(net_pivoted, "dyad_data")[["1"]][["verbCoop"]][1:5, 1:5] #>             Afghanistan Albania Algeria Angola Argentina #> Afghanistan          NA       0       0      0         1 #> Albania               5      NA       0      0         0 #> Algeria               5       1      NA      8         0 #> Angola                0       0       6     NA         7 #> Argentina             2       0       0      8        NA

what: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Creates customizable network visualizations netify objects using ggplot2. Supports cross-sectional longitudinal networks extensive options mapping network attributes visual properties.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
code: # S3 method for class 'netify' plot(x, auto_format = TRUE, ...)

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: x 'netify' object containing network data visualize. auto_format Logical. TRUE (default), automatically adjusts plot parameters based network characteristics size, density, structure. includes "intelligent" defaults : Node size (smaller larger networks) Edge transparency (lower denser networks) Text labels (enabled small networks) Curved edges (small dense networks) Isolate removal (large networks) Set FALSE disable automatic formatting. Individual parameters can still overridden even auto_format TRUE. ... Additional arguments controlling plot appearance:

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: ggplot2 object can customized additional layers, scales, themes, etc. longitudinal networks, includes facets time period. return_components = TRUE, returns list plot components can manually assembled modified.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Naming Conventions: function supports two naming styles parameters: Recommended: Use node_* node attributes *_by variable mappings (e.g., node_size_by = "degree") Legacy: Use point_* nodes *_var variables (e.g., point_size_var = "degree") Default Behaviors: weighted networks, edge transparency maps weight default directed networks, arrows added automatically longitudinal networks, time periods shown facets Isolates removed default (set remove_isolates = FALSE keep) Customization Tips: Use mutate_weight handle skewed weight distributions Combine fixed variable aesthetics (e.g., fixed color variable size) Add ggplot2 layers plot call customization Use select_text selective labeling dense networks

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: layout Character string specifying igraph layout algorithm. Options include: "nicely" (default), "fr" (Fruchterman-Reingold), "kk" (Kamada-Kawai), "circle", "star", "grid", "tree", "bipartite" (bipartite networks), "randomly", others. ego networks, additional options available: "radial" (ego-centric optional grouping) "concentric" (ego center alters rings). See get_node_layout get_ego_layout full details. point_layout Optional data.frame list data.frames containing pre-computed node positions columns 'actor', 'x', 'y'. Overrides layout provided. static_actor_positions Logical. longitudinal networks, node positions remain constant across time? Default FALSE. which_static Integer. static_actor_positions = TRUE, time period's layout use template? NULL (default), creates composite layout time periods. seed Integer reproducible layouts. Default 6886.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: add_edges Logical. Display edges? Default TRUE. add_points Logical. Display nodes points? Default TRUE. add_text Logical. Add text labels nodes? Default FALSE. add_text_repel Logical. Add text labels automatic repositioning avoid overlaps? Default FALSE. TRUE, overrides add_text. Uses ggrepel positioning. add_label Logical. Add boxed labels nodes? Default FALSE. add_label_repel Logical. Add boxed labels automatic repositioning avoid overlaps? Default FALSE. TRUE, overrides add_label. Uses ggrepel positioning. remove_isolates Logical. Remove unconnected nodes? Default TRUE. curve_edges Logical. Use curved edges? Default FALSE. use_theme_netify Logical. Apply netify theme? Default TRUE. facet_type Character. multilayer longitudinal networks, controls faceting style: "grid" (default) creates 2D grid time × layer, "wrap" creates wrapped facets combined time-layer labels. facet_ncol Integer. Number columns facet_wrap layouts. used facet_type = "wrap" single-dimension faceting. rescale_edge_weights Logical. multilayer networks, edge weights rescaled common 0-1 range across layers? useful layers different weight scales. Default FALSE.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: node_filter expression filter nodes. expression can reference nodal attribute. example: node_filter = degree_total > 5 show nodes total degree greater 5. expression evaluated context nodal data, node-level variable can used. edge_filter expression filter edges. expression can reference edge attribute (including 'weight' weighted networks). example: edge_filter = weight > 0.5 show edges weight greater 0.5. expression evaluated context edge data, edge-level variable can used. time_filter longitudinal networks, vector time periods include plot. Can numeric indices character labels matching time dimension. NULL (default), time periods plotted. cross-sectional networks, parameter ignored.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Fixed aesthetics (nodes): node_size point_size Numeric. Size nodes. node_color point_color Color node borders. node_fill point_fill Fill color nodes (note fill work certain shapes). node_shape point_shape Shape nodes (see ?pch). node_alpha point_alpha Transparency (0-1). node_stroke point_stroke Width node borders. Variable aesthetics (mapped data): node_size_by point_size_var Column name size mapping. node_color_by point_color_var Column name border color. node_fill_by point_fill_var Column name fill color (note fill work certain shapes). node_shape_by point_shape_var Column name shape. node_alpha_by point_alpha_var Column name transparency.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Fixed aesthetics: edge_color Color edges. Default "black". edge_linewidth Width edges. Default 0.5. edge_linetype Line type (1=solid, 2=dashed, etc.). edge_alpha Transparency (0-1). edge_curvature Curvature amount curve_edges = TRUE. edge_arrow Arrow specification directed networks. Example: arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")). Variable aesthetics: edge_color_by edge_color_var Column name color mapping. edge_linewidth_by edge_linewidth_var Column name width. edge_linetype_by edge_linetype_var Column name line type. edge_alpha_by edge_alpha_var Column name transparency. weighted networks, defaults weight variable specified.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Selective labeling: select_text Character vector node names show text. used, text labels automatically use geom_text_repel avoid overlaps. select_text_display Alternative text display (length select_text). select_label Character vector node names show boxes. used, labels automatically use geom_label_repel avoid overlaps. select_label_display Alternative labels (length select_label). Text aesthetics: text_size Fixed size text. Default 3.88. text_color Fixed color text. Default "black". text_alpha Fixed transparency text. text_size_by Variable map text size. text_color_by Variable map text color. Label (boxed text) aesthetics similar parameters label_ prefix. Text repel parameters (add_text_repel = TRUE): text_repel_force Force repulsion overlapping text. Default 1. text_repel_max_overlaps Maximum number overlaps tolerate. Default 10. text_repel_box_padding Padding around text. Default 0.25. text_repel_point_padding Padding around points. Default 0. text_repel_segment_color Color connecting segments. Default "grey50". Label repel parameters (add_label_repel = TRUE): Similar text_repel label_repel_ prefix  label_repel_label_padding Padding around label boxes. Default 0.25. label_repel_label_r Radius label box corners. Default 0.15.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Customize legend titles: node_size_label point_size_label Legend title size. node_color_label point_color_label Legend title color. edge_alpha_label Legend title edge transparency. edge_color_label Legend title edge color.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: highlight Character vector node names highlight different colors. Non-highlighted nodes colored grey. Highlighted nodes can also automatically enlarged highlight_size_increase greater 1. highlight_color Named vector colors highlighted nodes. NULL, uses default distinct colors (red, blue, green 3 nodes, color palette ). Names match values highlight parameter. Example: c('USA' = 'blue', 'China' = 'red', 'Russia' = 'green'). highlight_label Title highlight legend. Default "Highlighted". highlight_size_increase Numeric factor(s) increase size highlighted nodes. Can single value (applied highlighted nodes) vector length length(highlight) + 1 value corresponds highlighted node last value applies "" nodes. Default 1 (size increase). Example: c(3, 1, 1, 0.5) 3 highlighted nodes first 3x larger, next two normal size, others half size. show_other_in_legend Logical. Include "" category legend? Default FALSE. FALSE, highlighted nodes appear legend.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: ego networks (created ego_netify), additional layout options control ego-centric visualization: ego_group_by Character string specifying nodal attribute use grouping alters ego layouts. "radial" layout, creates sectors. "concentric" layout, determines ring assignment. ego_order_by Character string specifying nodal attribute use ordering alters within groups rings. Common options include "degree_total". ego_weight_to_distance Logical. weighted networks "radial" layout, edge weights determine distance ego? Higher weights place alters closer ego. Default FALSE. ego_ring_gap Numeric (0-1). Gap concentric rings proportion radius. "concentric" layout. Default 0.3. ego_size Numeric. Relative size central area reserved ego. Larger values create space ego alters. Default 0.1.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: mutate_weight Function transform edge weights plotting. Example: log1p log(x+1) transformation. Applied mapping aesthetics. return_components Logical. Return plot components instead assembled plot? Useful manual customization. Default FALSE. style Either style function (e.g., style_budapest) applies complete visual style including colors, shapes, layout preferences, string "heatmap" render adjacency matrix tile plot instead node-link diagram. style = "heatmap" edge weights cross zero (signed network), fill scale automatically uses diverging palette centred zero; otherwise sequential viridis ramp used. Optional low, mid, high arguments override diverging palette endpoints.

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Plotting method for netify objects — plot.netify
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Basic cross-sectional network icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ] net_10 <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop" )  # Simple plot with auto-formatting (default) plot(net_10)   # Plot without auto-formatting for full control plot(net_10, auto_format = FALSE)   # add nodal stats to netlet net_10 <- add_node_vars(     net_10,     summary_actor(net_10),     "actor" )  # Customized plot with new naming convention plot(net_10,     edge_color = "lightgrey",     node_size_by = "degree_total", # Instead of point_size_var     node_color = "steelblue",     edge_alpha_by = "verbCoop", # Instead of edge_alpha_var     node_size_label = "Degree",     edge_alpha_label = "Verbal Cooperation" )   # \donttest{ # Longitudinal network example net_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp") )  # Add network statistics net_longit <- add_node_vars(     net_longit,     summary_actor(net_longit),     actor = "actor",     time = "time" )  # Plot with multiple aesthetics plot(net_longit,     # Edges     edge_color = "grey70",     mutate_weight = log1p, # Transform weights     # Nodes     node_size_by = "degree_total",     node_color_by = "i_polity2",     # Labels     node_size_label = "Total Degree",     node_color_label = "Polity Score",     edge_alpha_label = "Log(Verbal Coop.)",     # Layout     static_actor_positions = TRUE # Keep positions constant )   # Selective labeling example plot(net_10,     node_size_by = "degree_total",     select_text = c("United States", "China", "Russian Federation"),     text_size = 3,     text_color = "darkred" )   # choose alternative labels for selected text plot(net_10,     node_size_by = "degree_total",     select_text = c("United States", "China", "Russian Federation"),     select_text_display = c("USA", "CHN", "RUS"),     text_size = 3,     text_color = "darkred" )   # Time subsetting example plot(net_longit,     time_filter = c("2010", "2011", "2012") )   # Node subsetting example # democracies with high GDP plot(net_longit, node_filter = ~ i_polity2 > 6 & i_log_gdp > 25)   # use return_components=TRUE # to get back ggplot2 pieces of plot g10 <- plot(     net_10,     node_alpha = .8,     arrow = ggplot2::arrow(length = ggplot2::unit(0.01, "inches")),     node_size_by = "degree_total",     node_size_label = "Log(Degree)",     edge_alpha_label = "Log(Verbal Coop.)",     remove_isolates = TRUE,     mutate_weight = log1p,     return_components = TRUE )  # Manually assemble with custom modifications # to scale aesthetics such as edges g10$base +     netify_edge(g10) +     ggplot2::scale_alpha_continuous(range = c(0.01, 0.2)) +     netify_node(g10) +     theme_netify()  # }

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
text: plot_actor_stats creates visualizations actor-level statistics summary_actor() output. function automatically adapts data structure (cross-sectional/longitudinal, single/multilayer) offers two main visualization approaches: distribution across actors tracking specific actors.

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
code: plot_actor_stats(   summary_df,   longitudinal = ifelse("time" %in% colnames(summary_df), TRUE, FALSE),   multilayer = ifelse("layer" %in% colnames(summary_df), TRUE, FALSE),   across_actor = TRUE,   specific_stats = NULL,   specific_actors = NULL )

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
text: summary_df data frame summary_actor() containing actor-level statistics. Must include "actor" column. May include "time" column longitudinal data "layer" column multilayer networks. longitudinal Logical indicating whether treat data longitudinal. NULL (default), automatically detected based presence "time" column. Set FALSE one unique time point exists. multilayer Logical indicating whether treat data multilayer. NULL (default), automatically detected based presence "layer" column. Set FALSE one unique layer exists. across_actor Logical. TRUE (default), visualizes distribution statistics across actors. FALSE, focuses tracking specific actors. TRUE specific_actors provided, shows distribution actors. specific_stats Character vector statistic names plot. NULL (default), plots available statistics. Must match column names summary_df. specific_actors Character vector actor names highlight focus . NULL (default) across_actor = FALSE, includes actors (warning > 25 actors). Must match values "actor" column.

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
text: ggplot object can customized saved. plot type depends data structure parameters: Cross-sectional, across actors: Density plots rug plots Cross-sectional, specific actors: Beeswarm plots Longitudinal, across actors: Ridge density plots time Longitudinal, specific actors: Line plots time plots faceted statistic , applicable, layer.

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
text: Visualization logic: function chooses appropriate visualizations based data structure: Distribution plots (across_actor = TRUE): Show statistics distributed across actor population Actor-specific plots (across_actor = FALSE): Track individual actors, specified actors highlighted color others appear gray plots use theme_stat_netify() consistent styling across netify visualizations. multilayer longitudinal data across_actor = FALSE, consider using specific_stats avoid overcrowded facets.

title: Visualize actor-level network statistics — plot_actor_stats
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: plot_graph_stats creates line bar plots visualize network-level statistics across multiple networks, time points, layers. function designed work output summary_net() similar functions produce network-level summary statistics.

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
code: plot_graph_stats(summary_df, type = "line", specific_stats = NULL)

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: summary_df data frame containing network-level statistics, typically summary_net(). Must include "net" column identifying network time point. May include "layer" column multilayer networks. columns contain numeric statistics plot. type Character string specifying plot type. Options : "line": Line plot points (default). Best temporal data "bar": Bar plot grouped bars. Required multilayer non-temporal data specific_stats Character vector statistic names plot. NULL (default), plots numeric columns summary_df. Must match column names exactly.

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: ggplot object displaying specified statistics. plot structure depends data: Single time/network: Returns error (single row plottable) Multiple times/networks: Line bar plot faceted statistic Multilayer temporal: Line plots colored layer Multilayer non-temporal: Grouped bar plots layer plots faceted statistic free y-axis scales better comparison across different value ranges.

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: Data structure detection: function automatically detects structure data: Longitudinal: Multiple unique values "net" column Multilayer: Multiple unique values "layer" column Single network: one row (returns error suggestion) Plot type selection: Line plots preferred temporal data show trends Bar plots automatically selected multilayer non-temporal data Bar plots can useful comparing discrete time points Faceting behavior: statistic gets facet panel : Independent y-axis scales (scales = "free_y") Shared x-axis across panels Automatic layout based number statistics

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: function requires least two networks/time points create meaningful plot. single network summaries, consider using table format instead.

title: Visualize network-level statistics — plot_graph_stats
text: Ha Eun Choi, Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

Required distribution plots extract actual similarity data. type Character string specifying plot type: "distribution" Shows similarity score distributions connected vs unconnected pairs (requires netlet) "comparison" Compares homophily across multiple attributes "temporal" Shows homophily evolution time (longitudinal data) attribute Character string. distribution plots, specifies attribute visualize. match attribute used homophily(). method Character string. distribution plots, similarity method used. match method used homophily(). sample_size Integer. distribution plots large networks, number dyad pairs sample visualization. Default NULL (use pairs). colors Character vector two colors connected/unconnected significant/non-significant pairs. Default uses package theme colors. ... Additional arguments passed ggplot2 functions.

title: Visualize attribute mixing matrix results — plot_mixing_matrix
text: mixing_results Output mixing_matrix() containing mixing matrices summary statistics. which_matrix Integer character. matrix plot multiple present. Default 1 (first matrix). show_values Logical. Whether display values tile. Default TRUE. value_digits Integer. Number decimal places displayed values. Default 2. color_scale Character vector three colors low, mid, high values. Default uses diverging color scale. midpoint Numeric. midpoint diverging color scale. Default NULL automatically calculates based data range. text_size Numeric. Size value labels tiles. Default 4. text_color Character. Color text labels. Default "black". text_color_threshold Numeric. provided, values threshold (0-1 scale) use white text, values use black text. Default NULL uses text_color . tile_border_color Character. Color tile borders. Default "white". tile_border_size Numeric. Width tile borders. Default 0.5. reorder_categories Logical. Whether reorder categories similarity. Default FALSE. diagonal_emphasis Logical. Whether emphasize diagonal cells (within-group mixing). Default TRUE. ... Additional arguments passed ggplot2 functions.

code: # Compare two networks data(icews) net1 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2010,], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j") net2 <- netify(icews[icews$year == 2011,], actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j") comp <- compare_networks(list("2010" = net1, "2011" = net2)) print(comp) #>  #> ── Network Comparison Results ────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Comparison type: cross_network #> Number of networks: 2 #> Comparison focus: edges #> Method: correlation #> Algorithm: correlation #> Permutation type: classic #> Correlation type: pearson #> Random seed: 1378665888 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Edge Comparison Summary ── #>  #>     comparison correlation #> 1 2010 vs 2011           1 #> ── Edge Changes  #> 2010_vs_2011: #> Added: 0 | Removed: 0 | Maintained: 22952

what: Simulate null-model networks from a netify object — simulate.netify
title: Simulate null-model networks from a netify object — simulate.netify
text: Generates nsim new netify objects one three standard null models, holding actor set fixed (, applicable, observed density / degree sequence). Useful sanity-checking whether observed network statistic (transitivity, modularity, etc.) surprising relative chance benchmark, without reaching statnet::ergm simple null.

title: Simulate null-model networks from a netify object — simulate.netify
code: # S3 method for class 'netify' simulate(   object,   nsim = 1L,   seed = NULL,   model = c("erdos_renyi", "configuration", "dyad_permutation"),   ... )

title: Simulate null-model networks from a netify object — simulate.netify
text: object netify object (cross-sectional per-period; longitudinal input period simulated independently). nsim Integer. Number simulated draws return. seed Optional integer. Local RNG seed; user's global set.seed() stream left untouched. model Character. One : "erdos_renyi" Independent Bernoulli edges matched observed density (directedness). "configuration" Configuration-model rewire preserves observed degree sequence (/directed inputs). Uses igraph::sample_degseq(). "dyad_permutation" Permute dyads (Snijders-Borgatti vertex relabel + symmetric reshuffle). Preserves density , conditional permutation symmetry, degree distribution shape. ... Passed underlying model implementation.

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: Extracts subset netify object based specified actors, time periods, /layers preserving netify attributes structure.

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
code: # S3 method for class 'netify' subset(   x,   actors = NULL,   time = NULL,   layers = NULL,   from = NULL,   to = NULL,   ... )

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: x netify object subset actors Character vector actor names numeric indices subset. Extracts subgraph among actors (includes ties actors). Default NULL, includes actors. time Time periods subset. Can : Numeric vector: used indices time dimension Character vector: matched time dimension labels NULL: includes time periods (default) layers Character vector layer names subset multilayer networks. single-layer networks, ignored. multilayer networks, least one layer must specified. Character vector actor names numeric indices actors sending ties (row actors). Overrides actors. Set NULL include sending actors. bipartite networks, refers actors first mode. Character vector actor names numeric indices actors receiving ties (column actors). Overrides actors. Set NULL include receiving actors. bipartite networks, refers actors second mode. ... Additional arguments (currently unused)

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: netify object containing requested subset : Subsetted adjacency matrix/matrices Corresponding nodal attributes (filtered included actors/times) Corresponding dyadic attributes (filtered included actor pairs/times) Updated netify attributes reflecting new dimensions returned object's structure depends subset: one time period selected longitudinal data, returns cross-sectional one layer selected multilayer data, returns single-layer Otherwise, maintains original structure type

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: function netify-aware wrapper around peek function, handles raw data extraction. peek returns raw matrices/arrays, subset additionally: Preserves updates netify attributes Filters nodal dyadic attribute data match subset Adjusts netify type dimensions change (e.g., longitudinal cross-sectional) Maintains consistency network data attributes parameters allow precise control ties include: Use actors get ties among set actors (subgraph extraction) Use get ties sent specific actors Use get ties received specific actors Use get ties specific sets actors bipartite networks, refers actors first mode (e.g., people) refers actors second mode (e.g., organizations).

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: subsetting longitudinal data single time period, function automatically converts result cross-sectional netify object. Similarly, subsetting multilayer data single layer produces single-layer object.

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Subset netify objects — subset.netify
code: # \donttest{ # load example directed event data from ICEWS data(icews)  # generate a longitudional netify object # with both dyadic and nodal attributes icews_matlConf <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j", time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE, weight = "matlConf",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp", "i_log_pop"),     dyad_vars = c("matlCoop", "verbCoop", "verbConf"),     dyad_vars_symmetric = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE) )  # subset to a few countries using S3 method icews_subset <- subset(     icews_matlConf,     actors = c(         "United States", "United Kingdom",         "Russian Federation", "China"     ) )  # subset to a few countries and a few years icews_subset_2 <- subset(     icews_matlConf,     actors = c(         "United States", "United Kingdom",         "Russian Federation", "China"     ),     time = c("2010", "2011") )  # can also use subset_netify directly icews_subset_3 <- subset_netify(     netlet = icews_matlConf,     actors = c(         "United States", "United Kingdom",         "Russian Federation", "China"     ),     time = c("2010", "2011") ) # }

what: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: Computes comprehensive graph-level statistics netify objects, including density, reciprocity, centralization measures, custom metrics. Handles cross-sectional longitudinal networks, well multilayer structures.

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
code: # S3 method for class 'netify' summary(object, ...)

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: object netify object containing network data ... Additional arguments, including: other_stats Named list custom functions calculate additional graph-level statistics. function accept matrix return named vector scalar values.

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: data frame one row per network/time period containing: Basic network properties: net Network/time identifier layer Layer name (multilayer networks) num_actors Number actors (num_row_actors num_col_actors bipartite networks) density Proportion possible ties exist num_edges Total number edges (count every non-zero entry; signed networks, negative-weight ties included alongside positive ones) prop_edges_missing Proportion potential edge dyads NA. Uses denominator density (-diagonal cells unipartite diag_to_NA, halved symmetric networks, cells bipartite), density + prop_edges_missing + observed_zero_fraction = 1. prop_unknown_edges Proportion potential edges NA unobserved data-entry stage (opposed structurally absent -diagonal). appears netlet built missing_to_zero = FALSE; otherwise unobserved dyads filled 0 column identically zero. weighted networks : mean_edge_weight Average weight existing edges sd_edge_weight Standard deviation edge weights median_edge_weight Median edge weight min_edge_weight, max_edge_weight Range edge weights Structural measures: competition (competition_row/competition_col) Herfindahl-Hirschman Index measuring concentration ties. Calculated \(\sum_{=1}^{n} (s_i)^2\) \(s_i\) actor 's share total ties. Ranges 1/n (equal distribution) 1 (one actor ties). sd_of_actor_means (sd_of_row_means/sd_of_col_means) Standard deviation actors' average tie strengths, measuring heterogeneity actor activity levels transitivity Global clustering coefficient (probability two neighbors node connected) directed networks : covar_of_row_col_means Covariance actors' sending receiving patterns reciprocity Pearson correlation adjacency matrix transpose. measures linear association outgoing incoming tie weights dyad (.e., similar \(a_{ij}\) \(a_{ji}\) across dyads). Values near 1 indicate strong reciprocity, values near 0 indicate relationship. Note: differs traditional edge-based reciprocity (proportion mutual dyads) used igraph packages. mutual Proportion mutual dyads: fraction connected dyad pairs directions present. Ranges 0 (mutual ties) 1 (ties reciprocated). traditional edge-based reciprocity measure used igraph network analysis textbooks.

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: function automatically adapts calculations based network properties: Bipartite networks: Reports row column actors separately Directed networks: Calculates separate statistics /ties Weighted networks: Includes weight-based statistics Multilayer networks: Processes layer independently Longitudinal networks: Calculates statistics time period Competition Index Interpretation: competition measure (HHI) captures concentrated network ties among actors. Low values indicate distributed activity across many actors (competitive), high values indicate concentration among actors (monopolistic). particularly useful analyzing power dynamics resource distribution networks. Custom Statistics: Add custom graph-level metrics using other_stats parameter:
code: # Example: Community detection modularity_stat <- function(mat) {   g <- netify_to_igraph(mat)   comm <- igraph::cluster_walktrap(g)   c(modularity = igraph::modularity(comm),     n_communities = length(unique(comm$membership))) }  summary(net, other_stats = list(community = modularity_stat))

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: large longitudinal multilayer networks, computation can intensive. Consider using subset_netify analyze specific time periods layers. Missing edges (NA values) excluded density calculations tracked prop_edges_missing statistic.

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: Dorff, C., Gallop, M., & Minhas, S. (2023). "Networks violence: Predicting conflict Nigeria." Journal Politics, 85(1).

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Calculate graph-level statistics for netify objects — summary.netify
