title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: Computes comprehensive actor-level statistics netify objects, including degree, strength, centrality measures, custom metrics. Handles different network types (directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted) appropriately.

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
code: summary_actor(   netlet,   invert_weights_for_igraph = TRUE,   other_stats = NULL,   stats = c("all", "fast") )

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: netlet netify object containing network data invert_weights_for_igraph Logical. TRUE (default), inverts edge weights calculating closeness betweenness centrality, igraph interprets weights distances. Set FALSE weights already represent distances. other_stats Optional named list custom functions calculate additional actor-level statistics. function accept matrix return vector one value per actor. stats One "" (default) "fast". "fast" path returns degree- strength-style columns skips closeness, betweenness, eigenvector, HITS. user pass stats explicitly, default auto-promotes "fast" number actors reaches getOption("netify.fast_threshold", 1500L); passing stats = "" explicitly always honors request.

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: data frame actor-level statistics. Columns always include: actor Actor name/identifier time Time period (longitudinal networks) layer Layer name (multilayer networks) Additional columns depend network type: undirected networks: degree Number connections. Calculated \(d_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}\), \(a_{ij}\) adjacency matrix element. prop_ties Proportion possible ties realized. Calculated \(p_i = \frac{d_i}{n-1}\), \(d_i\) degree \(n\) total number actors. net_share Actor's share total network connections. Calculated \(s_i = \frac{d_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} d_j}\). closeness Closeness centrality. Calculated \(C_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{j} d(, j)}\), \(d(, j)\) shortest path distance every actor \(j\). betweenness Betweenness centrality. Calculated \(B_i = \sum_{s \neq \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}()}{\sigma_{st}}\), \(\sigma_{st}\) total number shortest paths node \(s\) node \(t\) \(\sigma_{st}()\) number paths pass \(\). eigen_centrality Eigenvector centrality, based principal eigenvector adjacency matrix. directed networks: in_degree, out_degree Incoming outgoing connections. \(d_i^{} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ji}\) \(d_i^{} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}\). in_prop_ties, out_prop_ties Proportion possible /ties total_degree Sum degree in_closeness, out_closeness Directed closeness centrality betweenness Betweenness centrality authority, hub Authority hub scores (asymmetric ) weighted networks, additional columns: strength_sum Sum edge weights. undirected: \(s_i^{sum} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij}\). directed: separate /sums. strength_avg Average edge weight. Calculated \(s_i^{avg} = \frac{s_i^{sum}}{d_i}\). strength_sd Standard deviation edge weights. Calculated \(s_i^{sd} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{d_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_{ij} - s_i^{avg})^2}\). strength_median Median edge weight

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: function automatically adapts calculations based network properties: Centrality Measures: Degree: Count direct connections. directed networks, calculated separately incoming (-degree) outgoing (-degree) ties. Closeness: Measures quickly actor can reach others. Based inverse sum shortest path distances. Betweenness: Measures often actor lies shortest paths actors, indicating brokerage potential. Eigenvector: Measures importance based connections important actors. Computed using principal eigenvector adjacency matrix. Authority/Hub: directed networks . Authority scores measure importance targets ties important sources. Hub scores measure importance sources ties important targets. Weight Handling: default, function assumes larger weights indicate stronger relationships. calculating closeness betweenness centrality, weights inverted (1/weight) igraph treats edge weights distances. distance-based networks larger values already represent distances weaker relationships, set invert_weights_for_igraph = FALSE. Custom Statistics: Add custom metrics using other_stats parameter. function receives adjacency matrix return vector one value per actor: Mathematical Formulations: symmetric unweighted networks: Degree: \(d_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij}\) Proportion ties: \(p_i = \frac{d_i}{n-1}\) Network share: \(s_i = \frac{d_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} d_j}\) Closeness: \(C_i = \frac{1}{\sum_{j} d(, j)}\) Betweenness: \(B_i = \sum_{s \neq \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}()}{\sigma_{st}}\) symmetric weighted networks, additional measures: Strength sum: \(s_i^{sum} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_{ij}\) Strength average: \(s_i^{avg} = \frac{s_i^{sum}}{d_i}\) Strength standard deviation: \(s_i^{sd} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{d_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_{ij} - s_i^{avg})^2}\) asymmetric networks, statistics calculated separately rows () columns (), totals applicable.
code: # Example: Maximum tie weight for each actor max_out <- function(mat) apply(mat, 1, max, na.rm = TRUE) max_in <- function(mat) apply(mat, 2, max, na.rm = TRUE)  stats <- summary_actor(net,   other_stats = list(max_out = max_out, max_in = max_in))

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: longitudinal networks, statistics calculated separately time period. multilayer networks, statistics calculated separately layer unless layers aggregated beforehand. Missing values (NA) network excluded calculations. Isolates (actors connections) receive appropriate values (0 degree, NA centrality measures). function handles cross-sectional longitudinal data structures, well single-layer multilayer networks. ego networks created netify, function appropriately handles ego-alter structure.

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Calculate actor-level network statistics — summary_actor

what: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: Converts various network object types (igraph, network, matrices/arrays, lists ) netify objects (also available to_netify). Automatically extracts adjacency matrices nodal/dyadic attributes input objects.

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
code: to_netify(net_obj, weight = NULL, ...)

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: net_obj R object convert: igraph, network, matrix, array, list objects. weight Optional. Name weight attribute net_obj used main edge weight netify object. Default NULL. Important specify igraph network objects default weight. ... Additional arguments passed new_netify. Can include nodal_data dyad_data override extracted attributes.

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: netify object : Adjacency matrix list matrices Nodal attributes (present input) Dyadic attributes (present input) Weight specification (provided)

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: function handles different input types: igraph: Extracts adjacency matrix, vertex attributes nodal data, edge attributes dyadic data network: Extracts adjacency matrix, vertex attributes nodal data, edge attributes dyadic data matrix: Direct conversion, attribute extraction array: Assumes 3D arrays represent longitudinal networks list: Must contain objects type (igraph, network, matrices) longitudinal data (lists 3D arrays), function creates netify object time-indexed components. Actor ordering preserved input objects made consistent across components (adjacency, nodal, dyadic data).

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: converting igraph network objects, specify weight parameter designate edge attribute used primary edge weight netify object.

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert igraph, network, or matrix objects to netify format — to_netify
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # From igraph library(igraph) g <- sample_gnp(10, 0.3) E(g)$weight <- runif(ecount(g)) V(g)$type <- sample(c("A", "B"), vcount(g), replace = TRUE)  net <- to_netify(g, weight = "weight")  # From network library(network) n <- network(rgraph(10, tprob = 0.3)) set.vertex.attribute(n, "group", sample(1:2, 10, replace = TRUE))  net <- to_netify(n)  # From matrix adj_mat <- matrix(rnorm(100), 10, 10) net <- to_netify(adj_mat)  # From list of matrices (longitudinal) mat_list <- list(     "2001" = matrix(rnorm(100), 10, 10),     "2002" = matrix(rnorm(100), 10, 10) ) net <- to_netify(mat_list) } # }

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
text: netlet netify object converted dyadic format. remove_zeros Logical. TRUE, removes dyads zero edge weights output, resulting data frame non-zero relationships. FALSE (default), includes possible dyads network.

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
text: data frame one row per dyad containing: : Name/ID first actor dyad : Name/ID second actor dyad time: Time period (longitudinal networks) weight column: Edge weight values (column named weight parameter used netify) from_id: Unique identifier combining actor time (longitudinal ) to_id: Unique identifier combining actor time (longitudinal ) Dyadic attributes: edge-level covariates original data Nodal attributes: Actor-level covariates merged onto dyads, suffixed "_from" "_to" match corresponding actor

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
text: function essentially reverses netify process, making useful : Exporting network data analysis software Creating dyadic datasets regression analysis Inspecting network data familiar data frame format Merging network results back dyadic covariates Nodal attributes attached twice per dyad - "" actor (suffixed "_from") "" actor (suffixed "_to"). applies directed undirected networks, ensuring actors' attributes available dyadic analysis. function handles cross-sectional longitudinal netify objects, automatically detecting structure adjusting output accordingly.

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
text: large networks, setting remove_zeros = FALSE can result large data frames, includes possible dyads (n × (n-1) directed networks n × (n-1) / 2 undirected networks).

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
text: Cassy Dorff, Shahryar Minhas

title: Convert netify objects back to dyadic data frames — unnetify
code: # Load example data data(icews)  # Create a netify object with attributes icews_10 <- icews[icews$year == 2010, ]  verbCoop_net <- netify(     icews_10,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp"),     dyad_vars = c("verbConf", "matlConf") )  # Convert back to dyadic data frame dyad_df <- unnetify(verbCoop_net)  # Examine structure head(dyad_df) #>          from        to time verbCoop verbConf matlConf       from_id #> 1 Afghanistan   Albania    1        0        0        2 Afghanistan_1 #> 2 Afghanistan   Algeria    1        0        0        1 Afghanistan_1 #> 3 Afghanistan    Angola    1        0        0        0 Afghanistan_1 #> 4 Afghanistan Argentina    1        1        0        0 Afghanistan_1 #> 5 Afghanistan   Armenia    1        7        0        0 Afghanistan_1 #> 6 Afghanistan Australia    1      125        2       24 Afghanistan_1 #>         to_id i_polity2_from i_polity2_to i_log_gdp_from i_log_gdp_to #> 1   Albania_1             NA            9       23.49884     23.06701 #> 2   Algeria_1             NA            2       23.49884     25.67186 #> 3    Angola_1             NA           -2       23.49884     24.97089 #> 4 Argentina_1             NA            8       23.49884     27.03815 #> 5   Armenia_1             NA            5       23.49884     22.86492 #> 6 Australia_1             NA           10       23.49884     27.79745 names(dyad_df) #>  [1] "from"           "to"             "time"           "verbCoop"       #>  [5] "verbConf"       "matlConf"       "from_id"        "to_id"          #>  [9] "i_polity2_from" "i_polity2_to"   "i_log_gdp_from" "i_log_gdp_to"    # Remove zero-weight dyads for more compact output dyad_df_nonzero <- unnetify(verbCoop_net, remove_zeros = TRUE) nrow(dyad_df_nonzero) # Much smaller than full dyadic dataset #> [1] 9976  # Note how nodal attributes are added # For directed network: _from and _to suffixes head(dyad_df[, c("from", "to", "i_polity2_from", "i_polity2_to")]) #>          from        to i_polity2_from i_polity2_to #> 1 Afghanistan   Albania             NA            9 #> 2 Afghanistan   Algeria             NA            2 #> 3 Afghanistan    Angola             NA           -2 #> 4 Afghanistan Argentina             NA            8 #> 5 Afghanistan   Armenia             NA            5 #> 6 Afghanistan Australia             NA           10  # Longitudinal example verbCoop_longit <- netify(     icews,     actor1 = "i", actor2 = "j",     time = "year",     symmetric = FALSE,     weight = "verbCoop",     nodal_vars = c("i_polity2", "i_log_gdp") )  # Convert longitudinal network dyad_df_longit <- unnetify(verbCoop_longit, remove_zeros = TRUE)  # Check time periods are included table(dyad_df_longit$time) #>  #> 2002 2003 2004 2005 2006 2007 2008 2009 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014  #> 8692 8887 9514 9346 9429 9739 9988 9854 9976 9708 9841 9911 9774   # Each dyad now has associated time period # Note: weight column is named after the weight variable (verbCoop) head(dyad_df_longit[, c("from", "to", "time", "verbCoop")]) #>          from         to time verbCoop #> 1 Afghanistan    Albania 2002        6 #> 2 Afghanistan    Armenia 2002        3 #> 3 Afghanistan  Australia 2002       81 #> 4 Afghanistan    Austria 2002       17 #> 5 Afghanistan Azerbaijan 2002       14 #> 6 Afghanistan    Bahrain 2002       10  # Use the output for further analysis # \donttest{ # For example, regression analysis lm(verbCoop ~ i_polity2_from + i_polity2_to + verbConf, data = dyad_df) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = verbCoop ~ i_polity2_from + i_polity2_to + verbConf,  #>     data = dyad_df) #>  #> Coefficients: #>    (Intercept)  i_polity2_from    i_polity2_to        verbConf   #>        13.6092         -0.1287         -0.2820          4.2253   #>  # }

title: netify 1.5
text: New worked example dataset (classroom_edges, classroom_nodes) matching short-form vignette (foundations_lite) quick onboarding. Index common pipelines available ?netify_workflows, covering unipartite, bipartite, longitudinal, multilayer setups, plus handoff statnet, amen, lame, dbn, igraph. to_lame() now bundles posterior round-tripping via from_lame_fit(), including bootstrap per-cell credible-interval bounds binary bipartite fits. bind_netifies() gains align_actors = c("none", "union", "intersection") combining cross-sections mismatched actor sets. New helpers: drop_na_actors(), binarize(), validate_netify(), bootstrap_netlet(), compare_to_null(), simulate.netify(), plus type predicates (is_binary(), is_bipartite(), is_longitudinal(), is_multilayer(), n_actors(), n_periods(), n_layers()) accessors (nodal_data()). tidyverse broom interop: as_tibble.netify(), tidy.netify(), glance.netify(), plus .igraph.netify() .network.netify() S3 methods registered automatically relevant package attached. Plotting picks style = "heatmap" adjacency-matrix views, auto-diverging palettes signed weights, smarter label-repel small networks, two extra themes (theme_publication_netify(), theme_publication_netify_ts()). summary_actor() gains stats = "fast" mode (skips expensive centralities) auto-promotes networks getOption("netify.fast_threshold", 1500). Vignettes expanded reorganized: pipeline_netify_ergm, pipeline_lame_dbn, multilayer_networks, tracking_changes, attribute_analysis, manual_plotting, internals new substantially rewritten.
